quotes.quote: The model is data-calibrated: the free parameters have been fixed to reproduce the observed properties of Boötes I, i.e. the total luminosity, the average stellar iron abundance, the Metallicity Distribution Function (MDF; see Fig. figure1 ), and the star formation history. Due to the stochastic nature of the IMF sampling, the chemical enrichment history of Boötes I can slightly change between the different runs of the code. For this reason all the results presented here have been obtained by averaging over 1000 possible Boötes I evolutionary histories and by quantifying the scatter among them.

In the top panel of Fig. figure1 we show a comparison between the observed MDF of Boötes I and the simulated one, which are in good agreement, and the same is true for other observed properties (i.e. the total luminosity, the average stellar iron abundance and the star formation history). In fact, changing the properties of Pop III stars (e.g. yields, IMF) has very limited effects on the MDF, since the general stellar population is dominantly enriched by normal Pop II/I stars (see Sec.5 of Rossi+21 ). The central plot of Fig. figure1 shows the A(C) distribution with Fe/H , for the simulated stellar populations of Boötes I, where the CEMP-no stars ( C/Fe > +0.7 ) are highlighted (black symbols above solid line).

sources.summary: Boo dSph is described as a faint, metal-poor Milky Way satellite at 60 kpc with no detected H I, implying extreme gas loss.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension (RA) of 14^ h 00^ m 06^ s and declination of +14 30 00

quotes.quote: Among the least luminous is the M_V = -5.7 satellite in that was discovered by belokurov-etal06-boo and has an estimated distance of 60 kpc, with a right ascension (RA) of 14^ h 00^ m 06^ s and declination of +14 30 00 ( l=358 1 and b=69 6 in Galactic coordinates). Its central surface brightness is _ 0,V 28 mag arcsec^ 2 with an optical half-light radius of 13 . munoz-etal06 obtained spectra of a number of red giant branch and asymptotic giant branch stars in \ and found a heliocentric systemic velocity of 95.6 (or =106.2 , where \ is the velocity with respect to the Local Standard of Rest) and a velocity dispersion of 6.6 . This implies a dynamical mass of 10^7 , similar to that of most low luminosity dwarfs mateo98 . The combined spectra of \ member stars are consistent with a metallicity of 2.5 , making it the most metal-poor galaxy known in the Local Group.

We find no evidence of \ emission at the velocity of . In Figure mom0 boo figure we present the zeroth moment map integrated over a velocity width of 20 centred at =106.2 , excluding RFI-affected channels.

sources.summary: Bootes I is one of the nearby ultra-faint dwarf galaxies observed with HST; the sample CMDs indicate ancient, metal-poor stellar populations and synchronized star-formation histories.

quotes.quote: Our survey observed six UFD galaxies using two cameras on HST : the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) and the Wide Field Camera 3 (WFC3). We used the F606W (broad V ) and F814W ( I ) filters on each camera, with ACS surveying an area centered on each galaxy ( 202^ 202^ per tile), and WFC3 observing in parallel in the galaxy outskirts ( 162^ 162^ per tile). For the nearby ( < 100 kpc) satellites in the sample (Bootes I, Coma Berenices, Ursa Major I), we surveyed a relatively wide area (5--12 tiles in each camera) to a shallow depth (2 or 3 orbits, faint limit of V 28 mag).

It is clear that the CMD of each UFD looks like the CMD of an ancient globular cluster, and that each is dominated by an old (age > 12 Gyr) population of metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] < -2 ) stars. A prominent blue straggler sequence (BSS) is present in each galaxy -- ubiquitous in old populations.

sources.summary: The paper models Bo otes I as an ultrafaint dwarf with inefficient star formation, little evidence for sustained outflows, and gas removal likely requiring stripping mechanisms.

quotes.quote: We present different chemical evolution models for the ultrafaint dwarf galaxy Bo otes I. We either assume that the galaxy accretes its mass through smooth infall of gas of primordial chemical composition ( classical models) or adopt mass accretion histories derived from the combination of merger trees with semi-analytical modelling ( cosmologically-motivated models). Furthermore, we consider models with and without taking into account inhomogeneous mixing in the ISM within the galaxy, i.e. homogeneous versus inhomogeneous models. The theoretical predictions are then compared to each other and to the body of the available data. From this analysis, we confirm previous findings that Bo otes I has formed stars with very low efficiency but, at variance with previous studies, we do not find a clear-cut indication that supernova explosions have sustained long-lasting galactic-scale outflows in this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats Bo otes I as an ultra-faint dwarf satellite in a homogeneous very metal-poor stellar-parameter sample for comparing dwarf-galaxy and Milky Way halo populations.

quotes.quote: We present a homogeneous set of accurate atmospheric parameters for a complete sample of very and extremely metal-poor stars in the dwarf spheroidal galaxies (dSphs) Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Sextans, Fornax, Bo otes I, Ursa Major II, and Leo IV.

The comparison between ultra-faint, classical dwarf spheroidal galaxies (UFDs, dSphs), and the Milky Way population offers a fantastic opportunity to probe different galaxy masses, star formation histories, levels of chemical enrichement. Both nucleosynthetic processes and galaxy formation models largely benefit from the diversity of the populations sampled that way.

We selected 36 stars in total in the classical dSphs Sculptor (Scl), Ursa Minor (UMi), Fornax (Fnx), and Sextans (Sex) and the ultra-faint dwarfs Bo otes I, Ursa Major II (UMa II), and Leo IV (Table Tab:spectra ). This sample covers the 4 Fe/H < -1.5 metallicity range.

sources.summary: The paper uses Martin and Koposov spectroscopy for Bo o tes I, assigns velocity-based memberships where needed, and finds systemic proper motion agreement with Simon and Helmi.

quotes.quote: Bo o tes I We use two spectroscopic catalogs for this galaxy, from Martin_07 and Koposov_11 : the former contains binary membership classification and we adopt from that study also the values of the heliocentric l.o.s. systemic velocity ( v_ LOS,sys = 99 km/s) and velocity dispersion (5 km/s); no membership classification is given in the latter study. For stars that were observed in both works, we adopt the binary classification of Martin_07 . To those that are only in the catalog of Koposov_11 , we assign probabilities on the basis of the l.o.s. velocity. Our selection is somewhat different from that of Simon_18 but we agree within one in the systemic proper motion. We also agree within 1 with the PM determination by Helmi_18 , who used a selection based on photometry.

sources.summary: The paper derives a mean heliocentric distance of 60.61 kpc for Bootes I dSph, noting it is offset from some literature values but matches a 60.4 kpc estimate.

quotes.quote: The same is the case for the dwarf galaxy Bootes I dSph. We find a mean heliocentric distance of 60.61 kpc, which is about 5 kpc off from the distance given by . However, our distance estimate matches very well the distance of 60.4 kpc by .

For the dwarf galaxy Bootes I dSph, we find a mean heliocentric distance of 60.61 kpc, which is about 5 kpc off from the distance given by and about 1.5 kpc off from the distance given by . However, our distance estimate matches very well the distance of 60.4 kpc given by .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Bo o tes I members, derives its velocity and metallicity dispersions, finds weak support for a two-component model, and favors an extra-gas chemical evolution model.

quotes.quote: We summarize our key results for each UFD below: Bo o tes I We identify 69 member stars in Bo o tes I. Using this membership catalogue, we calculate a systematic velocity of 102.6 ^ +0.7 _ 0.8 km s ^ 1 , velocity dispersion of 5.1 ^ +0.7 _ 0.8 km s ^ 1 , systematic metallicity of 2.34 ^ +0.05 _ 0.05 dex and metallicity dispersion of 0.28 ^ +0.04 _ 0.03 dex .

We find weak evidence in support of the two-component kinematic model used by kop2011 , consistent with previous findings. Combining the VLT/GIRAFFE data with data from nor2010 and lai2011 , we re-analyze the Bo o tes I MDF. We use three distributions from lai2011 (leaky box, pre-enriched leaky box and extra gas model) and find that the extra gas model (i.e. a model including infall of pristine gas while the galaxy was forming stars) best describes the MDF, suggesting that Bo o tes I formed in a similar way to low mass classical dwarf galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper investigates Boötes I outer stellar content, finding candidate blue horizontal branch members beyond the nominal tidal radius and confirming an extended stellar distribution.

quotes.quote: We here describe our investigation of the outer stellar content of the Bo o tes I ultra-faint dwarf galaxy, a satellite of the Milky Way Galaxy. We identify candidate member blue horizontal branch and blue straggler stars of Bo o tes I, both tracers of the underlying ancient stellar population, using a combination of multi-band Pan-STARRS photometry and Gaia astrometry. We find a total of twenty-four candidate blue horizontal branch member stars with apparent magnitudes and proper motions consistent with membership of Bo o tes I, nine of which reside at projected distances beyond the nominal King profile tidal radius derived from earlier fits to photometry.

We also identify four blue straggler stars of appropriate apparent magnitude to be at the distance of Bo o tes I, but all four are too faint to have high-quality astrometry from Gaia . The outer blue horizontal branch stars that we have identified confirm that the spatial distribution of the stellar population of Bo o tes I is quite extended.

sources.summary: The paper treats BoöI as an extended, tidally stretched satellite whose sparse structure made member recovery harder for the density-based clustering algorithms.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, a satisfactory recovery seemed difficult for the satellite galaxies with elongated structures or tidal tails (e.g., BoöI, TucIII). As an example, we show two satellite galaxies with different characteristics to demonstrate the hyperparameter selection. We chose RetII as one of the satellite galaxies , due to its proximity and the highest anticipated membership. Additionally, it has a significant number of spectroscopic members, with the clear clustering of stars in proper-motion space and well-constrained satellite parameters. Along with a richly populated RGB and HB, the elongated and structured spatial distribution of its stars and isophote twists ( Fellhauer_2008 , Roderick_2016 ) suggestive of tidal stretching make BoöI an ideal candidate for demonstration.

In the case of BoöI, out of 147 spectroscopic members (87 present in Gaia-DELVE catalogs), the DBSCAN (HDBSCAN) approach successfully retrieves only 76 (71) spectroscopic members. The procedures also have not recovered the five proper-motion non-members reported in Simon_2018 and the members which are spread over large area in spatial and proper-motion space.

sources.summary: The paper uses DECam u-band metallicities to select Boötes I peripheral candidates, finding known and new members beyond five half-light radii and an elongation aligned with proper motion suggesting tidal disruption.

quotes.quote: We conducted an in-depth analysis of candidate member stars located in the peripheries of three ultra-faint dwarf (UFD) galaxy satellites of the Milky Way: Boötes I (Boo1), Boötes II (Boo2), and Segue I (Seg1). Studying these peripheral stars has previously been difficult due to contamination from the Milky Way foreground. We used u band photometry from the Dark Energy Camera (DECam) to derive metallicities to efficiently select UFD candidate member stars.

This approach was validated on Boo1, where we identified both previously known and new candidate member stars beyond five half-light radii. We then applied a similar procedure to Boo2 and Seg1. Our findings hinted at evidence for tidal features in Boo1 and Seg1, with Boo1 having an elongation consistent with its proper motion and Seg1 showing some distant candidate stars, a few of which are along its elongation and proper motion.

sources.summary: The paper reports Boo as a newly discovered Boötes companion with an old metal-poor CMD, a 220 pc scale length, irregular contours suggesting tidal disruption, and very low luminosity if it is a dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: We have discovered a new companion to the Milky Way galaxy in the constellation of Bo o tes. The object has a globular cluster-like CMD, dominated by an old, metal-poor stellar population. With a characteristic scale length of 220 pc, the size of the object is typical of the Galactic dwarf spheroidal satellites. The irregular nature of the density contours suggests that it may be undergoing tidal disruption. If, as seems likely, it is a dwarf galaxy, then Boo is one of the faintest ( M_V 5 8 ) so far discovered.

sources.summary: The paper studies the Boötes I dwarf spheroidal through red-giant spectra, finding a broad metallicity range and arguing that dark matter enabled self-enrichment.

quotes.quote: THE ABUNDANCE SPREAD IN THE BO O TES I DWARF SPHEROIDAL GALAXY John E. Norris 1 , Gerard Gilmore 2 , Rosemary F.G. Wyse 3 , Mark I. Wilkinson 2,4 , V. Belokurov 2 , N. Wyn Evans 2 , Daniel B. Zucker 2 1 Research School of Astronomy Astrophysics, The Australian National University, Mount Stromlo Observatory, Cotter Road, Weston, ACT 2611, Australia; email: jen@mso.anu.edu.au 2 Institute of Astronomy, University of Cambridge, Madingley Road, Cambridge CB3 0HA, UK 3 The Johns Hopkins University, Department of Physics Astronomy, 3900 N. Charles Street, Baltimore, MD 21218, USA 4 Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of Leicester, University Road, Leicester, LE1 7RH, UK. We present medium-resolution spectra of 16 radial velocity red-giant members of the low-luminosity dwarf spheroidal (dSph) galaxy that have sufficient S/N for abundance determination, based on the strength of the Ca II K line. Assuming [ Ca/Fe ] 0.3, the abundance range in the sample is [ Fe/H ] 1.7 dex, with one star having [ Fe/H = --3.4. The dispersion is ( [ Fe/H ] ) = 0.45 0.08 -- similar to those of the Galaxy's more luminous dSph systems and Centauri. This suggests that the large mass ( 10 ^ 7 M_ ) normally assumed to foster self-enrichment and the production of chemical abundance spreads was provided by the non-baryonic material in .

sources.summary: The paper treats Boo I as an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal, using photometry and prior spectroscopy to characterize its metallicity spread, old stellar population, and possible stripping.

quotes.quote: However, some authors have identified a few red giant stars in Boo I as carbon-rich lai11,gil13b . The 2.5 r_h distance of the radial-velocity-confirmed member, Boo-1137, from the center of \ might indicate that a much more massive original system is being stripped (see Figure 1). The half-light radius of \ is about 240 pc gil13a . At first examination, \ appears to be a normal, if extended, dSph at a Galactocentric distance of about 60 kpc, with e 0.2 , and 3.7 to (at least) 1.9 in [ Fe/H ] .

sources.summary: The paper models Bootes I with closely spaced bursts, finding its metallicity distribution requires enrichment shifts and may allow a two-burst scenario if Type II supernovae drive the alpha decline.

quotes.quote: The two-burst model of brown14 has 97 of the stars in Bootes I forming in the second burst, with the two bursts being only 100 Myr apart. This is the only galaxy for which the brown14 model favours such closely spaced bursts, as well as the only case where significantly more stars form in the second burst. 26 of the 38 observed stars (68 ) in Bootes I have [ Fe/H ] >-2.5 , compared to 50 for the other five galaxies. Our model for Bootes Iis shown in the first panel of Figs. f:afefeh and f:fehhist , along with the [ Fe/H ] data from brown14 and [ /Fe ] data from gilmore13 .

sources.summary: The paper studies Bootes I with BHB distance indicators and finds its modeled distance gradient is consistent with zero within 3 sigma.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We combine the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey (DECaLS) DR8 photometry with Gaia photometry to study the 3-D structure of Bootes I, Draco, Ursa Minor, Sextans and Sculptor dwarf galaxies using blue horizontal branch (BHB) stars as distance indicators. We construct a new colour-absolute magnitude of BHB stars that we use to measure the distance gradients within the body of the dwarf galaxies.

In the case of dwarf galaxies Bootes I, Draco and Ursa Minor, the observed distance gradient is consistent with zero. Even though we do not observe statistically significant non-zero distance gradient in Bootes I, Draco and Ursa Minor, that is probably because the number of stars is too small and the uncertainty is too large to show any possible distance gradient.

sources.summary: The paper finds Bo otes I has declining alpha abundance, low Sr and Ba relative to Fe and Mg, subsolar Sr/Ba, and possible SNe Ia enrichment.

quotes.quote: We put on a firm ground the evidence for a decline in /Fe with increasing metallicity in the Bo otes I ultra-faint dwarf galaxy (UFD), that is most probably due to the ejecta of type Ia supernovae.

The kinematics and morphology of Bo otes I point towards a complex system 2011ApJ...736..146K, 2016MNRAS.461.3702R , but as far as its chemical evolution is concerned, Bo otes I, and potentially Leo IV as well, seem to simply push back the frontier, at which galaxies can reprocess the SNeIa ejecta, with a knee at lower metallicity than their more massive counterparts, as expected from a classical chemical evolution.
The Bo otes I UFD reveals a decline in /Fe and possesses a low [ /Fe ] population, with consistent evidence from the three elements for which data are available, Mg, Ca, and Ti.

sources.summary: Bo otes I is described as an elongated ultra-faint dwarf with velocity and metallicity gradients suggesting tidal effects, plus two stellar populations that may imply multiple building-block origins.

quotes.quote: Bo otes I ( =0.68 ) is another example of elongated UFD. In addition to a velocity gradient, longeard2022 shows the presence of a metallicity gradient both suggesting that Bo otes I may have also been affected by tides. On the other hand, frebel2016 reported that Bo otes I also displays two stellar populations indicating that it could originate from different building-block objects.

gmw_00002119.name: Tucana II
gmw_00002119.overview: Tucana II is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy located approximately 57 kpc from the Milky Way [arXiv:1511.06296, arXiv:2402.07976]. It is characterized by an extremely metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:2103.01962, arXiv:2308.07394] and an extended stellar halo that may have formed through an ancient galaxy merger [arXiv:2103.01962, arXiv:2307.05130].

sources.summary: The paper uses outer-star abundances and velocities to confirm Tucana II membership, infer extended chemical evolution, find no tidal-disruption evidence, and assess merger or in-situ outskirts formation.

quotes.quote: We present chemical abundances and velocities of five stars between 0.3 kpc to 1.1 kpc from the center of the Tucana II ultra-faint dwarf galaxy (UFD) from high-resolution Magellan/MIKE spectroscopy. We find that every star is deficient in metals ( 3.6< [ Fe/H ] < -1.9 ) and in neutron-capture elements as is characteristic of UFD stars, unambiguously confirming their association with Tucana II. Other chemical abundances (e.g., C, iron-peak) largely follow UFD trends and suggest that faint core-collapse supernovae (SNe) dominated the early evolution of Tucana II.

We see a downturn in [ /Fe ] at [ Fe/H ] 2.8 , indicating the onset of Type Ia SN enrichment and somewhat extended chemical evolution. The most metal-rich star has strikingly low [ Sc/Fe ] = 1.29 0.48 and [ Mn/Fe ] = 1.33 0.33 , implying significant enrichment by a sub-Chandrasekhar mass Type Ia SN. We do not detect a radial velocity gradient in Tucana II ( d v_ helio / d _1=-2.6^ +3.0 _ 2.9 km s ^ 1 kpc ^ 1 ) reflecting a lack of evidence for tidal disruption, and derive a dynamical mass of M_ 1/2 (r_h) = 1.6^ +1.1 _ 0.7 10^6 M _ . We revisit formation scenarios of the extended component of Tucana II in light of its stellar chemical abundances. We find no evidence that Tucana II had abnormally energetic SNe, suggesting that if SNe drove in-situ stellar halo formation then other UFDs should show similar such features.

sources.summary: Tucana 2 is a metal-poor UFD with dwarf-galaxy kinematics; the paper favors an ancient dwarf-dwarf merger scenario to explain its extended stellar morphology.

quotes.quote: Tucana 2 Upon first glance, Tuc2's characteristics are fairly typial compared to other UFDs. Its intrinsic brightness ( M_ V 3.9 mag; L_ V 3150 L _ ) and physical size ( r_ h = 9.83 = 0.16 ^ , or 160 pc; bechtol2015 ) places it squarely in the locus of other MW satellites in the size-luminosity relation (see Figure 2 of simon2019 and Figure 3 of bechtol2015 ). Velocity dispersion measurements also support Tuc2's classification as a dwarf galaxy ( 3.8 km s ^ 1 ; chiti2023 ), and its mean metallicity falls well below the proposed “metallicity floor” for most MW globular clusters ( 2010 version harris1996 ; wan2020 and references therein). A somewhat unique characteristic is its low metallicity, suggesting Tuc2 is one of the most metal-poor systems in the MW ( [ Fe/H ] = 2.71 dex; ji2016 ; chiti2018 ), on average. Despite these parallels to other UFDs, Tuc2's recent publicity has shown it is a particularly interesting dwarf.

The lack of evidence for both the tidal an energetic SNe scenarios led chiti2021, chiti2023 to this final hypothesis: that Tuc2's morphology and steep metallicity gradient could be the remnants of an ancient merger. Simulations of a UFD (similar to Tuc2) in tarumi2021 showed that the early (first 100s Myrs) merging of two galaxies can produce extended features in the direction of the merger, which could explain the perpendicular stellar extension in Tuc2. These simulations also showed that accreted stars from the merger can be deposited in the outskirts of the central dwarf, as the central dwarf is dynamically heated which forms an extended stellar distribution.

sources.summary: The paper observes Tucana II with WINERED as a dwarf spheroidal dark-matter target, using sky-subtracted near-infrared spectra to set stringent limits on eV dark-matter line-photon decay.

quotes.quote: Applying this concept, we have performed spectrographic observations of dwarf spheroidal galaxies (dSphs) Leo V and Tucana II using WINERED by utilizing an object-sky-object nodding observation technique for background subtraction. We present the first result from this dark matter search. Employing zero consistent flux data after the sky subtraction, we have established one of the most stringent limits to date on dark matter decaying into line photons in the mass range of 1.8-2.7 eV. Our data can also be applied to constrain other spectra of photons from the dSphs.

Observations were conducted using the WINERED attached to the 6.5m Magellan Clay telescope on July 6th and November 2nd in 2023. We used the WIDE mode with a 0.29 arcsec slit that allows us to cover the wavelength region of = 0.92 -1.35 m with a spectral resolution of R=28,000 . The observation targets were Leo V (on 6th, Jul, 2023) and Tucana II (on 2nd, Nov, 2023), which are dSphs located at a distance of 178 kpc and 57 kpc, respectively. The high-level emission originating from eV dark matter is expected due to the high central concentration of these targets ( _ max 0.07^ and 1.0^ ), see Ref. .

sources.summary: The paper studies Tucana 2 as an ultrafaint DES system, confirming it as a dwarf galaxy with large dynamical mass-to-light ratio and placing it near Grus 1 in phase space.

quotes.quote: Based on simultaneous estimates of line-of-sight velocity and stellar-atmospheric parameters, we identify \ and \ stars as probable members of Tuc 2 and and Gru 1, respectively. Our sample for Tuc 2 is sufficient to resolve an internal velocity dispersion of \ km s ^ 1 about a mean of \ km s ^ 1 (solar rest frame), and to estimate a mean metallicity of [ Fe/H ] = . These results place Tuc 2 on chemodynamical scaling relations followed by dwarf galaxies, suggesting a dominant dark matter component with dynamical mass \ M _ enclosed within the central 160 pc, and dynamical mass-to-light ratio \ M _ /L_ V, .

Here we present an initial spectroscopic study of two ultrafaint systems discovered using data from the first year of the Dark Energy Survey: Tucana 2 and Grus 1. Tuc 2 is detected as a stellar overdensity with M_ V 3.8 and R_ h 165 pc, at distance 57 kpc (K15, `DES15' hereafter des15etal ).

Table tab:summarytable summarizes the observed properties of Tuc 2 and Gru 1, combining our new spectroscopic results with the previous photometric results from K15 and DES15. Our M2FS spectroscopy confirms that Tuc 2 is a dwarf galaxy, following the well-established luminosity-metallicity relation and possessing a large dynamical mass-to-light ratio.

sources.summary: The paper measures metal-poor Tucana II members, identifies two new members, and argues that an anomalous seventh star determines whether the galaxy is a surviving first galaxy or chemically evolved.

quotes.quote: We present chemical abundance measurements for seven stars with metallicities ranging from [ Fe/H ] = 3.3 to [ Fe/H ] = 2.4 in the Tucana II ultra-faint dwarf galaxy (UFD), based on high-resolution spectra obtained with the MIKE spectrograph on the 6.5 m Magellan-Clay Telescope. For three stars, we present detailed chemical abundances for the first time. Of those, two stars are newly discovered members of Tucana II and were selected as probable members from deep narrow band photometry of the Tucana II UFD taken with the SkyMapper telescope.

The seventh star shows chemical abundances that are discrepant from the other Tucana II members and an atypical, higher strontium abundance than what is expected for typical UFD stars. While unlikely, its strontium abundance raises the possibility that it may be a foreground metal-poor halo star with the same systemic velocity as Tucana II. If we were to exclude this star, Tucana II would satisfy the criteria to be a surviving first galaxy. Otherwise, this star implies that Tucana II has likely experienced somewhat extended chemical evolution.

sources.summary: The paper compares simulations to Tucana II, arguing its extended, metal-poor stellar halo and metallicity gradient are consistent with an early major merger.

quotes.quote: These results closely resemble the properties of a recently discovered UFD, Tucana II, which is extremely metal-poor and has a spatially extended stellar halo with the more distant stars being more metal-poor. Our simulation suggests that the extended stellar halo of Tucana II may have been formed through a past major merger.

Recently, 2021Chiti_TucIIhalo discovered an unusually extended stellar population around a UFD, Tucana II (Tuc II). Seven giant stars are located at distances larger than twice the half-light radius. The farthest star is more than 1 kpc away from the center. Considering the small sample size of 19 giants, the overall size of the stellar density distribution is indeed significant. Importantly, the extended stars are likely members of Tuc II because they have physical properties in agreement with those of the more centrally located stars: the systemic radial velocity ( 129.1 \ km \ s^ 1 ) and a very low metallicity ( [ Fe / H ] = -2.77 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Tucana 2 as an ultra-faint dwarf in SIDM constraints, adopting its average central density, luminosity radius, infall mass, and dark-matter-dominated systematics.

quotes.quote: These constraints rely on present-day measurements of each dwarf's central density, which are provided in Table  tab:inputs . Note that for Draco and Ursa Minor, we use _ 150 , defined as the density at 150 pc. For Segue 1 and Tucana 2, we use the average central density, _ 1/2 , within r_ 1/2 , the radius within which half of the galaxy's stellar luminosity is enclosed ( r_ 1/2 =36  pc for Segue 1  and r_ 1/2 =165  pc for Tucana 2  ).

For the ultra-faint dwarfs, Segue 1 and Tucana 2, the infall masses are taken to be 10^9 M_ , based on the approximate upper limit of high resolution cosmological simulations with low stellar content  . We assume here that infall properties and star formation physics are not significantly modified by self interactions, but this remains to be tested with simulations.
Second, Segue 1 and Tucana 2 are more DM dominated than either Draco or Ursa Minor, and thus have a different set of observational systematics.

sources.summary: The paper derives the HST-based color-magnitude diagram and star formation history of Tucana 2, finding early stellar-mass assembly and quenching older than 11.5 Gyr within a seven-UFD comparison.

quotes.quote: We present the color-magnitude diagrams and star formation histories (SFHs) of seven ultra-faint dwarf galaxies: Horologium 1, Hydra 2, Phoenix 2, Reticulum 2, Sagittarius 2, Triangulum 2, and Tucana 2, derived from high-precision Hubble Space Telescope photometry. We find that the SFH of each galaxy is consistent with them having created at least 80 of the stellar mass by z 6 . For all galaxies, we find quenching times older than 11.5 Gyr ago, compatible with the scenario in which reionization suppresses the star formation of small dark matter halos. However, our analysis also reveals some differences in the SFHs of candidate Magellanic Cloud satellites, i.e., galaxies that are likely satellites of the Large Magellanic Cloud and that entered the Milky Way potential only recently.

On average, we find _ 90 = 12.06 0.72 Gyr ago for Magellanic satellites and _ 90 = 12.68 0.23 Gyr ago for non-Magellanic satellites (this becomes 12.67 0.41 if we exclude Sag 2); even though they are comparable within the errors, there is a marginal difference between these values (a two-sample KS test of the average distributions gives a p-value of 5.7 10^ 10 ). Indeed, the candidate Magellanic satellites appear to have more prolonged SFHs compared to non-Magellanic ones, possibly due to the fact that they should have resided in low-density environments for most of their lifetimes; however, the uncertainties are too large to reach a definitive conclusion.

sources.summary: The paper reviews differing Tucana II size measurements, declines new structural fitting because HST covered only the inner galaxy, and cites spectroscopy confirming it as a dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: Tucana II The discovery parameters for Tuc II from and were quite different, with the former reporting an azimuthally-averaged r_h of 9 8 ( a_ h, exp 12 9 ) and the latter publishing an a_ h, p of 7 2 . This discrepancy could be due in part to different CMD masking techniques for member selection. While note that there was some elongation in the outer regions of the detected overdensity, they suggest that the distortion was likely noise-related and show that after their CMD-masking, the overdensity is much rounder.

Our FOV included four ACS pointings, for a maximum field-width of less than 7 . We did not fit the structural parameters as though literature a_h measurements varied from 7 13 5 , our FOV cover at most the inner one-half of the galaxy. Using eight probable member stars with spectroscopy from the Michigan/Magellan Fiber System (M2FS), measured a velocity dispersion for Tuc II of 8.6 ^ +4.4 _ 2.7 and a metallicity dispersion of 0.23 ^ +0.18 _ 0.13 dex and concluded that it is a dwarf galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses Walker memberships for Tucana II, keeps 19 stars, finds most form a proper-motion clump, and downweights scattered faint members.

quotes.quote: Tucana II We use the catalog of Walker_16 . It provides non binary memberships. With our standard cuts we obtain 19 members stars. Most of those form a clump in proper motion space. Some of the others are more scattered, but since they are fainter they not matter much for the overall motion.

sources.summary: The paper studies Tucana II with SkyMapper photometry, deriving stellar metallicities, membership probabilities, new candidate members, and a metallicity distribution function.

quotes.quote: We present a study of the ultra-faint Milky Way dwarf satellite galaxy Tucana II using deep photometry from the 1.3 m SkyMapper telescope at Siding Spring Observatory, Australia. We thus derive photometric metallicities and surface gravities for all stars down to a magnitude of g 20 within 75 arcminutes of Tucana II. By incorporating Gaia proper motions, we derive quantitative membership probabilities which recover all known members on the red giant branch of Tucana II. Additionally, we identify multiple likely new members in the center of the system and candidate members several half-light radii from the center of the system. Finally, we present a metallicity distribution function derived from the photometric metallicities of likely Tucana II members.

sources.summary: The paper uses Walker et al. data for Tucana II, notes membership-weighted dispersions disagree without a Milky Way background model, and includes two uncertain stars for a 10-star sample.

quotes.quote: The data for Grus I and Tucana II is from Walker2016ApJ...819...53W . We find that simply weighting the stars by their reported mean membership values gives a result that disagrees with the values reported in Walker2016ApJ...819...53W . We speculate that this disagreement is due to our lack of a Milky Way background model; there are several stars with large errors on the dSph membership probabilities that could be driving the dispersions apart. In order to reproduce the Walker2016ApJ...819...53W values, we exclude (include) the two stars in Grus I (Tucana II) with large membership errors but non-zero membership giving a sample of 5 (10) stars.

sources.summary: The paper uses Tucana II as a tidally isolated ultra-faint dwarf example, noting anisotropic member stars beyond its half-light radii and merger explanations for its extended halo.

quotes.quote: A notable example is Tucana II, a LG UFD located at a distance of 58 kpc away from the MW pace2022 , which exhibits multiple features characteristic of tidal isolation chiti2021 . chiti2021, chiti2022 have recently reported member stars anisotropically distributed around Tucana II, up to nine half-light radii from its galactic centre. These member stars reach out to and even extend past our calculated tidal radius estimates of Tucana II, r_ t 0.76 kpc.
For example, tarumi2021 argue that galaxy mergers could explain the extended stellar halo around Tucana II.

sources.summary: The paper finds Tuc2 has an orbital pole compatible within errors, but its velocity components do not match LMC debris, making LMC membership unfavorable.

quotes.quote: Ret2, Tuc2 and Gru1 have velocity components that are not consistent within 3 sigma of our predictions and are therefore less favorable.

Dra2, Tuc2, Hya2 and Gru1 are also consistent within their measured 1 errors, where the errors in the poles are calculated as standard deviation of 1000 Monte Carlo drawings over the measurement errors in Galactocentric X , Y , Z and V_X , V_Y and V_Z .
Ret2, Tuc2 and Gru1 have velocity components that are not consistent within 3 sigma of our predictions and are unlikely members of the LMC group according to our analysis.

sources.summary: Tucana II is used as an observed example of an extended stellar halo, with member stars far out and merger-built models reproducing a similar halo.

quotes.quote: Another interesting morphological feature is the extended stellar halo of the Tucana II galaxy chiti2021 revealing either a strong bursty feedback process or an early galactic merger.

Moreover extended stellar structures have been observed in Tucana II chiti2021 with member stars found up to 9 half-light radii. Such an extended halo is reproduced here by the merger of clusters ( h315 and h273 ), a scenario also supported by tarumi2021 .

gmw_00002120.name: TDG1
gmw_00002120.overview: TDG1 is an optical tidal dwarf galaxy candidate located along a tidal tail [arXiv:0910.2351]. The galaxy exhibits very blue optical colors, and its stellar populations appear older than those of the nearby blended ultraviolet source NUV1 [arXiv:0910.2351].

sources.summary: TDG1 is one of three optical tidal-tail TDG candidates; its UV light is blended with NUV1, it is very blue, and its pixels appear older than NUV1.

quotes.quote: We found three optical objects which appear to be related with the NUV surface brightness distribution along the tail. Optical sources around the tidal tail are indicated as ``TDG1,'' ``TDG2,'' and ``TDG3.'' However, note that none of them is located exactly at the peaks in the NUV light.

In the case of ``TDG1'', its UV light is blended with ``NUV1'' and we can see a faint optical counterpart for ``NUV1'' (the inset image in the right panel of Figure uvknots ). Both of them show very blue optical colors ( g - = 0.12 and -0.26 for ``TDG1'' and ``NUV1,'' respectively). In fact, the pixel-by-pixel analysis in Section sec:pbp showed that ``NUV1p'' has significantly younger stellar populations ( 5 Myr) than ``TDG1p'' ( 200 Myr).

gmw_00002121.name: KKH 98
gmw_00002121.overview: KKH 98 is an isolated dwarf spheroidal candidate [arXiv:2106.02672] located approximately 2.5 Mpc away [arXiv:1002.0592]. The galaxy has experienced relatively constant, low-level star formation throughout much of cosmic time [arXiv:1002.0592], though it may have undergone a starburst about 500 million years ago [arXiv:2106.02672].

sources.summary: The paper uses Keck AO and HST resolved-star CMDs to reconstruct KKH 98 star formation history, finding tight IR AGB sequences and persistent low-level star formation.

quotes.quote: We investigate the utility of the asymptotic giant branch (AGB) and the red giant branch (RGB) as probes of the star formation history (SFH) of the nearby ( D=2.5 Mpc) dwarf irregular galaxy, KKH 98. Near-infrared (IR) Keck Laser Guide Star Adaptive Optics (AO) images resolve 592 IR bright stars reaching over 1 magnitude below the Tip of the Red Giant Branch. Significantly deeper optical (F475W and F814W) Hubble Space Telescope images of the same field contain over 2500 stars, reaching to the Red Clump and the Main Sequence turn-off for 0.5 Gyr old populations. Compared to the optical color magnitude diagram (CMD), the near-IR CMD shows significantly tighter AGB sequences, providing a good probe of the intermediate age (0.5 - 5 Gyr) populations.

We match observed CMDs with stellar evolution models to recover the SFH of KKH 98. On average, the galaxy has experienced relatively constant low-level star formation ( 5 10^ 4 M _ yr ^ 1 ) for much of cosmic time.

sources.summary: The paper describes KKH 98 as an isolated dwarf spheroidal candidate with an H I halo, possible starburst about 5e8 years ago, and possible two-dwarf merger origin.

quotes.quote: The nearest large galaxy to KKH 98 is IC 10, 1.8 Mpc away; the closest known dwarfs are IC 5152, GR 8, and KKR 3, at 1.0 Mpc . I think the nearest large galaxy to KKH 18 is M 81, 1.3 Mpc away . The images of these three objects look to me like dwarf spheroidals, with no obvious signs of dust revealed by obscuration or scattered starlight. All three have detected HI halos, suggesting isolated evolution appropriate for their isolated present positions , though I don't know whether the HI is contributing to star formation.
KKH 98 may have had a starburst about 5 10^8 years ago . Maybe, like KK 246, KKH 98 is the remnant of the merger of two dwarfs that left a dwarf spheroidal with an HI halo.

gmw_00002122.name: NGC 6338
gmw_00002122.overview: NGC 6338 is a cD elliptical or lenticular galaxy that serves as the brightest member of a merging galaxy group [arXiv:2009.06656, arXiv:1901.08769]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus with radio-filled X-ray cavities [arXiv:1202.1364, arXiv:2312.02283], an X-shaped central radio source [arXiv:1901.08769], and complex structures of dust knots and ionized gas filaments [arXiv:1411.6040, arXiv:astro-ph/0411148].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 6338 as the large elliptical brightest galaxy of the more massive group, separated from VII Zw 700 and showing H filaments, cool gas, and possible X-ray cavities.

quotes.quote: Satsuki and Mei are associated with the less massive northern component, while the nearby large elliptical galaxy NGC 6338 is the brightest galaxy of the more massive group (Figure fig_cluster ). The dry merger (Satsuki and Mei) and NGC 6338 are separated by 42 kpc in projection and by 1400 km s ^ 1 in velocity. The two merging groups are expected to form a galaxy cluster in the future.

also show that the H gas of NGC 6338 consists of three diffuse filaments in the southeast and northwest quadrants, extending out to 9 kpc from the nucleus. The H filaments have also been revealed in previous observations by HST and the CALIFA Calar Alto Legacy Integral Field Area Survey San12 . survey Mar04,Pan12,Gom16 . Moreover, show that both NGC 6338 and VII Zw 700 contain potential X-ray cavities, which would indicate past AGN jet activity. In addition, both systems are observed to contain cool IGM structures correlated with the H emission see also Pan12 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Chandra data for NGC 6338, finding central X-ray cavities, cooler filaments, radio-filled cavities, and mechanical jet power dominating radio luminosity.

quotes.quote: We present results based on the systematic analysis of currently available Chandra archive data on the brightest galaxy in the Draco constellation NGC 6338, in order to investigate the properties of the X-ray cavities. In the central 6 kpc, at least a two and possibly three, X-ray cavities are evident. All these cavities are roughly of ellipsoidal shapes and show a decrement in the surface brightness of several tens of percent. In addition to these cavities, a set of X-ray bright filaments are also noticed which are spatially coincident with the filaments over an extent of 15 kpc.

Radio emission maps derived from the analysis of 1.4 GHz VLA FIRST survey data failed to show any association of these X-ray cavities with radio jets, however, the cavities are filled by radio emission. The total power of the cavities is 17 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 and the ratio of the radio luminosity to cavity power is 10^ 4 , implying that most of the jet power is mechanical.

sources.summary: NGC 6338 has two or three core X-ray cavities, luminous X-ray/H filaments, a companion pair, knotty inner dust and H filaments, and an inner abundance drop.

quotes.quote: NGC 6338 NGC 6338 has two, maybe three, X-ray cavities within just 6 kpc of its core, and luminous X-ray filaments which coincide with the H filaments Pandge12 . It forms a pair with MCG+10-24-117, which lies to its north Pandge12 . show that the dust distribution in the innermost 5 kpc of NGC 6338 consists of knots, while there are also H filaments in the same region. As can be seen from the radial abundance profile (Fig. fig:abundprofspossible ), there is an abundance drop in the inntermost 7 kpc, which correspond to the innermost two spectral bins.
We estimate that the missing iron mass, with respect to 1.3 times the Solar metallicity, for these two bins is 1.9 10 ^ 6 M _ . We were unable to obtain a dust mass measurement from the literature.

sources.summary: NGC 6338 is described as the cD/S0 brightest galaxy interacting with 2MASX J1715 and hosting an X-shaped central radio source whose lobes were inflated by AGN jets.

quotes.quote: The brightest galaxy NGC 6338 (cD, S0, M_ B =13.6 ; z=0.02743 ) and the member galaxy 2MASX J17152326+5725585 hereafter 2MASX J1715; E0, M_ B =15.4 ; z=0.03212 ; smit2004 present some evidence of galaxy-galaxy interaction in the optical band, as shown on the SDSS image (Fig. 1a ). Both NGC 6338 and 2MASX J1715 (located north about 1.3^ from NGC 6338) are elongated in the north-south direction; In particular, the latter contains a double nucleus Fig. 1a berl2006 , and has an asymmetrical optical morphology that are possibly due to tidal forces. In the VLA NVSS map Fig. 1a cond1998 , a point-like radio source is shown at the center of galaxy NGC 6338, as reported by dong2010 and pand2012 .

In the 1.4 GHz map, the radio source in the center of galaxy NGC 6338 is resolved, and the radio emission displays an X-shaped appearance. The radio lobes, extending to the north-east and south-west directions, are associated with two possible X-ray cavities (Fig. 6), which pand2012 found by analyzing Chandra X-ray data. These indicate that these two radio lobes were inflated by jets originating from the central AGN activity.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 6338 as a merging galaxy group with radio-filled X-ray cavities, a core-plus-jet morphology, and a radio source aligned with inner cavities but misaligned with the older outburst.

quotes.quote: NGC 6338 : merging galaxy group at z=0.027. The multifrequency study of this object has been carried out by 2019MNRAS.488.2925O , who found radio-filled X-ray cavities in both groups. For our analysis we focus on the southern, larger subgroup, that has two inner X-ray cavities oriented northeast -- southwest, and at least one X-ray cavity on larger scales, west of the core (see also 2020MNRAS.496.2613B,2023ApJ...948..101S ).

The VLBA data reveal a core + jet morphology. The source appears one-sided, although at a level of 3 ( 0.4 mJy/beam) there are indications for a counterjet. The radio source is oriented northeast -- southwest, thus being aligned with the inner X-ray cavities but misaligned w..t. the older, outer outburst.

sources.summary: NGC 6338 is described as a poor-cluster cD galaxy whose dust knots and ionized gas clumps and filaments are aligned on different scales and trace one another closely.

quotes.quote: NGC 6338 --- Fig. 1(h) : NGC 6338 is the cD galaxy of a poor cluster. Its dust distribution consists of several knots lying along PA 160 . The separation between the northernmost and southernmost knots is 3 kpc ( 5 3 ). The ionized gas consists of two luminous, compact clumps in the central arcsecond of the galaxy and of several broken and diffuse filaments extending out to 7.5 kpc ( 13 5 ) in the south-east and north-west quadrants.

The southern clump coincides with the nucleus of the galaxy. The central clumps are roughly oriented along the major axis of the galaxy while the large-scale filaments lie along the minor axis. Similar to NGC 6166, the dust knots trace the gas morphology very well.

gmw_00002123.name: ID=1
gmw_00002123.overview: The infrared galaxy ID=1 is characterized by a single photometric data point in the far-infrared regime [arXiv:1704.03655]. This sparse data, or potentially an overestimated flux density at 90 micrometers due to deblending issues, prevents the galaxy from being well-fitted by standard energy balance models [arXiv:1704.03655].

sources.summary: The paper says ID=1 could not be fit by SEABASs: its FIR model underpredicted 90 m flux, perhaps from sparse FIR data or deblending-inflated 90 m flux.

quotes.quote: It should be noted that one object with ID=1 cannot be well-fitted by SEABASs code. Its best-fit SED in FIR region provided significantly lower than observed flux at 90 . One possible reason is that this object has only one photometric point in the FIR regime that could not be enough to constrain the FIR SEDs. Another possibility is that its 90 m flux density might be overestimated due to the deblending issue and thus SEABASs with considering energy balance between UV/optical and IR cannot reproduce the FIR emission. Therefore, we excluded this object for the SED fitting and we derive the stellar mass, total IR luminosity, and SFR for the remaining 7 DOGs with ID=2-8 (see Table table ).

gmw_00002124.name: ULASJ1234+0907
gmw_00002124.overview: ULASJ1234+0907 is a heavily reddened, unlensed Type 1 quasar [arXiv:1312.0010, arXiv:1805.09825] with a very massive host galaxy [arXiv:2103.10174] and a supermassive black hole [arXiv:1203.5530]. The system is surrounded by companion galaxies [arXiv:2001.05507, arXiv:2103.10174] and exhibits extreme starburst activity located within 8 kiloparsecs of its central black hole [arXiv:1603.06419].

sources.summary: The paper presents ULASJ1234+0907 as an extremely red z=2.503 Type 1 quasar whose HyLIRG host, high star formation rate, X-ray luminosity, dust, and unlensed nature match an AGN blowout phase.

quotes.quote: Herschel observations indicate that the quasar host is a hyperluminous infrared galaxy (HyLIRG) with a total infrared luminosity of log _ 10 (L _ IR /L _ )=13.90 0.02. A greybody fit gives a dust temperature of T _d =60 3K assuming an emissivity index of =1.5 , considerably higher than in submillimeter bright galaxies observed at similar redshifts. The star formation rate is estimated to be > 2000M _ yr ^ 1 even accounting for a significant contribution from an AGN component to the total infrared luminosity or requiring that only the far infra-red luminosity is powered by a starburst.

Through very deep optical and near infra-red imaging of the field at sub-arcsecond seeing, we demonstrate that despite its extreme luminosity, it is highly unlikely that ULASJ1234+0907 is being lensed. We measure a neutral hydrogen column density of N _H = 9.0 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 corresponding to A _V 6 . The observed properties of ULASJ1234+0907 - high luminosity and Eddington ratio, broad lines, moderate column densities and significant infra-red emission from re-processed dust - are similar to those predicted by galaxy formation simulations for the AGN blowout phase. The high Eddington ratio combined with the presence of significant amounts of dust, is expected to drive strong outflows due to the effects of radiation pressure on dust. We conclude that ULASJ1234+0907 is a prototype galaxy caught at the peak epoch of galaxy formation, which is transitioning from a starburst to optical quasar via a dusty quasar phase.

sources.summary: The paper studies ULASJ1234 + 0907 as a heavily reddened quasar host with CO observations and a high-excitation CO SLED attributed to AGN heating.

quotes.quote: Heavily Reddened Quasars and Companions The sample studied consists of three heavily reddened quasars from Banerji:12, Banerji:15 ULASJ0123 + 1525 ( z_ H =2.630 ), ULASJ1234 + 0907 ( z_ H =2.503 ) and ULASJ2315 + 0143 ( z_ H =2.561 ) - where CO(3-2) emission has already been detected (B17).

We have, therefore, re-binned the spectral axis by factors of 4, 7, 7 and 4 for ULASJ0123, ULASJ1234, G1234N and ULASJ2315 respectively, to provide five or six resolution elements across the the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the line, as inferred from the CO(3-2) observations in B17 and Paper II. The CO(1-0) maps together with the binned CO(1-0) spectra for the four sources are shown in Fig. fig:CO10_spec . We model the CO(1-0) line profiles using a single Gaussian and derive line properties (see Table tab:all_obs ) from the model.

The ULASJ1234 quasar host galaxy has a CO SLED peaking at J>6 , which is inconsistent with even the most infrared luminous star-forming galaxies such as ULIRGs and SMGs Papadopoulos:12,Bothwell:13 and could almost certainly be ascribed to heating from the luminous AGN. The gas excitation is not as extreme as seen in the Cloverleaf quasar for example, but lies somewhere between the quasar and SMG/ULIRG values.

sources.summary: The paper finds ULASJ1234+0907 has ALMA-detected nearby sources at similar redshift; SCUBA-2 does not detect the quasar, while two companion galaxies appear brighter at 850 m.

quotes.quote: Two of the reddened quasars in our sample (ULASJ1234+0907 and ULASJ2315+0143) have also been observed with ALMA banerji17,banerji18 in Band 3 and Band 6, which trace the and continuum emission. With the higher resolution of the ALMA observations ( 2-3 arcsec compared to 15 arcsec with SCUBA-2), banerji18 find that both ULASJ1234+0907 and ULASJ2315+0143 have multiple sources present near the quasar. The locations of the ALMA continuum detected sources are overlaid on the SCUBA-2 850 m maps in Fig. fig:alma . With ALMA, banerji18 also detect multiple molecular emission lines from these sources and in both ULASJ1234+0907 and ULASJ2315+0143. The additional ALMA sources marked in Fig. fig:alma are found to lie at a similar redshift to the quasar, confirming that they are part of the same overdensity.

We note that ULASJ1234+0907 is undetected with SCUBA-2, lying below the 3 detection limit of our sample. However, the two companion galaxies near the quasar appear to have higher 850 m flux densities (7.48 2.03 mJy and 5.92 2.03 mJy) than the quasar.

sources.summary: The paper identifies ULASJ1234+0907 as the reddest quasar in its sample, with heavy extinction, extreme bolometric luminosity, a very massive black hole, and unlikely lensing.

quotes.quote: ULASJ1234+0907 : ULASJ1234+0907 is our reddest quasar with (H-K) =2.3 and an inferred dust extinction of A _V =6.0 derived from this colour. The extinction corrected bolometric luminosity for this source is L _ bol > 10 ^ 48 erg s ^ 1 and the inferred black-hole mass is > 10 ^ 10 M _ from the very broad H line seen in Fig. fig:spectra . This makes ULASJ1234+0907 one of the most bolometrically luminous and massive black-holes known at z 2.

As discussed in detail in Section sec:disc , the extinction-corrected absolute magnitude for ULASJ1234+0907 is M _i 31-- 32 which is comparable to the known lensed reddened quasar PKS0132 at similar redshifts Hall:02, Gregg:02 . Our ongoing follow-up observations of ULASJ1234+0907 at long wavelengths will enable a detailed characterisation of its host galaxy properties, shedding light on the reason for the extreme luminosity and dust extinction. Although we cannot exclude lensing as a possible reason for the extreme luminosity of this source we discuss why this is statistically improbable in Section sec:lens .

sources.summary: The paper constrains ULASJ1234+0907 to have an extreme far-infrared starburst whose H-alpha signal should be detectable outside 8 kpc, concluding the starburst lies within 8 kpc of the black hole.

quotes.quote: The total star formation taking place in ULASJ1234+0907 has been constrained as 2000 M_ yr ^ 1 from far infra-red observations Banerji14a , enabling a comparison to the limits on the level of unobscured star formation derived from our H narrow-band imaging. Given the extreme starburst luminosity of ULASJ1234+0907 and even accounting for a dust-extinction of A_V=6 mags as measured towards the central black-hole Banerji12 , the H flux due to star formation is expected to be 3.4 10 ^ 17 erg/s/cm ^2 .

Assuming this star formation is occurring over a region of 6 kpc or 0.75 arcsec consistent with observations of SMGs Swinbank06, Alaghband-Zadeh12, Menendez-Delmestre13 , this means that the H flux per pixel due to star formation is expected to be 7 10 ^ 18 erg/s/cm ^2 . The noise in our co-added narrow-band image is 1.7 10 ^ 18 erg/s/cm ^2 per pixel so we should have detected this star formation at the 4 level if it was occurring at radii > 8 kpc from the central black-hole. We conclude therefore that the starburst in ULASJ1234+0907 is located within 8 kpc of the central black-hole.

sources.summary: The paper presents ULASJ1234+0907 as the prototype and reddest known broad-line quasar, with high Eddington ratio, intermediate gas column, and X-ray/far-infrared luminosity suggesting post-starburst black-hole growth.

quotes.quote: Our survey also led to the identification of the prototype quasar: ULASJ1234+0907, the reddest broad-line quasar currently known. With an Eddington ratio of 0.65 (B12) and a neutral hydrogen column-density of 9 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 Banerji:14 , ULASJ1234+0907 lies in the sparsley-populated high L/L_ Edd and intermediate gas column regime found from the statistics of hard X-ray sources. Among the most luminous quasars known at both X-ray and far-infrared wavelengths, ULASJ1234+0907 may represent the emerging phase of a supermassive black-hole shortly after a starburst Banerji:14 . Larger surveys for NIR-selected, dust-obscured, luminous quasars may unearth more objects like ULASJ1234+0907.

sources.summary: ULASJ1234+0907 is modeled as a very massive, heavily reddened quasar host with two massive companions, a low molecular gas fraction, and optically obscured star formation.

quotes.quote: Two of the quasars from the sample - ULASJ1234+0907 ( z 2.50 ; J1234 hereafter) and ULASJ2315+0143 ( z 2.56 ; J2315 hereafter) - were marginally resolved in the Cycle 3 data and were therefore re-observed during ALMA Cycle 4 at 3 better angular resolution with the aim of spatially resolving the gas emission in both sources and constraining the gas dynamics.

J1234 has a very massive host – M _ dyn 5 10^ 11 M _ and two companion galaxies that are similarly massive located within 200 kpc of the quasar. The molecular gas fraction is low ( 6 ). The significant ongoing star formation in the host galaxy is entirely obscured at rest-frame UV and optical wavelengths.

sources.summary: The paper says J1234 was previously targeted, modeled over 0.2-10 keV, and has measured column densities consistent with earlier absorbed power-law fits.

quotes.quote: J1234 and J2315 were targetted through our previous programs (PI McMahon and Banerji, respectively), with the former data published in Banerji14 .

For subsequent analyses of these sources, we limit to the energy ranges where there is significant signal above the background: 0.2 - 10 keV for J1234, J2200, and J2315; 0.2 - 4.5 keV for J2243; 0.5 - 10 keV for J1216 and J1539; and 0.5 - 4.5 keV for J2314.
For J1234, J1539, and J2315 the column densities measured are in agreement with previous works, which also used simple absorbed power law models, within the uncertainties ( Banerji14,Martocchia17 ).

gmw_00002125.name: G1234N
gmw_00002125.overview: G1234N is a CO-luminous companion galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.514 [arXiv:2103.10174]. It lies at a projected distance of 170 kpc from the quasar host galaxy J1234 and has an estimated gas fraction of 14 to 18 percent [arXiv:2103.10174].

sources.summary: G1234N is a CO-luminous companion 170 kpc from J1234; ALMA resolves its CO size along with the J1234 system and infers a 14-18 percent gas fraction.

quotes.quote: The quasar host galaxy J1234 ( z=2.501 ) and its two CO-luminous companions - G1234N ( z=2.514 ; 170 kpc projected distance from the quasar) and G1234S ( z=2.497 ; 90 kpc projected distance from the quasar) - are from hereon referred to as the J1234 system.

The results from the two fitting approaches are consistent for all the galaxies and the full-width-half-maximum (FWHM) of the major axis is (0.48 0.14) , (0.57 0.18) and (0.53 0.24) for ULASJ1234, G1234N and G1234S respectively, after deconvolving from the beam. The FWHM values can be converted to a half-light radius using the equation, R _ 1/2 =0.5 FWHM for a Gaussian profile, and correspond to physical scales of 2 kpc at the redshifts of these galaxies. We summarise the half-light radius constraints for all three galaxies in Table tab:dynmod .
G1234N has a gas fraction of 14-18 whereas in G1234S the gas fraction is more uncertain owing to the discrepant gas masses from the CO and CI observations, and could be in the range 7-90 .

gmw_00002126.name: G1234S
gmw_00002126.overview: G1234S is a CO-luminous companion galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.497 [arXiv:2103.10174]. It lies at a projected distance of 90 kpc from the quasar host galaxy J1234 and exhibits a broad wing in its CO(7-6) emission line, which may indicate a dense molecular outflow [arXiv:2103.10174].

sources.summary: G1234S is a CO-luminous companion 90 kpc from J1234; its CO(7-6) line shows a broad wing, possibly a dense molecular outflow without detected AGN evidence.

quotes.quote: The quasar host galaxy J1234 ( z=2.501 ) and its two CO-luminous companions - G1234N ( z=2.514 ; 170 kpc projected distance from the quasar) and G1234S ( z=2.497 ; 90 kpc projected distance from the quasar) - are from hereon referred to as the J1234 system.

A double-peaked line profile is evident in the CO(7-6) emission from G1234S, which matches what is seen in the CO(3-2) emission. However for this particular galaxy we also see a very broad wing in the CO(7-6) emission extending out to velocities of > 1000 km/s. Note - the blue side of the broad-wing is not traced by our observations as the CI(2-1) emission line affects this portion of the spectrum. Curiously, no such broad wings are seen in the CO(3-2) line profile of this galaxy. If the broad wing is ascribed to a molecular outflow, it appears to only be affecting the dense gas in the galaxy.

gmw_00002127.name: ULASJ2315+0143
gmw_00002127.overview: ULASJ2315+0143 is a heavily reddened quasar [arXiv:1805.09825, arXiv:2103.10174] that is resolved as a close-separation major merger of two dusty galaxies [arXiv:2001.05507, arXiv:2103.10174]. The system features a high molecular gas fraction [arXiv:2103.10174] and hosts active star formation [arXiv:2001.05507, arXiv:2103.10174] alongside an overmassive black hole [arXiv:2103.10174].

sources.summary: The paper detects ULASJ2315+0143 at 850 m and cites ALMA dust continuum showing a close dusty-galaxy merger, with SED modelling indicating star formation contributes to the emission.

quotes.quote: Of the 19 quasars, only three sources are detected at > 3 confidence. These are ULASJ1216-0313, ULASJ2200+0056 and ULASJ2315+0143 and the corresponding 850 m maps can be seen in Fig. fig:detect .

Based on the model in however, we cannot rule out that the 850 m flux of ULASJ2315+0143 derives entirely from quasar heating. However, ALMA observations of ULASJ2315+0143 banerji18 have detected the dust continuum emission from this source at an observed frame wavelength of (rest-frame 330 m) where contributions from quasar heating are expected to be negligible even when considering the most extreme quasar models. Full SED modelling of this source in leads to an estimated star formation rate of 680 M _ yr ^ 1 . The source is additionally shown to be a close separation merger of two separate dusty galaxies. Thus at least some of the 850um emission in ULASJ2315+0143 is likely to be originating from dust heated by star formation.

sources.summary: The paper shows ULASJ2315 + 0143 is a close-separation merger whose CO and continuum properties differ across components, with integrated gas excitation unlike ULASJ1234.

quotes.quote: Heavily Reddened Quasars and Companions The sample studied consists of three heavily reddened quasars from Banerji:12, Banerji:15 ULASJ0123 + 1525 ( z_ H =2.630 ), ULASJ1234 + 0907 ( z_ H =2.503 ) and ULASJ2315 + 0143 ( z_ H =2.561 ) - where CO(3-2) emission has already been detected (B17).

fig:dustcont , we have seen that ULASJ2315 is a merger of two galaxies. Although our JVLA observations lack the spatial resolution to be able to resolve the two components, the peak in the CO(1-0) emission is spatially coincident with the northern companion galaxy rather than the quasar.

In ULASJ2315 on the other hand, the CO(1-0) emission does not spatially resolve the two components of the major merger. Considering the integrated line properties from both galaxies suggests that the CO SLED peaks at lower-J compared to ULASJ1234 and the SLED is in fact consistent with that seen in both ULIRGs and SMGs despite the presence of the luminous quasar. The ULASJ2315 CO SLED, integrated across both components of the merger, is also in good agreement with simulations of galaxy mergers Bournaud:15 , which were designed to match the observed excitation properties of distant galaxies.

sources.summary: ULASJ2315+0143 is a heavily reddened quasar resolved into a 15 kpc major merger with high gas fraction, UV star-forming regions, and an overmassive black hole.

quotes.quote: Two of the quasars from the sample - ULASJ1234+0907 ( z 2.50 ; J1234 hereafter) and ULASJ2315+0143 ( z 2.56 ; J2315 hereafter) - were marginally resolved in the Cycle 3 data and were therefore re-observed during ALMA Cycle 4 at 3 better angular resolution with the aim of spatially resolving the gas emission in both sources and constraining the gas dynamics.

J2315 is resolved into a close-separation major-merger ( =15 kpc; v=170 km/s) with a 1:2 mass ratio. The total dynamical mass is estimated to be 10 ^ 11 M _ and the molecular gas fraction is high ( > 45 ). A new HSC image of the galaxy shows unobscured UV-luminous star-forming regions co-incident with the extended reservoir of cold molecular gas in the merger.
While J1234 is consistent with the simulated z 2 relation, J2315 has a black hole that is over-massive relative to its host galaxy.

sources.summary: ULASJ2315+0143 is the reddest quasar in the sample; ALMA shows resolved CO and much higher obscured star formation than the UV-derived SFR.

quotes.quote: The reddest quasar in our sample - ULASJ2315+0143 - returns an E(B-V)_ QSO 0.7 larger than E(B-V)_ QSO,B , although the uncertainty on this value is much larger than for the rest of the sample.

One of these four quasars - ULASJ2315+0143 - overlaps with our sample, and shows resolved CO(3-2) emission elongated in the same direction as the rest-frame UV emission seen in Fig. fig:colour16 . The ALMA-derived SFR for this source is 6100 400 M _ yr ^ 1 and whilst this is significantly larger than the 230M _ yr ^ 1 UV-derived SFR inferred from the DES observations, we note that the ALMA SFR is derived from a single photometric data point at =103GHz tracing the Rayleigh-Jeans tail of the dust SED, and could be over-estimated for a number of reasons.
In fact, we see evidence for a much higher obscured SFR in ULASJ2315+0143 based on recent ALMA observations and cannot rule out a similar trend in the remainder of our sample.

sources.summary: The paper says J2315 was previously targeted, modeled over 0.2-10 keV, and has column densities consistent with earlier absorbed power-law fits.

quotes.quote: J1234 and J2315 were targetted through our previous programs (PI McMahon and Banerji, respectively), with the former data published in Banerji14 .

For subsequent analyses of these sources, we limit to the energy ranges where there is significant signal above the background: 0.2 - 10 keV for J1234, J2200, and J2315; 0.2 - 4.5 keV for J2243; 0.5 - 10 keV for J1216 and J1539; and 0.5 - 4.5 keV for J2314.
For J1234, J1539, and J2315 the column densities measured are in agreement with previous works, which also used simple absorbed power law models, within the uncertainties ( Banerji14,Martocchia17 ).

gmw_00002128.name: 1RXS J013106.4+612035
gmw_00002128.overview: 1RXS J013106.4+612035 is an extragalactic source that is considered a likely blazar candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0401518]. Observations of the object reveal a featureless continuum heavily absorbed at shorter wavelengths and a relativistic, one-sided radio jet [arXiv:astro-ph/0401518].

sources.summary: The paper presents 1RXS J013106.4+612035 as a likely extragalactic blazar candidate, citing a one-sided radio jet, featureless absorbed continuum, and an unlikely stellar interpretation.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J013106.4+612035 This source was observed to exhibit a relativistic one-sided radio jet in the EVN maps (with >0.3 ). Possible proper motion of the core at the 4 level was also reported, although Rib o et al. ( ) were very cautious about this claim. Its spectrum in Fig.  spec2 taken in service mode displays a featureless continuum heavily absorbed at shorter wavelengths. Only interstellar and atmospheric absorption features are detected.

The one-sided jet and the featureless continuum point to a possible blazar interpretation for 1RXS J013106.4+612035 . Thus, a stellar origin does not seem appropriate for this source which is most likely extragalactic.

gmw_00002129.name: 1RXS J042201.0+485610
gmw_00002129.overview: 1RXS J042201.0+485610 is an extragalactic source located at a redshift of z=0.114 [arXiv:astro-ph/0401518]. It is classified as a likely Seyfert 1 galaxy observed through the Galactic Plane, exhibiting broad hydrogen emission lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0401518].

sources.summary: The paper confirms 1RXS J042201.0+485610 as extragalactic, measuring z=0.114 from broad redshifted emission lines and interpreting it as a likely Seyfert 1 galaxy.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J042201.0+485610 The spectrum of this source in Fig.  spec3 clearly shows strong emission lines that we identify as redshifted H + [ N ii ] , [ O iii ] and H . The H + [ N ii ] blend has a broad Full Width Zero Intensity of 20 000 km s ^ 1 . Using all identified lines, the redshift is consistently estimated to be z=0.114 0.002 . An extragalactic origin is therefore confirmed.

This was already suspected in PRM02 given the Full Width Half Maximum (FWHM) 15 greater than point like sources in optical images. The broadness of the hydrogen lines suggests that this is a likely Seyfert 1 galaxy seen through the Galactic Plane.

gmw_00002130.name: 1RXS J072418.3 071508
gmw_00002130.overview: 1RXS J072418.3 071508 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar with a redshift of approximately 0.270 to 0.280 [arXiv:astro-ph/0401516, arXiv:astro-ph/0401518]. The galaxy features strong, redshifted emission lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0401518] and hosts a relativistic, one-sided parsec-scale radio jet extending from a compact core [arXiv:astro-ph/0401516, arXiv:astro-ph/0401518].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1RXS J072418.3 071508 as a z=0.270 flat-spectrum radio quasar, gives PMN/WGA aliases, and reports EVN/MERLIN imaging of its one-sided pc-scale radio jet and compact core.

quotes.quote: And finally, 1RXS J072418.3 071508 is a known quasar.

This radio source has recently been (March 2002) classified as a quasar in the SIMBAD database, and it is not any more a microquasar candidate. It is catalogued in the Parkes-MIT-NRAO (Griffith et al. ) and the Texas Survey (Douglas et al. ). It is listed as PMN J0724 0715 in the NED database, and is the source WGA J0724.3 0715 in Perlman et al. ( ), who reported a faint and quite broad H emission line (rest-frame W_ =30.3 , FWHM=4000 km s ^ 1 ), and classified it as a Flat Spectrum Radio Quasar (FSRQ) with z=0.270 .

It presents (Fig. fig:pmr10 ) a one-sided pc-scale jet oriented towards the northeast, changing from a P.A. of 50 at 5 mas from the core (EVN image) to 20 up to 200 mas, at MERLIN scales. Model fitting of the EVN visibilities with circular Gaussians reveals a compact core (0.7 mas FWHM) with 245.4 mJy, and two distinct components, one with 11.9 mJy at 6.2 mas (P.A. 46 , 1.9 mas FWHM, present in the right panel image of Fig. fig:pmr10 ) and another one with the size of the beam, a flux density of 1.7 mJy at a distance of 29.6 mas in P.A. 27 (visible in the middle panel image of Fig. fig:pmr10 ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1RXS J072418.3 071508 as a flat-spectrum radio quasar with a relativistic one-sided jet and redshifted emission lines, measuring z=0.280.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J072418.3 071508  This source was considered a candidate when we first performed VLA and optical observations at the early stages of our research. However, around this epoch the source was classified as a flat spectrum radio quasar by Perlman et al. ( ) with a redshift of z=0.270 . Our spectrum in Fig.  spec6 shows the presence of strong, redshifted emission lines of H + [ N ii ] and [ O iii ] for this relativistic one-sided radio jet source with >0.5 (Rib o et al. ). We derive a redshift estimate of z=0.280 0.001 , not very different from the previous finding.

The small discrepancy is probably due to the different signal-to-noise ratio between our spectrum and that of Perlman et al. ( ). H + [ N ii ] were noticeably stronger at the epoch of our observations.

gmw_00002131.name: GRB 090426 Host-Galaxy Complex
aliases_external: GRB 090426 host galaxy complex / northeastern host lobe
host galaxy of GRB 090426 / GRB 090426 afterglow site
gmw_00002131.overview: The GRB 090426 Host-Galaxy Complex is a bright, UV-luminous system with a complicated morphology [arXiv:0907.1661]. Located at a redshift of z=2.605, the host galaxy is a blue, star-forming galaxy that is likely interacting and merging with a nearby companion [arXiv:1101.3488, arXiv:1105.1312].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a bright, morphologically complex, UV-luminous host galaxy complex for GRB 090426, with close afterglow alignment and matching colors supporting physical association.

quotes.quote: Both optical images show a bright, extended object with complicated morphology (a bright, elongated object with fainter lobes of emission to the NE and S) near the afterglow location (Figure fig:imaging ).

To calculate the offset of the afterglow relative to the putative host galaxy, we aligned both the LRIS acquisition image (taken the night of the burst) and the Subaru V band observation to reference stars in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, giving a position of = 12^h36^m18^s.052 , = +32^ 59 09.14 (J2000). This position places the afterglow within 0.1 (800 pc in projection) of the centre of the northeastern lobe of the system that we subsequently identify as the host galaxy complex. Aperture photometry of the brightest (central) region of the host as well as the knot at the afterglow location was performed with IRAF using a 1 \ radius.

The resulting photometry, corrected for the modest Galactic extinction ( E(B-V) = 0.017 mag; sfd98 ), is presented in Table  tab:imaging . In addition to the spatial coincidence, the identical colours strongly suggest that the two objects are physically related. Interpolating to the flux at 1700  \ (see Reddy+2008 ), the photometric magnitude of the northeast component of the host corresponds to a rest-frame UV luminosity of approximately 0.7 L_ * , or 2L_ * for the entire host complex, indicating a luminous host galaxy.

sources.summary: H is identified as the real host galaxy of GRB 090426, near G1 at z=2.605, with Ly emission, 0.8 L* luminosity, and modest star formation.

quotes.quote: Using the acquisition images of the spectroscopic observations, we determined the magnitudes of the two galaxies G1 and H (the host) to be V_ G1 =24.13 0.05 and V_ H =24.93 0.08 , respectively, corresponding to absolute magnitudes of M_ AB (1500 )= --21.05 and --20.43. At the host redshift, this implies relative luminosities of 1.5 and 0.8 L_* (Reddy Steidel 2009). Fig. 090426:host shows a color image of the host galaxy complex (obtained from our acquisition images) and indicates the position of the afterglow.

In the FORS2 observations, both galaxies show clear Ly emission at redshifts of z=2.605 (H) and 2.611 (G1), indicating that the host has about the same redshift as the GRB while the companion is at a slightly higher redshift. At that redshift, the physical distance between their cores is only 9 kpc in projection, which implies that they are likely interacting and possibly in the process of merging. The Ly emission of the host galaxy gives a star formation rate of 3.1 M_ yr ^ 1 , on the lower end of what is typically observed in (long duration) GRB hosts (Savaglio et al. 2009).

sources.summary: The paper describes the host galaxy of GRB 090426 as an extended, blue star-forming galaxy whose bright N-E knot underlies the afterglow and whose optical/NIR flux was corrected in afterglow SED photometry.

quotes.quote: Levesque2010 noted that the blue star-forming host of this burst might also be consistent with a collapsar origin.

The host galaxy of GRB 090426 is an extended source (about 2 '' ) and the afterglow lies above the brightest part of this galaxy, the N-E knot Antonelli2009 . For the galaxy as well as this knot, g''i'z' magnitudes were previously published ( Antonelli2009 ), but in the NIR bands only upper limits are known ( Levesque2010 ). Therefore, for the construction of the afterglow SED, the GROND optical bands could be corrected for the contribution of the underlying host galaxy flux, but in the NIR only the maximum possible contribution of host galaxy light could be considered. Combining the GROND OBs 6 to 10 provides a good signal-to-noise ratio for the detection of the optical transient (afterglow plus underlying host galaxy) in g^ ,^ ,i^ ,z^ ,J , and H (Table tab:logGRONDNIR ). Given the upper limits to the J,H band magnitudes of the underlying host, the contribution of host galaxy flux in J and H at this time translates into an increase in brightness of the optical transient by at most 0.1 mag and 0.13 mag, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper uses the host of GRB 090426A as a known-redshift test case, finding its redshift unusually high for the conditioned galaxy population while stellar mass stays consistent.

quotes.quote: Two of the hosts, furthermore, have spectroscopic redshifts determined from their GRB afterglows: GRB 090426A ( z=2.609 ) and GRB 131004A ( z=0.717 ), but undetermined stellar masses.

Testing the \ Framework To better understand the stellar mass and redshift results for our faint short GRB hosts, we perform \ fits and \ ( M_* r_e ) setting the redshift free for the two GRBs with known redshifts, GRBs 090426A and 131004A, and compare these results to the fixed redshift results (which represents the likely ``true'' results). In Figure  fig:test_cases , we show the posterior distributions of their redshifts and stellar masses for the \ fits and \ ( M_* r_e ) fits and overplot the ``true'' results as black lines. We find that the true redshift for GRB 090426A ( z=2.609 ) lies near the 99th percentile of, but still within, the evolving galaxy population after conditioning on the observed flux and size, while the stellar mass is fairly consistent. This is an interesting result and may suggest that the host of this GRB has other unusual properties compared to the normal galaxy population.

gmw_00002132.name: SDSS J075234.33+292049.8
gmw_00002132.overview: SDSS J075234.33+292049.8 is a distant polar-ring galaxy [arXiv:0910.0589] with an inclined polar component [arXiv:1211.1307]. Its outer ring is significantly bluer than the central body [arXiv:0910.0589], and the system is modeled with an oblate dark matter halo [arXiv:1211.1307].

sources.summary: The paper studies SDSS J075234.33+292049.8/RG1 as a distant ring galaxy, favoring a rare polar-ring interpretation over a barred spiral after photometric and kinematic analysis.

quotes.quote: This paper presents an interpretation of existing observational data along with new observations of the object SDSS J075234.33+292049.8 (referred to as RG1) at z=0.060149, identified as a ring galaxy by EO during an investigation of large-separation gravitationally lensed AGNs (Ofek 2001, 2002). This object is significantly more distant than most ring galaxies published and studied so far, with the exception of those in Reshetnikov (1997), Lavery et al. (2004), and Reshetnikov Dettmar (2007).
