Each of the employed models did provide decent fits on statistical grounds, yet significant residuals in the and hard X-ray bands (especially above 10 ) were systematically present. In fact, in attempting to account for the steep and smooth soft excess, parameters such as black hole spin, inner-disc emissivity index and/or source height were forced to extreme values, which, instead, are not required, nor compatible, with the shape and strength of the feature and Compton hump.

sources.summary: The paper models Ton S 180 as a super-Eddington Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 whose broadband spectrum is fitted by an inner slim disk, outer self-gravitating disk, and dusty torus.

quotes.quote: Next, we demonstrate that the Mid-Infrared to X-ray spectrum of a bright, well-studied Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 galaxy, Ton S 180, is indeed well fitted by the spectrum arising from the following three components: an inner slim disc (with a corona), an outer, self-gravitating non-Keplerian disc and a dusty torus. The total mass, BH mass plus the entire disc mass, is found to be about (1.4 - 8.0) . If the surface density varies with radius in proportion to ^ 0.6 , the total mass is consistent with the central mass estimated by H and [ O III ] widths.

Ton S 180 has low Galactic and intrinsic extinction (Turner et al. 2002). Since Ton S 180 is a nearby object (z=0.06), we have not taken any spectral shifts or K-corrections into account. The BH mass estimated from the H width and optical luminosity (Wandel, Peterson Malkan 1999; Kaspi 2000) is 10^ 7.1 .
We demonstrate that a broadband spectrum of one NLS1, Ton S 180, is indeed well fitted by a summation of an inner, non self-gravitating disc, an outer, self-gravitating disc and a dusty torus.

sources.summary: Ton S180 is presented as a high-accretion-rate AGN with hints of a double-Lorentzian PSD and lags consistent with a very-high-state interpretation.

quotes.quote: Ton S180 Ton S180 In our recent PSD survey of 32 well observed AGN (Summons et al in preparation) the only AGN besides Akn 564 for which there are reasonable hints that a double-Lorentzian, rather than a single bending powerlaw model, is required is Ton S180 (Fig fig:tons_psd ). Interestingly Ton S180 has, like Akn 564, an accretion rate close to the Eddington limit and so may very well be another VHS system. As with Ark 564, we have calculated the lag spectrum but the data are not as good as for Ark 564. The lags are, however, consistent with a VHS interpretation, as in Ark 564.

Ton S180, another very high accretion rate AGN, has a less well measured PSD which is also similar to that of a VHS BHB. As yet we have no firm evidence, from PSD shapes, for a hard-state AGN, but this result is probably just due to two selection effects.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ton S180 as an NLS1 with a weak but higher-column warm absorber than Arakelian 564, narrow absorption lines, and C VI/O VIII/Ne X emission.

quotes.quote: Ton S180 has a weak warm absorber, though with a larger column than that identified in Arakelian 564. rozanska04 identify a number of narrow absorption lines with a minimum column density of 5.1 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 . There is also emission from C VI, O VIII and Ne X. Unfortunately, due to the weakness of these features, very little information can be determined in order to make a comparison with Arakelian 564. By comparing Arakelian 564 with other NLS1s we can see that it is an unusual object, but at least two other galaxies, Markarian 359 and Ton S180, may have similar warm absorber properties.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ton S180 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: Ton S180 or Tonantzinta S180 is a local (z=0.062) narrow line Seyfert 1 AGN which is considered one of the prototypes `bare' AGN with no trace of absorption and a featureless and prominent soft-excess Vaughan2002 . From Suzaku observations, Takahashi2010 also suggested the presence of an intriguing hard excess in the 15--55 keV range for this source. The X-ray spectrum of this source contains a strong soft-excess Vaughan2002 , a broad Fe K emission line and hard X-ray emission up to 30 keV Nardini2012 . From the XMM-Newton observation, Parker2018 suggested that the X-ray spectrum favoured two Comptonization components with a reflection component from a disc around a black hole of low spin. Turner2002 estimated the mass of the central black hole as 2 10^7 M _ .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Ton S180's 12-day X-ray variability, finding large soft and hard-band flux changes, a correlated variable soft hump, and broad plus narrow Fe K emission.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of the X-ray variability properties of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy PHL 912 Ton S180 , based upon a 12-day continuous observation with . Examination of the light curves reveals flux variations of a factor of 3.5 in the 0.7--1.3 keV band and 3.9 in the 2--10 keV band. Time-resolved spectroscopy, using approximately daily sampling, reveals that the broad ``soft hump'' component at energies <2 keV shows flux variations on timescales as short as 1 day that are well correlated with the photon index and the 2--10 keV band flux. A broad Fe K emission is detected. There is also a statistically significant evidence for a narrow Fe K line at 6.8 keV, indicating an origin in ionized material.

sources.summary: The paper describes TON S 180 as having detected Fe 13 but generally weak coronal-line emission, preventing detailed CLR kinematic analysis.

quotes.quote: [ Fe 13 ] 1.074 m is observed unambiguously in 1H 1934-063 (Fig. hcor ), ARK 564 (Fig. arkcor ), MRK 335 (Fig. 335cor ) and TON S 180.

MRK 1044 and TON S 180 were not included because they lack significant coronal line emission. Also listed in Table fwhm are the FWHM and shifts measured for the optical coronal lines [ Fe 7 ] , [ Fe 10 ] and [ Fe 11 ] measured in 1H 1934-063, ARK 564 and MRK 335. Correlations between line width, ionization potential and critical density Several interesting results arise from the data listed in Table fwhm . In 1H 1934-063, ARK 564 and MRK 335, there is a clear correlation between line width and IP, as can be graphically seen in Figure ipvsfwhm .

This might be the case for MRK 1044 and TON S 180, which present a very weak CLR spectra. However, the hydrogen column density N_ H of these objects, measured from X-ray observations, show little or no excess of neutral H over the Galactic value as is observed in columns 6 and 7 of Table tab1 ). Another possibility is that coronal line emitting material in the line of sight is absent, or there is a high anisotropy in the continuum radiation that reaches the NLR. Interestingly, low to intermediate forbidden lines in the optical region for the above two sources are also very weak or absent com98 , contrary to what is seen in the remaining objects.

sources.summary: The paper uses Ton S180 as a Narrow-line Seyfert 1 example, comparing its broad-band luminosity, starlight-free H flux, and X-ray slope with other high-accretion AGN.

quotes.quote: As examples, we include in the same plot the broad-band spectrum of quasar PG1211+143 (Janiuk et al. 2001) and two Narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies - Ton S180 ( et al. 2003) and Mrk 359 (Mathur et al. 2001; O'Brien et al. 2001). The bolometric luminosity corresponding to those spectra is 1.0 10^ 46 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , 9.0 10^ 45 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , 2.5 10^ 45 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and 9.7 10^ 43 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , assuming a Hubble constant equal to 50 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 . The optical flux for Mrk 359 has been corrected for starlight contribution (factor 2.5) on the basis of the ratio of the observed value of the H_ flux in the starlight-free source Ton S180 (46 ) and Mrk 359 (18 ) (V eron-Cetty et al. 2001). The sources covering two orders of magnitude in bolometric luminosity, and therefore possibly two orders of magnitude in mass, show nevertheless a surprisingly similar broad-band shape. The description of the X-ray spectra of Ton S180 and Mrk 359 may seem different (the X-ray photon index of Ton S180 is given as 2.44, Turner et al. 2001 and of Mrk 359 as 1.85, O'Brien et al. 2001).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Ton S180's X-ray spectrum, finding a steep hard power law, a cutoff soft excess, variability, and similarities to very-high-state black hole binaries.

quotes.quote: An analysis was made of 0.3--15 keV X-ray spectra of a Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 Galaxy, Ton S180, using archival data from ASCA, RXTE, and XMM-Newton. At energies above 2.5 keV, a power-law with a photon index of 2.3 successfully and consistently reproduced the spectra from all of these observatories. Assuming this power-law component to extend toward lower energies, a soft excess, which is one of the most remarkable features of Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 Galaxies, is explained by another power-law multiplied by a thermal cutoff at 0.4 keV. Some similarities have been observed between this object and Galactic black hole binaries in very high state, the latter being realized under high accretion rates. Attempts have been made to interpret the soft excess in terms of Comptonization of the disk photons by an electron cloud surrounding the accretion disk, like BHBs in a very high state.

sources.summary: The paper finds Ton S180 has ambiguous 1.2 keV absorption or emission, possible O VII absorption, and acceptable broad-emission or thermal-plasma fits to its ASCA spectrum.

quotes.quote: It is difficult to differentiate between absorption and emission at 1.2 keV in Ton S180. A single absorption edge at a rest energy of E =1.09 0.03 keV with an optical depth =0.17 0.03 , yields ^ 2 / =1072/986 . A second edge in the spectrum (at E =1.42 0.06 keV with =0.13 0.03 ) improves the fit further ( ^ 2 / =1059/985 ). There is some evidence for an additional edge at 0.73 keV ( ^ 2 / =1048/983 ) presumably corresponding to O vii . However SIS-1 seems to underestimate the flux compared to SIS-0 below 0.8 keV, so any features in the spectrum below 0.8 keV need to considered with caution.

sources.summary: The paper reviews XMM-Newton and Suzaku spectra of Ton S180, finding stable broad-band X-ray emission and fitting its strong soft excess with a dual-reflector accretion-disc model.

quotes.quote: In this context, we review from a reflection perspective the three highest-quality X-ray observations of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy Tonantzintla S180, obtained by XMM-Newton and Suzaku . The X-ray spectrum of Ton S180 shows only moderate variations over a time span of several years, suggesting that the same physical process accounts for the bulk of the broad-band X-ray emission at the different epochs, and that the properties of the X-ray source are fairly stable. We have successfully applied in our spectral analysis a dual-reflector model, consisting of two separate components: one arises from the surface of the accretion disc, is highly ionized and blurred by relativistic effects; the other is cold, quite faint, and can be associated with a distant reprocessor. Due to the strength and the nearly power-law shape of its soft excess emission, Ton S180 is one of the most challenging sources to test the X-ray reflection scenario.

sources.summary: The paper uses Ton S180 as a soft-excess test case, noting state-dependent soft X-ray variability and arguing against retrograde orbiting objects while allowing IMBH accretion in low states.

quotes.quote: For example, the narrow line Seyfert galaxy Ton S180 cv06 in a low state reveals a substantially stronger fractional variability in the softer X-rays than in hard X-rays b33 . In high states, the fractional variability of soft X-rays in Ton S180 appears to be similar to that of hard X-rays. In this AGN, we can rule out objects on retrograde orbits as a cause of the soft X-ray excess. If the soft excess is due to IMBH accretion, it evidently dominates emission during the low state while continuum emission dominates during the high state.

sources.summary: The paper presents an X-ray observation of Tonantzintla S180, describing it as an X-ray-bright ultrasoft Seyfert/quasar with a strong soft excess and smooth, featureless high-resolution continuum.

quotes.quote: Here we report the results of a 30 ksec \ observation of Tonantzintla S180 ( z=0.062 ) obtained as part of a guaranteed time programme (PI: Th. Boller) to study the timing and spectral properties of ultrasoft NLS1s using . \ is one of the X-ray brightest ultrasoft Seyferts (Vaughan \ 1999; Leighly 1999a) and its luminosity is such that it is often classified as a quasar. Previous X-ray observations with \ (Turner, George Nandra 1998; Vaughan \ 1999; Leighly 1999b; Ballantyne, Iwasawa Fabian 2001) and \ (Comastri \ 1998) showed a strong excess of soft emission and tentative evidence for an ionised iron line. The more recent high-resolution \ LETGS spectrum (Turner \ 2001b) showed no strong, narrow absorption or emission features and showed the soft X-ray continuum is smooth and featureless.

sources.summary: The paper reports an exceptionally long EUVE light curve for Ton S180, evidence for a 2.08-day periodic signal, and notes its narrow-line Seyfert 1 classification.

quotes.quote: Halpern, Leighly Marshall (2003) present an atlas of EUVE light curves from Seyfert galaxies. Three objects had exceptionally long light curves: 33 days for Ton S180, 26 days for 1H 0419 577, and 20 days for RX J0437.1 4711. Periodicity analysis as outlined above revealed evidence for a 2.08-day signal in Ton S180 with global confidence level of 98 , a 0.908-day signal in RX J0437.4 4711, and a 5.8-day signal in 1H 0419 577 with a global confidence level of only 64 .
Ton S180 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, 1H 0419 577 is a Seyfert 1.5, and both NGC 4395 and RX J0437.1 4711 are both classified as ordinary Seyfert 1 galaxies.
If that were the case, and if R=3R_S for Ton S180, we infer that R=23R_S for NGC 4395, and R=95R_S for Cyg X-1.

sources.summary: The paper fits Suzaku spectra of Ton S180 as a Seyfert galaxy hosting a near-extremal supermassive black hole and finds its measurements remain consistent with Kerr spacetime.

quotes.quote: We apply our model to fit Suzaku data of four Seyfert galaxies (Ton S180, Ark 120, 1H0419--577, and Swift J0501.9--3239). These galaxies host at the center supermassive black holes that were previously interpreted as near-extremal Kerr black holes. For Ton S180 and 1H0419--577, our measurements are still consistent with the Kerr hypothesis.

sources.summary: The paper compares Ton S180 with 1H 0707 495, describing its shallower soft excess, nearly identical hard and soft light curves, and correlated variability suggesting non-distinct emission components.

quotes.quote: In contrast, the soft excess in Ton S180 is shallower and it requires two blackbodies to model it successfully; the spectrum was modelled successfully using two Comptonized plasmas in Ref. rf:8 .
In Ton S180, the hard and soft light curves are nearly identical.

In Ton S180, although the spectrum is parameterized in terms of a soft excess and a power law, the correlated variability suggests that these components are not distinct; perhaps Comptonization is occurring in gas with clumps having a range of optical depths and temperatures.

gmw_00002170.overview: 1624+37 is a high-redshift, radio-loud broad absorption line quasar [arXiv:0711.0862, arXiv:0907.2251]. It features a compact, GHz-peaked radio spectrum [arXiv:0711.0862, arXiv:0907.2251], a one-sided jet [arXiv:0907.2251], and highly polarized emission with an extreme rest-frame rotation measure [arXiv:0711.0862, arXiv:1203.4509].

sources.summary: The paper presents this high-redshift BAL quasar as a resolved mas-scale, highly polarized, GHz-peaked radio source whose emission is confined to a very compact region.

quotes.quote: J162453.4+375806 A high-redshift ( z=3.38 ) quasar with a resolved mas-scale structure in the NE-SW direction (Fig. images s). Benn et al. ( ) describe this source as the most radio-luminous broad absorption line (BAL) quasar known, with unusual radio and optical properties. Its radio emission is strongly polarised (11 at 10 GHz) and the rotation measure (18350 rad m ^ 2 ) is among the highest values known for any extragalactic source. The GHz-peaked radio spectrum of the source suggests a young age.

Based on their 22-GHz VLA observations, Benn et al. ( ) give 0 4 0 1 for the angular size of J162453.4+375806. On the other hand, the spectral turnover frequency ( 2 GHz in the rest frame) indicates 0.1-1 kpc linear size. The integrated flux density in our VLBI components (23.6 mJy) is practically equal to the GB6 value (22 mJy) and the total flux density measured by Benn et al. ( ) (23.3 mJy). Thus we can conclude that the entire radio emission comes from a region as small as 10 mas, corresponding to a projected linear size of less than 0.1 kpc (assuming a flat cosmological model with _ m =0.3 , _ =0.7 and H_ 0 =70 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 ; this model is used throughout the paper).

sources.summary: The paper describes J162453.47+375806.6 as an unusual BAL radio-loud quasar with extreme C IV BAL velocities, a mini-BAL, a GHz-peaked spectrum, and a one-sided jet.

quotes.quote: J162453.47+375806.6 is described in detail by Benn et al. (2005), and we use their value of BI=2990 km s ^ 1 and estimate V_ max =28300 km s ^ 1 rather than taking measurements from Gibson et al. (2009) (for which BAL absorption was integrated to 25000 km s ^ 1 ). The large minimum ( V_ min =20560 km s ^ 1 ) and maximum velocities of the C IV BAL in this source are unusual for BAL RLQs and unique within our snapshot and archival samples. There is also low-velocity absorption, described by Benn et al. (2005) as a mini-BAL (defined as total velocity range <2000 km s ^ 1 ; the mini-BAL is shaded gray along with the primary BAL in Figure 6 for identification). The radio spectrum is GHz-peaked (steep at high frequencies) and milliarcsecond imaging reveals a one-sided jet (Benn et al. 2005; Montenegro-Montes et al. 2008, 2009).

sources.summary: The paper highlights 1624+37 as a BAL QSO with confirmed compact GPS-scale structure, high fractional polarization, and an extreme rest-frame rotation measure.

quotes.quote: In particular, this classification is confirmed for 1624+37, with a size of 60 pc at 5 GHz and 75 pc at 8 GHz (Montenegro-Montes et al. 2008b, 2012 in preparation).

For the remaining source, 1624+37, our data only provide a high upper limit for m , but the source shows a high fractional polarisation from the data reported in Benn et al. (2005, their Table 2), with m=6 at 4.8 GHz and m=11 at 10 and 22 GHz.

The RM listed in Table pol1 for 1624+37 was taken from Benn et al. (2005). With a rest-frame RM of 18350 570 rad m ^ 2 , this was and still is the second-highest RM known, after that of quasar OQ172 (Kato et al. 1987; O'Dea 1998), with RM = 22400 rad m ^ 2 .

gmw_00002171.overview: The source 1603+30 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:0808.2105] that exhibits intrinsic radio variability [arXiv:1203.4509]. It displays a symmetric parsec-scale structure typical of a young gigahertz-peaked spectrum source [arXiv:1304.3021], while its misaligned arcsecond-scale structure suggests a possible merger or jet precession [arXiv:1304.3021].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1603+30 as a symmetric parsec-scale, young GPS-like source with hot-spot-dominated emission and misaligned arcsecond-scale structure, possibly from merger or jet precession.

quotes.quote: 1603+30 In this case a rather symmetric structure can be seen on the parsec scale. The south-western component can be fitted with two (A and B) Gaussians. The separation between A and C (at the north-eastern edge) components is 8.6 mas, with a projected linear size of 74 pc. This source presents a convex radio spectrum, with a peak frequency at 1.4 GHz, and thus could be classified as a young GPS source, in which the emission is dominated by the hot spots. On the arcsecond scale, a VLA map at 22 GHz shows a resolved structure in which some additional emission is present at about 2 arcsec (17 kpc) to the south.

Such a linear size is more typical of CSS sources (1-20 kpc), although the overall spectrum is typical of GPS sources. The structure seen with the EVN is not aligned with the one seen with the VLA: probably a merger or a jet precession could justify the different morphologies of the same object on different scales.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1603+3002 as the most luminous source in the BAL-quasar sample, with steep radio spectrum, high EVN/MERLIN flux, and low fractional polarization.

quotes.quote: J1603+3002 is a HiBAL quasar. It is the most luminous source in these observations. It has a radio loudness logR=2.04 (Becker et al. 2000). The flux densities at 20 cm and 3.6 cm detected by the VLA in A-configuration are 54.2 mJy and 18.1 mJy respectively, which suggests a spectral index of = -0.6 . The total flux density is about 52.1 mJy in the EVN image (see Fig.3()).

sources.summary: The paper describes 1603+30 as a resolved BAL QSO with core-lobe radio morphology, significant intrinsic variability, and a borderline flat-to-steep spectral index.

quotes.quote: 1603+30 is resolved at 22 GHz, showing a core component (C) coincident with the optical position of the QSO and another component towards the south (A), which could be interpreted as a lobe. The angular size is 2 arcsec, corresponding to 17 kpc. The total spectral index of the source is _ 4.86 ^ 8.46 =-0.45 0.13 .

For 1603+30 we could compare 2.6 and 4.8-GHz Effelsberg data as well as 8.4 and 22-GHz VLA data. We found _ var =4.9 and fractional variability 49 for the 4.8-GHz Effelsberg data, over an interval of 4.5 years, indicating significant intrinsic variability.

gmw_00002172.overview: The object 1005+48 is a non-BAL quasar [arXiv:1203.4509] that exhibits modest intrinsic variability [arXiv:1203.4509] and high fractional polarization [arXiv:1203.4509]. High-frequency observations resolve the source into a core-jet structure [arXiv:1505.03394], with missing flux indicating the presence of extended emission [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: 1005+4805 resolves from point-like at 1.7 GHz into a complex core-jet structure at higher frequencies, with steep spectrum and missing flux indicating extended emission.

quotes.quote: 1005+4805 . A point-like object on the 1.7-GHz image has been resolved into a more complex structure in the 5- and 8.4-GHz maps. The brightest component C1 is probably a radio core and the steep spectrum components C2 and S are parts of the one-sided radio jet. The overall spectrum of 1005+4805 is steep and does not present a peak in the gigahertz range. The large fraction of the total flux density, 85 at 1.7 GHz and 49 at 5 GHz, is lost in our observations implying the existence of extended emission not sampled by EVN/VLBA spacings. Structure and spectral indices of this object suggest core-jet morphology.

sources.summary: The paper classifies 1005+48 as a non-BAL comparison quasar with modest intrinsic variability, a steep spectral index, and high fractional polarization across several frequencies.

quotes.quote: 1005+48 shows modest variability, just at the considered threshold.
1005+48 shows modest variability (just at the adopted thresholds of significance and fractional variability) and its spectral index is _ 4.8 ^ 8.4 = -0.67 .
In particular 1005+48 shows fractional polarisation above 10 at a wide range of frequencies, from 2.6 to 22 GHz.

gmw_00002173.overview: The object 1521+43 is a non-BAL comparison quasar [arXiv:1203.4509]. It is classified as a variable source that exhibits strong radio flux-density variations and possesses one of the flattest radio spectra in its sample [arXiv:1203.4509].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1521+43 as a non-BAL comparison quasar with strong flux-density variability and one of the flattest radio spectra in the sample.

quotes.quote: 0029 09 and 1521+43 show both a high variability significance, _ Var > 10 , and a high fractional variability, 40-50 per cent, with 1521+43 being the most extreme case, showing large variations at the two frequencies.

Summarizing the results from the comparison between VLA and Effelsberg data, we found three sources with likely intrinsic variability, 0029 09, 1005+48 and 1521+43, all of them in the comparison sample.

The QSO sample from our work includes three sources classified as variable: the unresolved non-BALs 0029 09, 1005+48 and 1521+43 and the resolved BAL QSO 1603+30. 0029 09 and 1521+43 are the flatter sources in the total QSO sample, both with _ 4.8 ^ 8.4 > 0.5 .

gmw_00002174.name: PGC 1127938
gmw_00002174.overview: PGC 1127938 is a dwarf galaxy that lacks a clear bulge component [arXiv:1902.04077]. It has been identified as a candidate host for a hard X-ray selected tidal disruption event and shows no emission lines in its spectrum [arXiv:1902.04077].

sources.summary: The paper finds PGC 1127938 has narrow absorption, very low velocity dispersion, faint hard-X-ray TDE-host properties, no emission lines, and a small inferred black hole.

quotes.quote: Furthermore for three sources, PGC 1127938, PGC 1185375 and UGC 1791, the templates provide a poor fit to the overall galaxy spectrum but it is clear that the absorption lines are narrow. For these galaxies we indeed measure very low velocity dispersion values ( 40 km s ^ 1 ). Although the systematic errors may be larger than the statistical errors quoted in Table tab:results , we do not exclude these measurements as there is additional evidence that the black holes inhabiting these galaxies must be small ( 10 ^6 M _ , see Section sec:comparison ).

In particular there are three hard X-ray selected TDE candidate hosts, UGC 1791, PGC 1127938 and PGC 1185375, that have absolute magnitudes around M _ g = -16 or fainter and are morphologically very similar to each other, lacking a clear bulge component. The latter two sources show no emission lines, whereas UGC 1791 is classified as a SF galaxy. Because the M - relation is increasingly uncertain at the low velocity dispersions measured for these galaxies, we provide an alternative estimate using the relation between total stellar mass and black hole mass Reines2015 . This results in M _ BH in the range 10 ^ 5.5 0.47 M _ for these galaxies.

gmw_00002175.name: PGC 1185375
gmw_00002175.overview: PGC 1185375 is a faint dwarf galaxy that lacks a clear bulge component [arXiv:1902.04077]. It has been identified as the host of a hard X-ray selected tidal disruption event candidate and shows no emission lines in its spectrum [arXiv:1902.04077].

sources.summary: The paper treats PGC 1185375 as a faint hard-X-ray TDE host with narrow absorption, very low velocity dispersion, no emission lines, and a small inferred black hole.

quotes.quote: Furthermore for three sources, PGC 1127938, PGC 1185375 and UGC 1791, the templates provide a poor fit to the overall galaxy spectrum but it is clear that the absorption lines are narrow. For these galaxies we indeed measure very low velocity dispersion values ( 40 km s ^ 1 ). Although the systematic errors may be larger than the statistical errors quoted in Table tab:results , we do not exclude these measurements as there is additional evidence that the black holes inhabiting these galaxies must be small ( 10 ^6 M _ , see Section sec:comparison ).

In particular there are three hard X-ray selected TDE candidate hosts, UGC 1791, PGC 1127938 and PGC 1185375, that have absolute magnitudes around M _ g = -16 or fainter and are morphologically very similar to each other, lacking a clear bulge component. The latter two sources show no emission lines, whereas UGC 1791 is classified as a SF galaxy. Because the M - relation is increasingly uncertain at the low velocity dispersions measured for these galaxies, we provide an alternative estimate using the relation between total stellar mass and black hole mass Reines2015 . This results in M _ BH in the range 10 ^ 5.5 0.47 M _ for these galaxies.

gmw_00002176.name: UGC 1791
gmw_00002176.overview: UGC 1791 is a dwarf galaxy that lacks a clear bulge component [arXiv:1902.04077]. It is classified as a star-forming galaxy and has been identified as a host candidate for a hard X-ray tidal disruption event [arXiv:1902.04077].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 1791 as a faint hard-X-ray TDE host with narrow absorption, low velocity dispersion, star-forming classification, weak bulge, and small inferred black hole.

quotes.quote: Furthermore for three sources, PGC 1127938, PGC 1185375 and UGC 1791, the templates provide a poor fit to the overall galaxy spectrum but it is clear that the absorption lines are narrow. For these galaxies we indeed measure very low velocity dispersion values ( 40 km s ^ 1 ). Although the systematic errors may be larger than the statistical errors quoted in Table tab:results , we do not exclude these measurements as there is additional evidence that the black holes inhabiting these galaxies must be small ( 10 ^6 M _ , see Section sec:comparison ).

In particular there are three hard X-ray selected TDE candidate hosts, UGC 1791, PGC 1127938 and PGC 1185375, that have absolute magnitudes around M _ g = -16 or fainter and are morphologically very similar to each other, lacking a clear bulge component. The latter two sources show no emission lines, whereas UGC 1791 is classified as a SF galaxy. Because the M - relation is increasingly uncertain at the low velocity dispersions measured for these galaxies, we provide an alternative estimate using the relation between total stellar mass and black hole mass Reines2015 . This results in M _ BH in the range 10 ^ 5.5 0.47 M _ for these galaxies.

gmw_00002177.name: LEDA 095953
gmw_00002177.overview: LEDA 095953 is a quiescent, moderately Balmer-strong post-starburst galaxy [arXiv:1902.04077]. It has been identified as the host galaxy of a soft-X-ray tidal disruption event [arXiv:1902.04077].

sources.summary: The paper classifies LEDA 095953 as a soft-X-ray TDE host satisfying quiescent moderately Balmer-strong criteria from its H_A and H equivalent-width measurements.

quotes.quote: For the host galaxy LEDA 095953, we measure H _ A EW = 1.8 \ and H EW = 1.9 \ in absorption. We estimate that the uncertainties on these measurements are 0.5 . To obtain a lower limit on the H EW, we correct the measurement by 2.5 \ to estimate the EW corrected for stellar absorption. This is motivated by the finding of French2016 that the largest stellar absorption correction to the H EW for sources in their sample is 2.5 . This yields H EW -0.4 \ for LEDA 095953.

gmw_00002178.name: host of DES14C1kia
gmw_00002178.overview: The host of DES14C1kia is a galaxy that lacks narrow Balmer emission lines and forbidden narrow emission lines, indicating no ongoing star formation or nuclear black hole activity [arXiv:1902.04077]. It is a candidate member of the moderately quiescent Balmer-strong host galaxy population [arXiv:1902.04077].

sources.summary: The paper treats the host of DES14C1kia as a possible moderately Balmer-strong TDE host, with H-alpha obscured by atmospheric absorption and no narrow emission lines detected.

quotes.quote: In addition, we measure EW(H _ A ) = 3.5 \ for the host of DES14C1kia. Unfortunately, the H line is redshifted into the strong atmospheric absorption band near 7800 , making it difficult to gauge whether there is absorption or emission in the H line. We do not identify any narrow Balmer emission lines in the spectrum of DES14C1kia, nor any forbidden narrow emission lines, such as the Si or N doublets, nor O iii at 5007 , that would indicate ongoing star formation or nuclear black hole activity. This host galaxy could therefore be an additional member of the (m)QBS host galaxy population, although the lack of significant H emission would require a spectrum that does not suffer from atmospheric absorption bands to confirm.

gmw_00002179.name: host of iPTF--16axa
gmw_00002179.overview: The host of iPTF--16axa is tentatively classified as a moderately Balmer-strong galaxy [arXiv:1902.04077]. This classification is based on a central extraction of the host spectrum, which reveals stronger H_A absorption than whole-galaxy measurements [arXiv:1902.04077].

sources.summary: The paper remeasures the central host spectrum of iPTF--16axa, finding stronger H_A absorption and tentatively classifying the host as moderately Balmer-strong.

quotes.quote: Additionally, while remeasuring the Lick index for the source iPTF--16axa, we find a value of EW(H _ A ) = 4.1 , inconsistent with the measurement of Graur2018 . Our measured value would result in a classification as mQBS rather than quiescent, even when adopting the same measurement uncertainty of 1.5 . This difference can potentially be attributed to the fact that in Graur2018 , an extraction of the whole galaxy light along the slit was used to allow for a fair comparison to the wide fiber measurements from SDSS, whereas we repeated the measurement using the central extraction. Gradients in the H _ A EW as a function of distance to the nucleus have been observed in other post starburst galaxies Pracy2012 . We therefore tentatively classify the host of iPTF--16axa as an mQBS galaxy.

gmw_00002180.name: NGC 1560
gmw_00002180.overview: NGC 1560 is an isolated [arXiv:0706.3703], low surface brightness [arXiv:0901.4935, arXiv:0906.0668] dwarf galaxy [arXiv:0901.4935, arXiv:astro-ph/9704274] classified as a spiral [arXiv:0906.0668] or irregular galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0201372]. It features a gas-dominated disk [arXiv:0906.0668, arXiv:0706.3703] with a stellar population estimated to be approximately 10 billion years old [arXiv:astro-ph/0201372].

sources.summary: The paper uses dwarf LSB galaxy NGC 1560 as a MOND test case, showing that scaling hidden molecular gas changes the critical acceleration while preserving the observed rotation-curve shape up to moderate gas factors.

quotes.quote: For example, we examine the dwarf LSB galaxy NGC 1560, dominated by dark matter in the Newtonian frame. Its rotation curve reaches a plateau at  7 kpc. In accordance with MOND, this maximum velocity can be written: v_c^4=GMa_0. The mass M, to a first approximation, corresponds to the mass of the atomic gas (HI+He) M_ at , since at this outer radius, the stellar content is negligible.

It works correctely for the galaxy NGC1560, as shown in the Fig. fig:CR_H2 . The most remarkable is the shape of the rotation curve which is conserved by modifying the values (,a_0) , not just the maximum velocity. The wiggle at 5 kpc is well reproduced until =5 . Beyond this value, the oscillation is more and more pronounced and no longer matches the data.

sources.summary: The paper averages three distances for NGC 1560, adopts Swaters and Balcells R-band photometry, uses Broeils and H-alpha rotation data, and warns that edge-on geometry may compromise HI measurements.

quotes.quote: NGC 1560 The distance to this galaxy is taken as the average of three values. At first the Hubble distance of 3.25 Mpc. Secondly a distance of 2.5 0.1 Mpc (Lee Madore 1993) based on bright stars and Tully-Fisher relation, and thirdly Krismer et al. (1995) give a distance of 3.5 0.7 Mpc based on the TF relation. The average of the three amounts to 3.1 Mpc.

Broeils (1992b) presents photometry in the B-band and Swaters Balcells (2002) in the R-band. The profile of the latter authors declines slightly steeper and was used to represent the mass distribution of the disc. Total R-band luminosity also from Swaters. For radii beyond 120 \ the rotation curve of Broeils (1992b) is adopted. Because this galaxy is rather edge-on ( i 80 to 82 ) the available may be compromised by beam smearing and integration effects. Therefore long slit H data of de Blok Bosma (2002, which have kindly been made available by these authors) have been converted to rotational velocities.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 1560 as a low-density simple disc whose exponential surface density and baryonic distribution reproduce a MOND-like mean rotation curve without dark matter.

quotes.quote: NGC 1560 The rotation curve of the low-density galaxy NGC 1560 has already been analysed in the framework of the model (Pascoli, 2022). However, this analysis was very succinct, starting from the Newtonian curve supplied by Famaey and McGaugh (Famaey and McGaugh, 2012). Here, we re-examine the question to produce a mean rotation curve without the fluctuations but in a more self-consistent way. The surface density is displayed in Fig. 1.

Thus, for NGC 1560, p_c=1.78. We emphasize that no extraneous parameters are used in the fitting process. Fig. 2 shows that the model produces a mean velocity curve very similar to that of MOND. This result strongly suggests that for NGC 1560, the baryonic distribution alone is sufficient to accurately predict the observed velocity curve, removing the need for DM.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 1560 as a high-resolution LSB dwarf test case for beam smearing and disk-halo decompositions, finding low-resolution halo parameters comparable to high-resolution values.

quotes.quote: We have selected NGC 1560 for this purpose. This galaxy is a LSB dwarf galaxy ( _0(B) = 23.2 \ mag \ arcsec ^ 2 , for other properties see Table 1), for which a high-resolution rotation curve is available (Broeils 1992). This curve is shown in the left panel of Fig. smoreg1560 . The beam size for this WSRT observation was 13'' 14'' , and the extent of the rotation curve is 38 beams. Combined with the small distance of only 3 Mpc, we can be that sure beam-smearing is of no importance in this observation.

We test this by again looking at the case of NGC 1560 (Figure smoreg1560 ). Even the high-resolution decompositions of this galaxy are not entirely unambiguous, showing that even in a maximum disk decomposition it is difficult to entirely constrain the (M/L)_ value. The low-resolution halo parameters are given Table 6, and are comparable with the high-resolution values.

sources.summary: The paper presents higher-resolution GMRT H I observations of NGC 1560, confirms its rotation-curve wiggle, and finds MOND reproduces the wiggle better than dark-halo fits.

quotes.quote: We present HI observations performed at the GMRT of the nearby dwarf galaxy NGC 1560. This Sd galaxy is well-known for a distinct ``wiggle'' in its rotation curve. Our new observations have twice the resolution of the previously published HI data. We derived the rotation curve by taking projection effects into account, and we verified the derived kinematics by creating model datacubes. This new rotation curve is similar to the previously published one: we confirm the presence of a clear wiggle.

The main differences are in the innermost 100 of the rotation curve, where we find slightly ( 5 km s ^ 1 ) higher velocities. Mass modelling of the rotation curve results in good fits using the core-dominated Burkert halo (which however does not reproduce the wiggle), bad fits using the a Navarro, Frenk White halo, and good fits using MOND (Modified Newtonian Dynamics), which also reproduces the wiggle.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 1560 as the low-luminosity example where Newtonian predictions are poor but both MOND and the density-based model fit the observed rotation curve.

quotes.quote: For this, we choose two examples, a high luminosity galaxy (New General Catalogue of Nebulae and Clusters of Stars (NGC) 6946) and a low luminosity galaxy (NGC 1560). These two examples are taken from the review of Famaey and McGaugh (2012, see the references therein for the original data sources).

With _ =60 \ M_ \ pc ^ 2 (from Famaey, and McGaugh, 2012, figure 19; Moni Bidin et al, 2012, figure 5), we find that _E _0 ^ 0.5 =0.36 for NGC 6946 and 1.24 for NGC 1560. We, however, note that a better fit of the v curves is realized by taking _E _0 ^ 0.5 =0.46 (instead of 0.36 ) for NGC 6946 and 1.30 (instead of 1.24 ) for NGC 1560.

The galaxy NGC 1560 illustrates this situation, whereas both MOND and the model are fairly consistent with the observed curve (figure 10). Figure 10 NGC 1560: the observed rotation curve (black circles) is displayed together with the theoretical curves predicted by the baryonic mass model (black line), MOND (red line) and model (blue line) The results from both MOND and the model are superimposed well between 3>>6 \ kpc , even though outside this interval, MOND seems slightly better.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 1560 as a resolved edge-on dwarf disk and finds the sample's thick-disk stellar populations have weak color gradients and consistently red giant branches.

quotes.quote: In the present paper we analyze NGC 784, 1560, UGC 1281, and UGCA 442. They span a range of rotation velocities from 95 to 150 km/sec (21 cm line full width at 20 peak). WFPC2 on HST reaches below the tip of the red giant branch (RGB) in these galaxies, allowing us to diagnose the star formation history of their thick disk stellar populations.

The noteworthy feature of Figure 6 is the weakness of the color gradient in the disks of these galaxies out to 2 kpc from the plane. The average over the four galaxies is 0.06 0.02 mag/kpc in the sense that the thick disk is redder further from the thin disk at the 3 level. Column (2) of Table 2 records the weighted average V--I for the region of the disk beyond 900 pcs, and column (3) gives the reddening in the Milky Way according to NED and Schlegel (1998). The giant branches of the thick disk regions of these galaxies are consistently red, with V--I colors ranging from 1.39 to 1.54 (column 4).

sources.summary: The SIDM fits include NGC 1560 among similar-velocity galaxies, assigning it a median halo and noting a mild disk effect consistent with its luminosity.

quotes.quote: In Fig.  fig:examples , we show SIDM fits for UGC 5721 , NGC 1560 , and UGC 5750 . All have similar V_f 80  km/s , but the shapes of the rotation curves are very different in the inner regions. UGC 5721 and UGC 5750 are at opposite extremes for the rotation curve diversity in this mass range. Despite the diversity, the SIDM halo model provides an impressive fit to the rotation curves.

We find that NGC 1560 has a median halo, UGC 5721 has a denser halo, and UGC 5750 has an underdense halo, but all within 2 of the median expectation. The observed (2  ) is close to 20  km/s for UGC 5750, while the corresponding CDM halo has (2  ) 30  km/s even with a concentration 2 lower than the median value. The effect of the disk is most significant in UGC 5721, resulting in a SIDM halo similar to the CDM one and a flat V_ cir even at 2  kpc . The effect becomes mild in NGC 1560 and negligible in UGC 5750, consistent with their luminosities.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1560 as a gas-dominated low-surface-brightness spiral whose rotation curve and low stellar mass-to-light ratio test the authors' formalism against MOND.

quotes.quote: In contrast, NGC 1560 is a low surface brightness spiral with a dominant gas component. Its observed rotation curve extends out to about 8 kpc and does not seem to have reached its asymptotic regime. In both galaxies our _ NN ^2 is noticeably small. Significant, however, is the low stellar mass-to-light ratio of the young and gas-dominated NGC 1560. Our =0.3 is, by far, closer to 0.4 estimate of McGaugh (2002) than to 1.1 of MOND.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 1560 as a benchmark isolated low surface brightness disk galaxy, matching its baryon distribution and finding an external acceleration compatible with its rotation curve.

quotes.quote: We also model NGC 1560, a benchmark Low Surface Brightness (LSB) disk galaxy (Broeils 1992). We use an exponential stellar disk of 1.97 10^8 and a multi-Gaussian gaseous component of 1.07 10^9 to match the observed baryon distribution(Broeils 1992). A MOND Poisson solver (Ciotti, Londrillo, Nipoti 2006) with 256 64 64 grid points is applied, the radial grid being r_i=10.0 (i+0.5) 0.5 256+1 kpc. Again it is found that an acceleration of 0.01a_0 is compatible with the rotation curve of NGC1560, which is an isolated galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 1560 among four galaxies whose rotation curves are fit with the modified acceleration law, reporting good agreement and the galaxy's fitted luminous mass and core radius.

quotes.quote: In Fig. 4, we show fits to the four galaxies NGC 1560, NGC 2903, NGC 4565 and NGC 5055 using the modified acceleration law ( coremodel ) including the galaxy core with =2 . As can be seen from the data fitting, the agreement with the core and extended rotation curves data is good for the two fitting parameters M and the core radius r_c . In Fig. 5, we display a 3-dimensional plot of v versus the range of distance 0.1 kpc < < 10 kpc and the range of total galaxy mass M used in the fitting of rotational velocity data.
The luminous mass used to fit the data for NGC 1560 is M=1.51 10^ 10 M_ , while the core radius is r_c=1.5 10^ 22 cm =4.85 kpc .

sources.summary: The review presents NGC 1560 as a low-surface-brightness galaxy whose 21 cm rotation curve is fit by MOND, rising slowly to its asymptotic form with reasonable mass-to-light ratio.

quotes.quote: These are 21 cm line rotation curves of a LSB, low-surface-brightness, (NGC 1560) and a HSB, high surface brightness galaxy, (NGC 2903), where the points show the observations, the dotted and dashed curves are the Newtonian rotation curves of the observed baryonic components (stars and gas), and the solid curves are the MOND curves resulting from the simple algorithm (with a_0 = 1.2 10^ 10 m/s ^2 ). One should note that the rotation curves have the general forms for LSB and HSB predicted by Milgrom in 1983: in the LSB galaxy the curve slowly rises to its asymptotic form and in the HSB the curve falls in a near Keplerian fashion to the final value. The required mass-to-light ratios (shown on the figure) are quite reasonable for a gas dominated and a star dominated galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper applies a new evolutionary synthesis code with chemical evolution models to irregular galaxy NGC 1560, using photometric constraints to estimate a roughly 10 Gy dominant stellar population age.

quotes.quote: We compare results of this new code with other published codes, and we apply it to the irregular galaxy NGC 1560 using sophisticated chemical evolution models.
The application of the code with the chemical models to the galaxy NGC 1560 constrain the star formation age for its stellar population around 10.0 Gy.

In this work, we compute spectro-chemical evolution models only to NGC 1560 because: i) For NGC 1560 and II Zw 33 the nonbaryonic dark matter inside the Holmerg Radius is known, which constrains the chemical evolution models.

gmw_00002181.name: AGC 114905
gmw_00002181.overview: AGC 114905 is a gas-rich ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2201.12852, arXiv:2404.06537] that features a stellar disc with spiral-arm-like structures [arXiv:2404.06537] and an offset between its stellar and neutral hydrogen centroids [arXiv:2101.01753]. The galaxy is characterized by an extremely low-density dark matter halo [arXiv:2404.06537] and a very low gas velocity dispersion [arXiv:2004.14392].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes AGC 114905 as a gas-rich ultra-diffuse galaxy whose low dark-matter content challenges MOND but is fit better by nonlocal gravity.

quotes.quote: AGC 114905 As a recent example, let us first discuss AGC 114905, which is a gas-rich UDG with total baryonic mass M =(1.4 0.2) 10^9 M_ . Its rotation curve seems to be flat and approaches v_c 28 km / s at radius 8 kpc. The recent high-resolution interferometric observations imply that this UDG does not follow the standard concentration-halo mass relation . More specifically, the dark matter content of the galaxy is much lower than expected.

This is why the NLG rotation curve does not deviate strongly from the baryonic contribution in AGC 114905. This fact can be seen in the left panel in the third row of Fig. AGC114905 , where the effective dark matter density according to NLG is shown at the plane of the galaxy, i.e., z=0 . The gray region indicates the 1 region. We see that the effective dark matter density is much smaller than the baryonic mass density.

sources.summary: The paper presents ultra-deep imaging of AGC 114905, a gas-rich ultra-diffuse dwarf with unusually low-density halo and high baryon fraction, confirming similar stellar/gas morphology and a 31-degree inclination.

quotes.quote: AGC 114905 is a dwarf gas-rich ultra-diffuse galaxy seemingly in tension with the cold dark matter (CDM) model. Specifically, the galaxy appears to have an extremely low-density halo and a high baryon fraction, while CDM predicts dwarfs to have very dense and dominant dark haloes. The alleged tension relies on the galaxy's rotation curve decomposition, which depends heavily on its inclination. This inclination, estimated from the gas morphology, remains somewhat uncertain. We present unmatched ultra-deep optical imaging of AGC 114905 reaching surface brightness limits _ ,lim 32  mag/arcsec ^2 ( 3 ; 10 arcsec 10 arcsec) obtained with the 10.4--m Gran Telescopio Canarias.

With the new imaging, we characterise the galaxy's morphology, surface brightness, colours, and stellar mass profiles in great detail. The stellar disc has a similar extent as the gas, presents spiral arms-like features, and shows a well-defined edge. Stars and gas share similar morphology, and crucially, we find an inclination of 31 2^ , in agreement with the previous determinations.

sources.summary: The paper presents AGC 114905 as an ultradiffuse low-surface-brightness galaxy challenging dark matter and MOND fits, with thickness offering an ultrathin-galaxy interpretation.

quotes.quote: Belonging to this peculiar category of galaxies, AGC 114905 seems to pose a challenge to the standard dark matter model as well as to MOND. Figures 12 and 13 show the high-resolution HI interferometric observations obtained by Mancera Piña et al (2021) for AGC 114905. Figure 12 SMD profile (stars + gas) of AGC 114905 (from figure 1 of Mancera Piña et al, 2021). Figure 13 The rotation curve of AGC 114905 (from figure 5 of Mancera Piña et al, 2021).

As shown in figure 13, we note that MOND and the model give very similar profiles. The agreement between MOND and the model, already identified in the preceding section, naturally underlines that the domain of weak accelerations (MOND) is related to the domain of weak mean densities (the model). Nevertheless, we see that all three theoretical models, dark matter, MOND and , fail to represent the observational curve. Mancera Piña et al (2019) suggest that a drastic modification of the inclination angle, from 26^ within the range 10^ 15^ , would be needed in order to find agreement between the dark matter or MOND prediction and the observational profile of AGC 114905.

Thus, rather than seeing AGC 114905 as a galaxy deprived of dark matter, such an explanation, i.e., by considering the mean thickness, would lead to envisaging this galaxy as a very smeared and hence ultrathin object.

sources.summary: The paper models AGC 114905 with two H I rings, finds a very low gas velocity dispersion, and places it within the observed specific-angular-momentum relation scatter.

quotes.quote: AGC 114905: The size of the beam is 14.64" 13.31", with a North-West orientation of 5^ . The galaxy position angle is 85 ^ . The component of the beam projected along the kinematic major axis has a size of approximately the size of the beam minor axis. Given the extension of the galaxy, we use two rings, with RADSEP = 14.5".
The mean velocity dispersion for AGC 114905, AGC 219533, AGC 248945 and AGC 749290, with a channel width of _ v 4 km s ^ 1 , is = 3 2 km s ^ 1 , which is below _ v .
Three of our galaxies (AGC 114905, AGC 248945 and AGC 749290) lie within the 1 scatter of the relation.

sources.summary: The paper says AGC 114905 has a disk-like but irregular optical component, an offset between stellar and H I centroids, and poorly constrained optical inclination.

quotes.quote: Two sources, AGC 334315 and AGC 749251, have some arc-like features reminiscent of spiral arms, but without distinct patterns or strong evidence of a stellar disk. Three sources, AGC 114905, AGC 748738, and AGC 749290, have observed optical components that loosely resemble stellar disks in our optical images, but still would be better classified as irregular galaxies at the detected level. Also, one source, AGC 219533, has a bright, very blue higher surface brightness clump superimposed on its low surface brightness emission; it is not fully clear if it is a bright star forming region associated with the galaxy, or if it is a foreground or background source.

In many cases the centroid of the stellar population is aligned with the peak in the \ column density, but there are several exceptions, including AGC 114905, AGC 238764, and AGC 248945. However, we note that AGC 238764 and AGC 248945 are less well resolved and lower signal-to-noise detections than the other sources in the sample, so the position of their peak column density is somewhat less well defined. Importantly, we note that we only poorly constrain the galaxies' inclinations using the stellar populations.

sources.summary: The paper argues that AGC 114905's baryon-only rotation-curve interpretation makes its disc violently unstable in simulations, so stability likely requires a lower inclination and a substantial dark halo.

quotes.quote: Recent 21 cm line observations of the ultra-diffuse galaxy AGC 114905 indicate a rotating disc largely supported against gravity by orbital motion, as usual. Remarkably, this study has revealed that the form and amplitude of the HI rotation curve is completely accounted for by the observed distribution of baryonic matter, stars and neutral gas, implying that no dark halo is required. It is surprising to find a DM-free galaxy for a number of reasons, one being that a bare Newtonian disk having low velocity dispersion would be expected to be unstable to both axi- and non-axisymmetric perturbations that would change the structure of the disc on a dynamical timescale, as has been known for decades.

We present N body simulations of the DM-free model, and one having a low-density DM halo, that confirm this expectation: the disc is chronically unstable to just such instabilities. Since it is unlikely that a galaxy that is observed to have a near-regular velocity pattern would be unstable, our finding calls into question the suggestion that the galaxy may lack, or have little, dark matter. We also show that if the inclination of this near face-on system has been substantially overestimated, the consequent increased amplitude of the rotation curve would accommodate a halo massive enough for the galaxy to be stable.

sources.summary: The paper argues that MOND simulations of AGC 114905-like low-surface-brightness disks can make face-on isophotes non-circular, causing inclination overestimates and reconciling its rotation curve with MOND.

quotes.quote: We present hydrodynamical star-forming simulations in the Milgromian dynamics (MOND) framework of a gas-rich disc galaxy with properties similar to AGC 114905, which has recently been argued to have a rotation curve (RC) that is inconsistent with the MOND prediction. Our first model considers the galaxy in isolation, while our second model includes an external field of 0.05 a_ _0 , the estimated gravitational field from large-scale structure. We show that isophotes in the face-on view can differ from circular at the 50 level.

This could mislead observers into overestimating the inclination i between disc and sky planes. Because RCs require a correction factor of 1/ i , the actual RC could be much higher than reported by observers. This plausibly reconciles AGC 114905 with MOND expectations.

sources.summary: The paper finds AGC114905's atomic gas disc appears unstable in 2D, but its 3D vertical structure stabilizes it; alternate lower-dispersion data would make it unstable.

quotes.quote: We thus conclude that, at z 0 , only the atomic gas disc of the UDG AGC114905 can be classified as locally unstable across a significantly large region disc. Unsurprisingly, the atomic gas disc of AGC114905 is also unstable if we use the data from , who measure lower with respect to . For AGC114905, the radial extent of the unstable region where Q 1 is L_Q 4 kpc (see Table tab:gas_inst ).

Interestingly, we find that two galaxies with Q<1 in some radial interval, namely AGC114905 and SPT2146-55, have >1 everywhere throughout the disc. Therefore, no galaxy in our sample at z 0 host unstable gas discs, considering both the atomic gas and the molecular gas. We note that, using the data from , we would find that AGC114905 is locally unstable, as the stabilising effect of the gas vertical structure is less effective since is slightly lower than in .

sources.summary: The paper compares AGC 114905 with SIDM halo predictions, finding its low inner density and large core require an unusually low-concentration halo to fit.

quotes.quote: Among the UDGs, AGC 114905  PinaMancera211200017 has the lowest inner halo density and the largest core size; our SIDM halos are still too dense in the inner regions to fit this system. It is possible that AGC 114905's halo concentration is even lower than any halos in our simulation. To test this, we used a parametric model to predict SIDM profiles as a function of halo concentration  Yang:2023jwn ; we found that our SIDM model can fit AGC 114905 for c_ 200 2.2 with M_ 200 1.4 10^ 10   M_ ( V_ max 34  km s^ 1 and _ eff /m 99  cm^2 g^ 1 ). This concentration would be `` 7 " below the median according to the standard c_ 200 - M_ 200 relation  2014MNRAS.441.3359D . However, the low-concentration tail of this relation is a power law, rather than a Gaussian.

gmw_00002182.name: Leo V
gmw_00002182.overview: Leo V is a faint satellite galaxy that shows signs of being tidally stripped [arXiv:1111.6608]. These indications include a disturbed structure, an apparently extended blue horizontal branch stellar distribution, and suggestions of a stellar bridge connecting it to Leo IV [arXiv:1111.6608].

sources.summary: The paper highlights Leo V as a faint satellite with several possible tidal-stripping signs, including disturbed structure, extended BHB evidence, and suggestions of a connection to Leo IV.

quotes.quote: Leo V bears several hints that it might have been tidally stripped, including a spectroscopic [ Fe/H ] that is higher than MW satellites of similar luminosity, and more compatible with brighter objects. Leo V also shows signs of being disturbed, with an apparently extended blue horizontal branch distribution leov and kinematic members more then 10 half light radii away from the satellite center Walker09 . There have also been suggestions that Leo V and the nearby satellite Leo IV are connected by a `stellar bridge', and share a common origin but see Sandleoiv leoivleov .

However, given our Leo V structural parameters for the Plummer (exponential) profile fit, there is only a 0.4 (0.3 ) chance of getting a BHB sample with an observed median radius that is at least as small as that measured for the bulk of Leo V stars -- strong evidence that the BHB stars in Leo V are more extended than the fitted profile see also leov . Intrigued by the possibility that Leo V may have an extended BHB stellar distribution, and seeking additional evidence, we ran our ML structural analysis code (see sec:paramfit ) directly on our BHB sample. The advantage of using the ML code is that it naturally incorporates a background surface density ( _ b ) into the structural measurement. In this case, we found a half light radius of r_ h =2.9 0.8 arcmin for our BHB stellar sample (assuming an exponential profile), which is 1.8 discrepant from that found for Leo V's ridgeline stars. While tantalizing, we can not say definitively that Leo V has an extended BHB stellar distribution.

gmw_00002183.name: Pisces II
gmw_00002183.overview: Pisces II is a faint, distant satellite galaxy of the Milky Way located approximately 182 kpc away [arXiv:1111.6608]. It is characterized by an old stellar population older than 10 Gyr and a metal-poor composition [arXiv:1111.6608].

sources.summary: The paper studies Pisces II with deep wide-field imaging, measures it as a faint distant satellite, and finds it has an old, metal-poor stellar population similar to Leo V and CVn II.

quotes.quote: We have utilized deep and wide field imaging of three of the most distant, faint and recently discovered MW satellites -- Leo V, Pisces II and CVn II -- to study their SFH and structure. Our data reached between 0.5 and 1 magnitudes below the main sequence turnoff, and our imaging fields of view were a factor of 10 larger than the satellites' half light radii. In their structural properties and SFH, all three objects are relatively similar, with half light radii between 60 r_ h 90 pc and stellar populations which are old ( >10 Gyr) and metal poor ( [ Fe/H ] 2 ).

The distance to Pisces II determined in the discovery paper of Belokurov10 , D 182 kpc, is consistent with our measurements to that satellite. Given the ambiguity of M92's distance, the lack of a spectroscopic [ Fe/H ] for Pisces II, and the reasonable consistency of their stellar populations with the [ Fe/H ] = 2.0 isochrones (see sec:sfh ), we utilize our [ Fe/H ] = 2.0 distance measurements for Leo V and Pisces II for the remainder of this work.

gmw_00002184.name: Mrk 463W
gmw_00002184.overview: Mrk 463W is a galaxy that hosts a Compton-thin obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1803.11223]. It exhibits a small bolometric-to-X-ray luminosity ratio, which suggests a low Eddington ratio and an early phase of merger-triggered activity [arXiv:1803.11223].

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 463W is a Compton-thin obscured AGN with an unusually small bolometric-to-X-ray luminosity ratio, suggesting a low Eddington ratio and early merger-driven AGN activity.

quotes.quote: Mrk 463W We determine the absorption of Mrk 463W to be 2.9 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 , confirming that the galaxy contains a Compton-thin obscured AGN.

The bolometric to X-ray luminosity ratio of Mrk 463W, estimated from the L^ band luminosity, shows an unusually small value, 1--3. We suggest that the Eddington ratio of this AGN is small ( <10^ 3 ), in contrast to Mrk 463E. We speculate that Mrk 463W might be still in an early phase of AGN activity triggered by the merger.

gmw_00002185.name: D52
gmw_00002185.overview: D52 is a bright galaxy characterized by a D-type morphology [arXiv:0807.3545]. It is the only target in its sample to display line emission, which is most likely LINER-like rather than driven by star formation [arXiv:0807.3545].

sources.summary: The paper identifies D52 as the only target with line emission, describes it as a bright D-type galaxy with likely LINER-like emission, and estimates small age effects from that emission.

quotes.quote: From the NFPS spectra, the only target to display line emission is D52 ( EW [ O iii 5007 ] = 0.55 ).
D52 is a bright galaxy with a `D'-type morphology, so the emission is most likely LINER-like rather than due to star formation.
Using the tho03-897,tho04-19 stellar population model grids, we find that this causes an over-prediction in the ages of < 5 per cent ( < 1 Gyr for D52).

gmw_00002186.name: D60
gmw_00002186.overview: The galaxy D60 is characterized by a high signal-to-noise ratio within its effective radius [arXiv:0807.3545]. Kinematic mapping of the galaxy reveals a major-axis rotation of approximately 110 kilometers per second [arXiv:0807.3545].

sources.summary: The paper uses D60 as the highest-SNR example for radial binning and kinematic mapping, reporting adopted bins and major-axis rotation of about 110 kms ^ 1.

quotes.quote: As an illustration, Figure fig:bins (left panel) shows the adopted bins for D60, alongside a histogram of the resulting SNRs (inset).
For the galaxy with the highest total SNR within r_ e (D60), we created maps of the kinematics ( V and ).
The maps for V and (Figure fig:kin_maps ) show that D60 has major axis rotation of 110 kms ^ 1 .

gmw_00002187.name: FRB 121102 host galaxy
gmw_00002187.overview: The host galaxy of FRB 121102 is a low-mass, low-metallicity dwarf irregular galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.19273 [arXiv:1701.02370, arXiv:1906.08749]. It is characterized by active star formation [arXiv:1701.02370, arXiv:2005.13160] and contains a compact, persistent radio source [arXiv:1704.08097, arXiv:1811.10748].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the FRB 121102 dwarf host galaxy as low-mass and star-forming, similar to LGRB and SLSN-I hosts, supporting a young magnetar origin.

quotes.quote: Sub-arcsecond localization of the repeating fast radio burst FRB 121102 revealed its coincidence with a dwarf host galaxy and a steady (`quiescent') non-thermal radio source. We show that the properties of the host galaxy are consistent with those of long-duration gamma-ray bursts (LGRB) and hydrogen-poor superluminous supernovae (SLSNe-I).

Optical imaging and spectroscopy by Tendulkar+17 identify an extended source coincident with the burst displaying prominent Balmer and [ OIII ] lines at a redshift of z = 0.19273 , indicating a dwarf galaxy of estimated stellar mass M_ 4-7 10^ 7 M_ at a luminosity distance of D = 972 Mpc 3 10^ 27 cm. Based on H flux of the galaxy, Tendulkar+17 infer a total star formation rate of 0.4 M_ yr ^ 1 , which they use to estimate a maximum DM through the plane of the galaxy of 324 pc cm ^ 3 .

In Figure fig:host compares the properties of the host of \ to those of LGRBs and SLSN-I at z 1 . In particular, we show the rest-frame optical absolute magnitudes, stellar masses, star formation rates, and metallicities. We find that in all of these fundamental properties, the host of \ is remarkably similar to the hosts of LGRBs and SLSN-I. It is located roughly at, or slightly below, the medians of the LGRB and SLSN-I host galaxy distributions, especially when considering the low redshift population at z 0.3 .

aliases_in_paper: host galaxy of the bursts and the persistent radio source
sources.summary: The paper describes FRB 121102 as occurring in a low-metallicity star-forming dwarf galaxy with a compact persistent radio source and a plausible low-luminosity AGN/jet origin.

quotes.quote: They found that FRB 121102 originates very close to a faint and persistent radio source with a continuum spectrum consistent with non-thermal emission and a faint optical counterpart. This latter optical source has been identified by tendulkar2017 as a low-metallicity, star-forming, dwarf galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.19273(8) , corresponding to a luminosity distance of 972 Mpc. Further insights on the persistent radio source were provided by marcote2017 through very-long-baseline radio interferometric observations. Marcote et al. were able to simultaneously detect and localize both, the bursts and the persistent radio source, on milliarcsecond scales. The bursts are found to be consistent with the location of the persistent radio source within a projected linear separation of less than 40 pc, 12 mas angular separation, at 95 confidence, and thus both are likely related. The unambiguous association of FRB 121102 with persistent radio and optical counterparts, along with the identification of its host galaxy, impose, for the first time, very strict constraints upon theoretical models for FRBs, beyond the general limits imposed by variability timescales and energy budgets.

The host galaxy of the bursts and the persistent radio source is a dwarf star-forming galaxy with a diameter of less than 4 kpc and a high star-forming rate of 0.4 M _ yr ^ 1 . The stellar mass of the galaxy is estimated to be in the range 4 - 7 10^ 7 M _ . Current evidence supports the idea that the supermassive black hole-to-galaxy mass ratio lies within 0.01-0.05 targett2012 ; there are few exceptions for which this ratio can be as high as 0.15 e.g. vanderbosch2012 . Therefore, the presence of a supermassive black hole with mass M_ BH >10^7 \ M _ in the host galaxy of is unlikely, because it would already have a mass larger than, or of the order of, the total stellar mass of the galaxy. This leads to an estimation of the mass of the putative black hole of M_ BH 10^5 - 10^6 \ M _ .

sources.summary: The paper observes the FRB host galaxy in CO, reports non-detections and molecular-gas upper limits, and describes it as a low-mass, low-metallicity dwarf with star formation but no optical AGN evidence.

quotes.quote: We present SMA and NOEMA observations of the host galaxy of \ in the CO 3-2 and 1-0 transitions, respectively. We do not detect emission from either transition. We set 3 upper limits to the CO luminosity L_ CO < 2.5 10^7 pc^ 2 for CO 3-2 and L_ CO < 2.3 10^9 pc^ 2 for CO 1-0. For Milky-Way-like star formation properties, we set a 3 upper limit on the H_2 mass of 2.5 10^8 , slightly less than the predictions for the H_2 mass based on the star formation rate. The true constraint on the H_2 mass may be significantly higher, however, because of the reduction in CO luminosity that is common for low-metallicity dwarf galaxies like the FRB host galaxy.

The FRB is associated with a dwarf galaxy at z=0.19273 0.00008 , <10^8 in stellar mass, and diameter 2 ( 3 \ kpc ) 2017ApJ...834L...7T,2017ApJ...843L...8B,2017ApJ...844...95K . High resolution imaging with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) shows a bright, compact (80 mas) knot of continuum emission coincident with the FRB and the persistent radio source, along with diffuse, extended emission. Analysis of emission lines in the optical spectrum shows evidence of star formation with SFR 0.2 and no evidence for an AGN.

sources.summary: The paper compares FRB 121102 host galaxy properties with nearby-galaxy catalogs, noting its faint redshifted star-forming region and arguing its luminosity is not exceptional in the catalog.

quotes.quote: The localization is consistent with the position of a star formation region in a faint galaxy ( =25.1 mag) at a redshift of 0.19273 (Tendulkar et al. 2017; Bassa et al. 2017). Furthermore, Chatterjee et al. (2017) reported the existence of a persistent radio source within 1'' of the FRB location, and Marcote et al. (2017) showed that the FRB and the persistent source are separated by less than 12 mas ( 40 pc projected). The radio source has a mean flux density of about 0.18 mJy, it is variable, presumably due to scintillations, and unresolved with an angular size smaller than about 1.7 mas.

For example, based on the low luminosity of the FRB 121102 host (extinction corrected band abs. mag. 16.8 ), Nicholl et al. (2017) claimed that FRBs prefer dwarf galaxies. Using the Karachentsev et al. (2004) catalog, I calculate the fraction of B band luminosity in galaxies fainter than the host galaxy of FRB 121102 (i.e., g band abs. mag. 16.6 ; assuming g- 0.2 mag). Therefore, I cannot rule out the possibility that the FRB 121102 host galaxy luminosity follows the general galaxy luminosity function, at more than 97 confidence. I note that the luminosity of the FRB 121102 host galaxy is near the peak of the luminosity function of galaxies in my catalog.

sources.summary: The paper describes the FRB 121102 host as an irregular, low-metallicity dwarf with a bright star-forming FRB site, but argues its DM and luminosity make it atypical among nearby FRB hosts.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of FRB 121102 is an irregular, low-metallicity dwarf galaxy. FRB 121102 resides in the bright star-forming region in the galaxy. The properties of the host and the sub-galactic localization of the source is similar to those of Long Gamma Ray Bursts (LGRBs) and SuperLuminous Supernova (SLSNe), some of which have been suggested to leave behind rapidly spinning magnetars.

FRB 121102 has an estimated DM_ host in the range of 55-225 pc cm ^ 3 . It is consistent with the estimated value of LGRBs and SLSNe. Also, the absolute magnitude of its host galaxy, 17 , is consistent with the values for the LGRB and SLSN host samples. Other FRB host candidates, on the other hand, are not consistent with the LGRB and SLSN host samples.

sources.summary: The FRB 121102 host is described as a low-luminosity star-forming dwarf with an active region, persistent radio source, high rotation measure, and elevated SFR for its stellar mass.

quotes.quote: The first localization was achieved for the first known ``repeating FRB" 121102 spitler2016repeating . It resides in a low-luminosity ( M_r 17 ), star-forming dwarf galaxy at a redshift of z=0.192 VLAlocalisation,host . Its spatial coincidence with an active star-forming region in the host, a persistent high luminosity radio source and an extreme magnetoionic environment led to a ``concordance picture" of the source of FRB 121102 as a flaring magnetar embedded in a magnetized ion-electron wind nebula Margalit+2018 .

The RM observed for FRB 121102 is a thousand times higher than for any of the ASKAP FRBs, suggesting an unusual (or at least atypical) environment for this repeating FRB. In terms of the radio properties of host galaxies, none of the ASKAP host galaxies have a compact persistent radio source as luminous as the one in the FRB 121102 host galaxy, despite the ASKAP bursts being at least a hundred times more energetic. The host galaxy for FRB 121102 is a low-mass dwarf and has an elevated SFR for its stellar mass. As Bannister+19 pointed out (their Table S8), fewer than 1 of stars lie in galaxies as faint as this.

sources.summary: The review describes FRB 121102's host as a low-metallicity, low-mass dwarf at z = 0.193, associated with an intense star-forming region and useful for links to other transients.

quotes.quote: In parallel, 2017ApJ...834L...7T acquired Gemini North spectroscopic data that detected the optical source and measured its redshift: z = 0.193 , which corresponds to a luminosity distance of 1 Gpc. The extragalactic origin and host galaxy of \ were thus conclusively established. 's host galaxy turned out to be a low-metallicity, low-mass dwarf 2017ApJ...834L...7T .

Deeper observations of the host using the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) revealed that \ is coincident with an intense star-forming region 2017ApJ...843L...8B . The EVN radio position is offset from the optical centroid of the star-forming region by 55 mas, statistically significant, but within the half-light radius.
The repeating FRB 121102 resides in a low-metalicity dwarf galaxy, and searches for galaxies of similar type have been done for other FRBs 2018ApJ...867L..10M .

sources.summary: The paper describes FRB 121102 as localized to a star-forming region of a dwarf host galaxy at 1 Gpc, with the host type motivating magnetar-origin scenarios.

quotes.quote: Discovered in the Arecibo PALFA pulsar survey cfl+06,lbh+15 , \ is a source of sporadically repeating fast radio bursts sch+14,ssh+16a,ssh+16b . The direct and precise localization of these bursts has shown that \ is hosted in the star-forming region of a dwarf galaxy at a luminosity distance of 1 Gpc z = 0.193 ; clw+17,tbc+17,mph+17,bta+17 . This association thus confirms the extragalactic distance of , as was previously inferred from its DM sch+14 . \ is also associated with a compact (diameter < 0.7 pc), persistent radio source with isotropic luminosity L_ radio 10^ 39 erg s ^ 1 clw+17,mph+17 .

Based on the host galaxy type, which is also known to host superluminous supernovae (SLSNe) and long gamma-ray bursts (LGRBs), it has been suggested that \ originates from a millisecond magnetar formed in the last few decades mbm17,tbc+17,mph+17 . This scenario can also naturally explain the co-location of \ with a star-forming region, as well as its association with the persistent radio source, which would represent a pulsar or magnetar wind nebula (PWN or MWN) and/or a supernova remnant (SNR) pir16,mkm16,km17,mmb+18 .

sources.summary: The paper includes the FRB 20121102A host as a dwarf-galaxy non-detection with literature CO limits, deriving a molecular-gas upper limit using a metallicity-dependent conversion factor.

quotes.quote: We report the properties of molecular gas in a sample of six host galaxies of fast radio bursts (FRBs) obtained from CO observations with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (FRBs 20180924B, 20190102C, and 20190711A) and results of one non-detection in a dwarf galaxy (FRB 20121102A) and two events detected in M81 (FRB 20200120E) and the Milky Way (FRB 20200428A).

conducted Submillimeter Array and Northern Extended Millimeter Array observations of the host galaxy of FRB 20121102A in the CO(3--2) and CO(1--0) transitions, respectively, and did not detect emission from either transition. In this Letter, we present molecular gas observations in the host galaxies of three FRBs (20180924B, 20190102C, and 20190711A) performed with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA). Adding the other three host galaxies of FRBs (20121102A, 20200120E, and 20200428A) for which molecular gas data are available in the literature, we discuss the properties of molecular gas in the six host galaxies.

The CO(1--0) and (3--2) observations of the FRB 20121102A host by provided 3 upper limits of L'_ CO(1-0) < 2.3 10^9 K km s ^ 1 pc ^2 and L'_ CO(3-2) < 2.5 10^8 K km s ^ 1 pc ^2 . The molecular gas mass of M_ gas < 9.1 10^9 M_ is derived from the CO(3--2) line luminosity by using the metallicity-dependent _ CO of 200 M_ (K km s ^ 1 pc ^2 ) ^ 1 schr12 and the same CO line luminosity ratio adopted for our sample.

sources.summary: The review describes the FRB 121102 host as a z=0.19 dwarf star-forming galaxy, analogous to long-GRB and superluminous-supernova hosts but atypical among FRB hosts, shaping young-magnetar interpretations.

quotes.quote: This was followed by the detection of the first repeating source, known as FRB 121102 (ref. ) and its localization in a dwarf star forming galaxy at redshift z = 0.19 (refs. ), establishing the extragalactic/cosmological origin (in contrast to the Galactic origin ) of these events.
The host galaxy of FRB 121102 is analogous to those of long GRBs and superluminous supernovae , but it is atypical.

The special host galaxy properties of FRB 121102 (ref. ) drove the idea that young magnetars born in long GRBs and superluminous supernovae are the sources of repeating FRBs . Searches for FRBs in GRB or superluminous supernova remnants have been carried out and so far fruitless (but see ref. ). Later observations suggest that the host galaxies of the majority of FRBs are unlike that of FRB 121102 (refs. ) casting doubt on the ansatz that magnetars from extreme explosions make the main sources of repeating FRBs .

sources.summary: The paper describes the FRB20121102A host as a low-metallicity, highly star-forming dwarf at z=0.1927, associated with a radio nebula and remaining an outlier among FRB hosts.

quotes.quote: FRB20121102A originates in a highly magnetized environment Michilli2018 co-located with a radio nebula in a low-metallicity, highly star-forming dwarf galaxy at z=0.1927 VLAlocalisation,Tendulkar2016 . The immediate environment and host galaxy properties of the FRB20121102A source led to a concordant model for FRBs in which bursts are produced by young magnetars, remnants from SLSNe or LGRBs Margalit+2018 .
