Both these properties exceed what has been commonly observed in ram pressure-stripped galaxies, and we conclude that they are the result of the interaction with the plume. In turn, the first impact of the galaxy on the radio plume might have triggered a mild SFR enhancement in the past 2-5 10^8 yrs, in agreement with the SFH of JO36 derived from the modeling of the MUSE spectra.

sources.summary: JO36 has a truncated H disc, a small tail, and a very low tail SFR fraction; the paper suggests most stripped gas is already lost into the ICM.

quotes.quote: The only other GASP galaxy with a truncated disc is JO36. This galaxy has a =0.014 , barely above the threshold we adopted, and indeed it lies in the same region of Fig. fig:psd as the other truncated discs (see Table tab:tab ) and has a similarly low . We note that JO36 is likely undergoing also a gravitational interaction by a fly-by or a close encounter with another galaxy in the cluster which may have stripped part of the gas and hence increased the gaspIII .

JO36 has a small tail and a truncated H disc (see gaspIII for a detailed study of JO36); for this galaxy (red square in fig:model_obs ) in fact we measured a very low fraction of SFR in the tail ( < 1 ) while our model predicts a consistent fraction of stripped gas. The most plausible scenario is therefore that most of the gas stripped from the galaxy is already lost and dispersed in the ICM and just a very minor fraction of it is close to the galaxy and in dense regions still able to form stars.

gmw_00002227.name: JO135
gmw_00002227.overview: JO135 is a massive galaxy featuring a long tail of ionized gas [arXiv:2006.16032]. The galaxy hosts a central active galactic nucleus with an ionization cone that extends into this tail [arXiv:1811.00823, arXiv:2006.16032], while star formation accounts for only one to two percent of the total star formation rate [arXiv:1811.00823].

sources.summary: JO135 has a central AGN ionization cone extending into the tail; nevertheless, much tail H emission remains SF-powered, pure-SF contribution is lower, and tail SFR is 1-2% of total.

quotes.quote: Finally, we note that all three DDs agree that two of the galaxies (JO135 and JO204) present a ionization cone from the central AGN that extends for several kpc and contributes to the ionization of gas even in the tails see also Poggianti2017b . Nonetheless, also in these galaxies a large fraction of the tail H emission appears to be powered by SF (53 /68 /37 in JO135 and 77 /85 /71 in JO204 from the NII/SII/OI DDs, see Table 2).

SF+Composite emission accounts for at least 98 of the tail emission in all galaxies, except in JO135 and JO204 that, as discussed above, have AGN ionization cones extending in the tail, and in JO147 where there is a 10 LINER component. SF still accounts for more than the half of the tail emission in JO175 and JO60 (66 and 58 , respectively), and only for about 20-30 in JO135 (29 ) , JO204 (36 ) and JO194 (21 ). In another 5 galaxies the tail SFR is about 4-5 of the total (JO60, JO147, JO175, JO95, JO194), while for the remaining 5 galaxies it is at the 1-2 level (JO113, JO141, JO135, JO49, JO160).

sources.summary: JO135 is an outlier with a long ionised-gas tail but low measured tail SFR, likely because central-AGN ionization makes part of its tail excluded from the SFR calculation.

quotes.quote: It is JO135 (shown as a yellow diamond). This massive galaxy has a rather long tail of ionised gas but we measure a low SFR in the tail ( =0.03 , 1 ). This is likely due to the fact that part of the gas in its tail is ionised by the radiation from the central AGN gaspVI,gaspXIX and this was consequently not considered in the computation of the total SFR in the tail. The measured could therefore be substantially under-estimated.

gmw_00002228.name: UHS J0439+1634
gmw_00002228.overview: UHS J0439+1634 is a strongly lensed quasar at a redshift of approximately 6.5 [arXiv:1810.11924, arXiv:2007.15657]. Its host galaxy is a compact, rotation-dominated ultraluminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:2106.07429, arXiv:2404.15413] that exhibits extreme star formation [arXiv:2106.07429].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0439+1634's host as a lensed z=6.5 compact ultraluminous infrared galaxy with exponential-disk-like emission, rotation-like kinematics, high gas fraction, oversized SMBH, and extreme star formation.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of J0439+1634 can be well-fitted by a S ersic profile consistent with an exponential disk, both in the far-infrared (FIR) continuum and the emission. The overall magnification is 4.53 0.05 for the continuum and 3.44 0.05 for the line. The host galaxy of J0439+1634 is a compact ultra-luminous infrared galaxy, with a total star formation rate (SFR) of 1.56 10^ 3 M_ / year after correcting for lensing and an effective radius of 0.74 kpc.
The resolved regions in J0439+1634 follow the `` deficit," where the to-FIR ratio decreases with FIR surface brightness. The reconstructed velocity field of J0439+1634 appears to be rotation-like.

J0439+1634 is likely to have a high gas-mass fraction and an oversized SMBH compared to local relations. The SFR of J0439+1634 reaches the maximum possible values, and the SFR surface density is close to the highest value seen in any star-forming galaxy currently known in the universe. (galaxies:) quasars: general, (galaxies:) quasars: individual, submillimeter: galaxies In the past two decades, more than 200 quasars at z>6 have been discovered e.g., matsuoka16, matsuoka18a, matsuoka2018b, matsuoka19, venemans13, venemans15, yang19a, yang20, wang17, wang19a, jiang16, banados16, banados18 .

sources.summary: The paper uses J0439+1634 as the first known strongly lensed z>6 quasar, with z=6.51, three modeled images, high magnification, and possible survey misclassification at lower magnification.

quotes.quote: report the detection of J0439+1634, a z = 6.51 quasar whose best-fit model predicts three images and a total magnification = 51.3 1.4 . This lensed quasar is the first example of this class detected during the reionization epoch BL01, Fan_2006 . suggested the possibility of a high fraction (up to 1/3 ) of lensed sources among high- z quasars, due to the large lensing optical depths reached at z 6 .

For J0439+1634 ( m_ AB = 21.04 0.01 ) we thus expect a bias factor of 10 . While the detection of J0439+1634 was serendipitous and not necessarily representative of the population from which it was drawn, a comprehensive probabilistic analysis with more examples can inform us about the value of the slope , once the magnification bias factor is taken into account.

It is remarkable to note that, if we de-magnified J0439+1634 by a factor 5 , reducing its total magnification to 10 , its flux would have been comparable to the one from the foreground lens galaxy and, thus, the quasar would have been misclassified.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0439+1634 as the first and only known z>5 lensed quasar, valuable for high-redshift quasar studies, with a compact configuration that is rare but possible.

quotes.quote: In fan19 , we reported the discovery of the first gravitationally lensed quasar at z>5 (J0439+1634 at z=6.52 ). High-redshift lensed quasars such as J0439+1634 are valuable, as they enable in-depth investigations of distant quasars with enhanced sensitivity and resolution e.g., yang19b, yue21 .
However, there are only three lensed quasars at z>4.5 known so far, including a couple at z=4.8 mcgreer10, more16 and J0439+1634 at z=6.52 .
The only lensed quasar at z>5 known to date, J0439+1634 at z=6.52 , is a compact system with =0 2 . Figure fig:sep suggests that such systems are rare but possible.

sources.summary: The paper treats J0439+1634 as a z=6.52 gravitationally lensed quasar host with ALMA/NOEMA observations, flux-only FIR treatment, and rotation-dominated kinematics.

quotes.quote: The NOEMA observation of the gravitationally lensed quasar J0439+1634 at z=6.52 was published in and the high-resolution (C43-5) ALMA observation of this object was published in . In this paper, we present the ALMA C43-3 observation of J0439+1634 obtained from this program.

There is no precise magnification measurement in FIR of the gravitationally lensed quasar, J0439+1634, thus we only report the flux measurements of this source and discard the luminosity and size measurements in the following analyses. We refer the readers to and for a detailed characterization of the host galaxy of this quasar.

We therefore conclude to classify J0038--1527, J0224--4711, J0229--0808, J0246--5219, J0439+1634, J0525--2406, J0706+2921, J0910--0414, J1058+2930, and J2002--3013 (43 ) as rotation-dominated galaxies (Fig. fig:mom_vel ).

sources.summary: The paper cites J0439+1634 as another z>6 QSO resolved into multiple components, with lensing magnification that changes inferred luminosity and black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: Moreover, recent HST observations report that another SDSS QSO at z=6.34 , J0439+1634, known as the brightest QSO ( L_ bol =5.9 10^ 14 L_ ) at z>6 , is resolved into multiple objects with the existence of the nearby, low-luminous galaxy at the photometric redshift z 0.7 fan2019,pacucci2019 . The authors argue that the magnification factor is estimated to be 50 in their best-fit mass model that dramatically changes the intrinsic physical properties of J0439+1634 such as L_ bol and M_ BH . Importantly, the g , , i band AB magnitudes of the foreground galaxy of J0439+1634 are 23--25 mag that are lower than the SDSS limiting magnitudes (5 21--22 mag) and thus negligible in the color diagnostics used for the QSO selection. Our and recent results suggest a possibility that a number of other bright QSOs at z 6 are caused by the gravitational lensing effect by such low-luminous, but nearby foreground galaxies, even in the case that the morphology appears to consist of a single component.

sources.summary: The paper uses J0439+1634 as the cautionary lensed z=6.52 quasar: HST resolved its lensing structure, and correcting magnification raises its lifetime estimate from about 10^3 to 10^6 years.

quotes.quote: One example of this is the quasar J0439+1634, a lensed quasar at z=6.52 with a small lensing separation of =0 2 and a large magnification of =51 fan19 . J0439+1634 is unresolved in ground-based imaging even with adaptive optics, and its lensing nature was confirmed only after the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) was able to resolve its lensing structure and detect the foreground deflector galaxy. J0439+1634 is also an excellent example of how lensing magnification affects quasar lifetime estimates. davies20 shows that the estimated lifetime of J0439+1634 is t_Q 10^3 yrs before accounting for the magnification effect, which becomes t_Q 10^6 yrs after correcting for the lensing magnification.

sources.summary: The paper reports J043947.08+163415.7 as a strongly lensed z=6.51 quasar, multiply imaged by a foreground galaxy with about 50-fold magnification and exceptional apparent brightness.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of J043947.08+163415.7, a strongly lensed quasar at z=6.51 , the first such object detected at the epoch of reionization, and the brightest quasar yet known at z>5 . High-resolution HST imaging reveals a multiple imaged system with a maximum image separation 0.2 , best explained by a model of three quasar images lensed by a low luminosity galaxy at z 0.7 , with a magnification factor of 50 . The existence of this source suggests that a significant population of strongly lensed, high redshift quasars could have been missed by previous surveys, as standard color selection techniques would fail when the quasar color is contaminated by the lensing galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats UHS J0439+1634 as a confirmed strongly lensed z=6.52 quasar and shows its proximity zone recovers a magnification consistent with previous lens models.

quotes.quote: Apart from the recent discovery of UHS J0439+1634 at z 6.52 , no other strongly lensed z 6 quasar has been conclusively identified.

We first show that our proximity zone analysis can recover the strongly lensed nature of UHS J0439+1634, with an estimated magnification =28.0^ +18.4 _ 11.7 (^ +44.9 _ 18.3 ) at 68 (95 ) credibility that is consistent with previously published lensing models.

While observational efforts to detect multiple images of z>5 quasars at 0 1 resolution have been unsuccessful Richards06lens,McGreer14 , the recent discovery of the strongly lensed z=6.5 quasar UHS J0439+1634 ( Fan19 ; henceforth J0439+1634), and its closely separated lens galaxy, has lead to suggestions that a larger population of lensed quasars may have been missed due to color selection biases Fan19,PL19 .

sources.summary: The paper cites J0439+1634 as a compact z=6.51 multiply imaged quasar whose large magnification makes it exceptionally bright but too small-separation for ground-based discovery criteria.

quotes.quote: As an example, fan19 report a compact lens with separation of 0 2 at z=6.51 , J0439+1634, which is the only known multiply-imaged quasar at z>5 to date. J0439+1634 has a large magnification of =51 , making it the brightest quasar at z>6 in nearly all wavelengths, and provides so far the best case study of a high-redshift quasar. Although J0439+1634 is bright (m_z=19.49 0.02) , the small separation makes it undiscoverable in ground-based imaging surveys.

sources.summary: The paper treats J0439+1634 as the sole confirmed z>6 lensed quasar, noting its z=6.51 redshift, possible high magnification, selection-bias implications, and HST confirmation.

quotes.quote: Although > 200 quasars are now detected at z 6 , remarkably only one lensed quasar at z > 6 was discovered Fan_2019 : J0439+1634 at z=6.51 , with a possible magnification factor 50 .
The discovery of the z > 6 lensed quasar J0439+1634 prompted a deeper look into selection criteria.
In fact, the final confirmation that the z = 6.51 quasar J0439+1634 is lensed came from a high-resolution image obtained with the HST Fan_2019 .

gmw_00002229.name: SDSS J0100+2802
gmw_00002229.overview: SDSS J0100+2802 is an ultraluminous quasar at a redshift of 6.326 [arXiv:1705.06388] that hosts a supermassive black hole of approximately ten billion solar masses [arXiv:2211.16261]. The host galaxy is a starburst system [arXiv:2309.04614] undergoing active star formation [arXiv:2303.11961] and interacting with a nearby companion [arXiv:2306.01644].

sources.summary: The paper tests whether J0100+2802, a z=6.3 quasar with an inferred 1.2e10 solar-mass black hole, is strongly lensed and explores implications for high-redshift quasars.

quotes.quote: Among the z>6 quasars, J0100+2802 stands out as the one hosting the most massive SMBH, M_ 1.2 10^ 10 at z 6.3 Wu_2015 . By stretching the parameter space of mass and time to an extreme, this quasar offers a unique view of the population of early SMBHs. Assuming a continuous accretion with Eddington ratio _ Edd M / M _ Edd , the ratio between accretion rate and Eddington rate, and radiative efficiency _ rad 0.1 , the growth time from the initial mass M_ seed is _ growth 0.45 _ rad (1- _ rad ) _ Edd M_ M_ seed Gyr . Assuming that seeding occurs at z 30 (e.g., BL01 ), the existence of this source requires continuous accretion at the Eddington limit from a seed with a minimum mass M_ seed 2,000 .

Our study is motivated by the claim Fugimoto_2019 that J0100+2802, a z 6.3 quasar with M_ 1.2 10^ 10 Wu_2015 is lensed by a magnification factor =450 . The black hole mass is calculated via a virial estimator based on the MgII line which scales as M_ ( L_ , 3000 )^ 0.5 Fugimoto_2019 , such that the final, unlensed mass would be 8 10^8 , decreasing the growth time by 20 . It is worth noting that also the Eddington ratio for J0100+2802 would change dramatically, from _ Edd 1 to _ Edd 0.07 . This source could be the second z > 6 lensed quasar found in less than one year Fan_2019 and, by far, the one with the highest magnification.

sources.summary: The paper presents a deep XMM-Newton observation of the z=6.326 quasar SDSS J010013.02+280225.8, measuring its X-ray spectrum, luminosity, absorption limits, and sub-Eddington accretion properties.

quotes.quote: SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 (hereafter 01) is an ultraluminous quasar at redshift of 6.326, which has an optical and infrared luminosity several times greater than any other high redshift quasars and is inferred to host a 10 ^ 10 M _ black hole wu15 . The quasar is clearly detected in the exploratory \ observation with exposure of 14.8 ks, found to have a steep spectrum with = 3.03 ^ +0.78 _ 0.70 derived from the detected 14 counts ai16 . This super-massive black hole might be growing with rapid accretion, as the bolometric luminosity yielded from X-ray to near-infrared observations close to the Eddington luminosity wu15, ai16 .

A simple power-law model provides acceptable fits to the spectrum with inferred photon index of = 2.30^ +0.10 _ 0.10 . The value of is consistent with the one found by nanni17 . The 1 upper limit on any intrinsic absorption column density is N_ H = 6.07 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 . No significant iron emission lines were detected.

sources.summary: The paper reports a Chandra detection of the ultraluminous z=6.30 quasar, finding high X-ray luminosity, soft spectrum, possible extension, and evidence for super-Eddington accretion viewed at small inclination.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The quasar is detected at equatorial coordinates of 01:00:13.038, +28:02:25.75.

quotes.quote: We report exploratory \ observation of the ultraluminous quasar SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 at redshift 6.30. The quasar is clearly detected by \ with a possible component of extended emission. The rest-frame 2-10 keV luminosity is 9.0 ^ +9.1 _ 4.5 10 ^ 45 erg s ^ 1 with inferred photon index of = 3.03 ^ +0.78 _ 0.70 . This quasar is X-ray bright, with inferred X-ray-to-optical flux ratio \ =-1.22^ +0.07 _ 0.05 , higher than the values found in other quasars of comparable ultraviolet luminosity. The properties inferred from this exploratory observation indicate that this ultraluminous quasar might be growing with super-Eddington accretion and probably viewed with small inclination angle.
Recently, Wu et al. (2015) reported the discovery of SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 (hereafter 01), an ultraluminous quasar with an estimated black hole mass of 12 billion solar masses.

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J0100+2802 as the brightest known high-redshift quasar, with a precisely measured redshift and a surprisingly small proximity zone for its luminosity.

quotes.quote: Additionally, given its extreme brightness, the proximity zone of SDSS J0100+2802 , R_p=7.12 0.13 pMpc and R_ p, corr =3.09 0.06 pMpc, is also exceptionally small.

SDSS J0100+2802 This object is the brightest high redshift quasar known so far Wu2015 with an absolute magnitude of M_ 1450 =-29.14 Banados2016 . Its redshift, z=6.3258 , has been measured precisely by the detection of the line Wang2016 . Given its extreme brightness the quasar exhibits a very small proximity zone of R_p=7.12 0.13 pMpc ( R_ p, corr =3.09 0.06 pMpc, when normalized to a luminosity of M_ 1450 =-27 ) compared to a proximity zone size of R_p= 12.0 2.0 pMpc, that one would expect to see for an object this bright. The redshift uncertainty v=100 km/s results in an uncertainty of the proximity zone measurement of R_p 0.13 pMpc. Continuum uncertainties do not play any significant role for these measurements.

sources.summary: The paper examines whether J0100+2802 is strongly lensed, finding ALMA continuum split into four components and arguing lensing could lower its intrinsic black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: We present morphological properties in the high-resolution (FWHM 0 15 ) Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) 1-mm map for an ultra-luminous quasar (QSO) at z=6.30 , SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 (hereafter J0100+2802), whose black hole mass M_ BH is the most massive ( 1.2 10^ 10 M_ ) at z>6 ever known. We find that the continuum emission of J0100+2802 is resolved into a quadruple system within a radius of 0 2 , which can be interpreted as either multiple dusty star-forming regions in the host galaxy or multiple images due to strong gravitational lensing. The Mg ii absorption and the potential Ly line features have been identified at z=2.33 in the near-infrared spectroscopy towards J0100+2802, and a simple mass model fitting well reproduces the positions and flux densities of the quadruple system, both of which are consistent with the latter interpretation. Although a high-resolution map taken in the Advanced Camera for Survey (ACS) on board Hubble Space Telescope (HST) shows a morphology with an apparently single component, in our fiducial lens mass model it can simply be explained by a 50 pc scale offset between the ALMA and HST emission regions.

In this case, the magnification factor for the observed HST emission is obtained to 450 , reducing the intrinsic M_ BH estimate to even below 10^ 9 M_ . The confirmation or the rejection of the gravitational lensing scenario is important for our understanding of the super-massive black holes in the early Universe.

sources.summary: The paper argues J0100+2802 is likely not strongly lensed: its X-ray flux matches no-lensing expectations, lacks X-ray-faint spectral signatures, and conflicts with required high magnification distributions.

quotes.quote: The z=6.327 quasar SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 (hereafter J0100+2802) is believed to be powered by a black hole more massive than 10^ 10 \ M _ , making it the most massive black hole known in the first billion years of the Universe. However, recent high-resolution ALMA imaging shows four structures at the location of this quasar, potentially implying that it is lensed with a magnification of 450 and thus its black hole is significantly less massive. Furthermore, for the underlying distribution of magnifications of z 6 quasars to produce such an extreme value, theoretical models predict that a larger number of quasars in this epoch should be lensed, implying further overestimates of early black hole masses.

For both cases, J0100+2802's X-ray flux is consistent with the no-lensing scenario; while this could be explained by J0100+2802 being X-ray faint, we find it does not have the X-ray or optical spectral features expected for an X-ray faint quasar. Finally, we compare the overall distribution of X-ray fluxes for known, typical z 6 quasars. We find a 3 tension between the observed and predicted X-ray-to-UV flux ratios when adopting the magnification probability distribution required to produce a =450 quasar.

sources.summary: The paper presents JWST/NIRCam and ground-based spectra of high-redshift quasar J0100+2802, finds a roughly 10^10 solar-mass SMBH, and reports no evidence for strong lensing.

quotes.quote: We present the first rest-frame optical spectrum of a high-redshift quasar observed with JWST/NIRCam in Wide Field Slitless (WFSS) mode. The observed quasar, J0100+2802, is the most luminous quasar known at z>6 . We measure the mass of the central supermassive black hole (SMBH) by means of the rest-frame optical \ emission line, and find consistent mass measurements of the quasar's SMBH of M_ 10^ 10 M_ when compared to the estimates based on the properties of rest-frame UV emission lines \ and , which are accessible from ground-based observatories.

We readdress the question whether this ultra-luminous quasar could be effected by strong gravitational lensing making use of the diffraction limited NIRCam images in three different wide band filters (F115W, F200W, F356W), which improves the achieved spatial resolution compared to previous images taken with the Hubble Space Telescope by a factor of two. We do not find any evidence for a foreground deflecting galaxy, nor for multiple images of the quasar, and determine the probability for magnification due to strong gravitational lensing with image separations below the diffraction limit of 0.05 to be 2.2 10^ 3 . Our observations therefore confirm that this quasar hosts a ten billion solar mass black hole less than 1 Gyr after the Big Bang, which is challenging to explain with current black hole formation models.

sources.summary: The paper uses ALMA Band 9 continuum data to constrain dust temperature, dust mass, star formation, and black-hole versus host-galaxy growth in the z=6.327 quasar SDSS J0100+2802.

quotes.quote: We present ALMA Band 9 continuum observation of the ultraluminous quasi-stellar object (QSO) SDSS J0100+2802, providing a 10 detection at 670 GHz. SDSS J0100+2802 is the brightest QSO with the most massive super massive black hole (SMBH) known at z>6 , and we study its dust spectral energy distribution in order to determine the dust properties and the star formation rate (SFR) of its host-galaxy. We obtain the most accurate estimate so far of the temperature, mass and emissivity index of the dust, having T_ dust =48.4 2.3 K, M_ dust =(2.29 0.83) 10^7 M _ , =2.63 0.23 . This allows us to measure the SFR with the smallest statistical error for this QSO, SFR =265 32 \ M_ yr^ 1 .

Our results enable us to evaluate the relative growth of the SMBH and host galaxy of J0100+2802, finding that the SMBH is dominating the process of BH-galaxy growth in this QSO at z=6.327 , when the Universe was 865 Myr old. Such unprecedented constraints on the host galaxy SFR and dust temperature can only be obtained through high frequency observations, and highlight the importance of ALMA Band 9 to obtain a robust overview of the build-up of the first quasars' host galaxies at z>6 .

sources.summary: The paper reviews SDSS J0100+2802 as an ultraluminous quasar with debated lensing: ALMA clumps suggested a high-magnification model, but JWST and HST imaging find no strong-lensing evidence.

quotes.quote: Additional Notes on SDSS J0100+2802 SDSS J0100+2802 was initially reported by wu15 as a ultraluminous quasar with a SMBH mass of 10^ 10 M_ . SDSS J0100+2802 was later observed by the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array with a beam size of 0 15 fujimoto20 . The ALMA image of SDSS J0100+2802 exhibits four clumps, which are interpreted as the lensed images of the quasar host galaxy in fujimoto20 . The fiducial lensing model suggested by fujimoto20 has an image separation of =0 2 and a magnification of =450 .

SDSS J0100+2802 is also a target of the Guaranteed Time Observation program (Proposal ID: 1243, PI: Lilly) of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) . eilers22 present the NIRCam F115W, F200W, and F356W imaging of SDSS J0100+2802, finding no evidence of strong lensing with separation larger than 0 05 . The JWST observation is consistent with the HST images reported in this work, and rules out the lensing model suggested by fujimoto20 . In the rest of this paper, we use _ max =0 05 as the maximum possible strong lensing separation for SDSS J0100+2802.

sources.summary: The paper studies SDSS J010013.02+280225.8, alias J0100, as a z=6.33 ultraluminous quasar in a massive halo candidate surrounded by a large LBG overdensity.

quotes.quote: We study the environment of the z=6.33 ultraluminous quasar SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 (J0100) to understand its association with large-scale structure. J0100 is an ultraluminous quasar with the most massive black hole known at z 6 , suggesting a high likelihood of residing in a massive DMH. We present wide-field ( 522 square arcminute) imaging in the , i , and z bands from the Large Binocular Camera on the Large Binocular Telescope, with Y and J band imaging from the Wide-field Infrared Camera on the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope, centered on J0100.

Applying color selections, we identify 23 objects as i droput Lyman Break Galaxy (LBG) candidates in the J0100 field. The observed surface density of LBG candidates in the J0100 field corresponds to a galaxy overdensity of =4 (at 8.4 ). This large-scale overdensity suggests that the 22 square arcminute overdensity found by Kashino et al. using JWST data extends out to much larger scales.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 as an ultraluminous z=6.30 quasar whose enormous black-hole mass creates a severe growth-timescale problem for the standard cosmological model.

quotes.quote: The recent discovery of SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 (henceforth J0100+2802), an ultraluminous quasar at redshift z=6.30 , has accentuated the problem of supermassive black-hole growth and evolution in the early Universe . Each of the 50 previously discovered quasars at redshifts z>6 contains a black hole with mass 10^9 M_ , challenging the standard model's predicted timeline, which would have afforded them fewer than 900 Myr to grow after the big bang, but likely even fewer than 500 Myr since the onset of Population II star formation. With an estimated mass of 10-12 10^ 9 M_ , ten times greater than the others, J0100+2802 significantly compounds this time-compression problem.

It is now straightforward to see why the discovery of J0100+2802 at redshift 6.3 presents such a big problem for the standard model. With an inferred mass of 10-12 10^9 M_ , the minimal growth time implied by the Salpeter relation is t-t_ seed 910 Myr (assuming conservatively that M_ seed = 20 M_ ). And since in the standard model t(z=6.3) 880 Myr, not only is this quasar inconsistent with what is known about the transition from the Dark Ages to the EoR, but it would have had to start growing before the big bang, an obviously non-sensical interpretation.

sources.summary: The appendix says this high signal-to-noise spectrum required subdividing the iron template, while telluric correction problems prevented including one line model.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0100+2802 This spectrum has a high signal-to-noise ratio. As a consequence the monolithic iron template around the line ( 2200-3500 ) was not able to properly model the continuum. Therefore, we divided the iron template into three regions similar to Tsuzuki2006 ( 2200-2660 , 2660-3000 , 3000-3500 ) and modeled their amplitudes separately. Furthermore, the telluric correction algorithm was not able to fully correct the region around the line ( 11000-11600 ). This strongly affects any attempts to model the line and we decided against including an line model.

sources.summary: The paper finds J0100+2802 has dominant nuclear radio emission plus a real one-sided 70 kpc jet-like feature with a self-absorbed core and steep-spectrum jet-like structure.

quotes.quote: Results in radio band J0100+2802 shows, in images in the L and C band, a dominant nuclear emission with a faint jet-like emission in the northern direction. In the L-band image the structure has a surface brightness at the 1.5 level; however, the same structure is visible in the C-band image (see Fig. fig:0100-vla ) at a more than 3 level. Therefore, we conclude that it is a real one-sided jet emission. Its length is 12-14 arcsec, which corresponds to 70 kpc. The core is self-absorbed ( ^ 1.5 _ 5.0 = -0.31 0.1 ), and the jet-like structure shows a steep spectrum with = 1.18 0.15 (Table tab:radio-obs ).

sources.summary: The review identifies SDSS J0100+2802 as the most massive and most luminous known z>6 quasar, with an actively accreting massive black hole near Eddington luminosity.

quotes.quote: The two noteworthy record holders are ULAS J1120+0641 (J1120) and SDSS J0100+2802 (J0100), hosting the most distant ( z 7.1 , Mortlock11 ), and the most massive ( 1.2 10^ 10 M_ , Wu15 ) SMBHs ever observed respectively. In the right panel of Fig. fig:fig1 we show the bolometric luminosity as a function of the BH mass for the collection of z 6 quasars presented by Wu15 . The nuclei of these objects are actively accreting massive BHs, shining close to or above the Eddington luminosity (green dashed line). Colored points show three of the most interesting objects observed to date: the two record holders introduced above, J1120 (magenta triangle) and J0100 (blue square) and quasar SDSS J1148+5251 (red circle, hereafter J1148) which is one of the best studied quasar, discovered at z=6.4 Fan01 . As it can be seen from the figure, J0100 is the most luminous quasar known at z>6 , with bolometric luminosity L_ Bol =L_ Edd 4 10^ 14 L _ Wu15 , making it 4 times brighter than J1148 (red circle), and 7 times brighter than J1120 (magenta triangle).

sources.summary: The paper argues SDSS J0100+2802 has the largest z>6 proximity zone, implying intrinsic ionizing luminosity and ruling out large claimed gravitational-lensing magnifications.

quotes.quote: The hyperluminous z 6.33 quasar SDSS J0100+2802, believed to host a supermassive black hole of 10^ 10 M_ , has recently been claimed to be lensed by a factor of 450 , which would negate both its extreme luminosity and black hole mass. However, its Ly transparent proximity zone is the largest known at z>6 , suggesting an intrinsically extreme ionizing luminosity.
We then show that the large proximity zone of SDSS J0100+2802 rules out lensing magnifications of >4.9 at 95 probability, and conclusively rule out the proposed >100 scenario.

Recently, Fujimoto20 suggested that the brightest z>6 quasar known, SDSS J0100+2802 ( Wu15 ; henceforth J0100+2802) is strongly gravitationally lensed, based on re-analysis of high spatial resolution ALMA and HST imaging.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS 010013.02+280225.8 as an ultraluminous z=6.30 quasar whose black-hole growth aggravates early-Universe aging problems, then estimates J0100+2802 seed redshifts in coupled dark-energy models.

quotes.quote: Moreover, as stated in , the recent discovery of SDSS 010013.02+280225.8 (hereinafter ``J0100+2802''), an ultraluminous quasar at redshift z=6.30 , has aggravated the problem of supermassive black-hole growth and evolution in the early Universe .

Since in \ (with H_0=67.4 \ and _ m_0 =0.315 given by ), t(z=6.3) 873 Myr, not only is this age of quasar J0100+2802 inconsistent with the transition from the Dark Ages to the EoR, but also the quasar would have had to grow beyond the big bang. This is still true even if we are using the lower bounds of H_0=66.82 \ and _ m_0 =0.308 from , which result in t(z=6.3) 891 Myr .

Model 2 With the best-fitting parameters in this Model, we have: EoR ( 6 z 15 ), for OHD ( H(z) + BAO), from around 478.7 Myr (534.1 Myr) to 1.371 Gyr (1.462 Gyr), which lasts 892.4 Myr (927.8 Myr); The seed redshift of J0100+2802, for OHD ( H(z) + BAO), z_ seed 17.75 \ (16.46) ; If the first star formed at z 20 , then the age of the universe at that time would be, for OHD ( H(z) + BAO), 337.1 Myr (381.4 Myr); Old globular cluster M92 (NGC 6341) would appear at z 18.19 \ (18.98) with age of 14.0 Gyr for OHD ( H(z) + BAO) ; For OHD ( H(z) + BAO), 3.5-Gyr-old ratio galaxy 53W091 ( z 1.55 ) and 4-Gyr-old radio galaxy 53W069 ( z 1.43 ) are formed at z 5.95 \ (6.20) and z 6.85 \ (7.22) , respectively; For OHD ( H(z) + BAO), QSO APM 08279+5255 with age around 2.1 Gyr would have formed at z 92.16 \ (44.83) .

sources.summary: The paper presents ALMA observations of J0100+2802, the most luminous z>6 QSO, revealing extended emission, an interacting companion, a [CII] outflow, and gas-rich merger evidence.

quotes.quote: In this work we present ALMA band 3 and band 6 observations of the HYPERION QSO SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 (hereafter J0100+2802) at z_ CII =6.327 wang2019 . wu2015 estimated a bolometric luminosity of L_ bol =4.29 10^ 14 \ L_ and a BH mass of M_ BH =1.24 10^ 10 \ M_ for J0100+28, making it the most optically luminous QSO with the most massive SMBH known, at z>6 . James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) observations by eilers2022 have confirmed that this QSO is not lensed.

In this paper we present very high-sensitivity ALMA data of J0100+2802, revealing for the first time in this source clear velocity gradients indicative of the presence of an interactive companion and a [ CII ] outflow.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0100+2802 as a saturated hyperluminous quasar whose host is not detected by EIGER imaging, while prior ALMA work suggests a clumpy starburst host.

quotes.quote: J0100+2802 (z=6.31) is a hyperluminous quasar discovered by wu15 . Due to its high luminosity, J0100+2802 is saturated in all three bands in EIGER imaging, and we are not able to detect its host galaxy. ALMA observations have suggested that the host galaxy of J0100+2802 is a starburst galaxy with a star formation rate of 850 M_ / yr wang19b , which exhibit clumpy structures fujimoto20 . eiger3 reported the NIRCam observations and ground-based spectroscopy of J0100+2802, who showed that the images of this quasar are well-described by a point source, and the , , and emission lines give consistent estimated BH masses.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0100+2802 as an extremely luminous z>6 HYPERION QSO with a very massive SMBH and uncertain dust SED properties.

quotes.quote: For QSO SDSS J010013.02+280225.8 (hereafter J0100+2802) at z_ CII =6.327 wang2019 , wu2015 estimated a bolometric luminosity of L_ bol =4.29 10^ 14 \ L_ and a BH mass of M_ BH =1.24 10^ 10 \ M_ , making it the most optically luminous QSO with the most massive SMBH known at z>6 . Both measurements have been recently confirmed by JWST eilers2022 . wang2019 performed a multi-frequency analysis of the dust SED, but they could not obtain a precise determination of the dust properties, concluding that J0100+28 has either a high dust emissivity ( 2 ) or a high dust temperature ( T_ dust 60 K), or a combination of thereof.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0100+2802 as a super-luminous quasar whose field contains 180 emitting systems and a significant nearby galaxy overdensity, with a <2 cMpc density contrast of 63.

quotes.quote: Within the field around quasar J0100+2802 we now discover a total of 180 emitting galaxy systems ( 213 individual galaxies, see sec:grouping for details on how we define a group) compared to the previously reported 117 .
In close vicinity of the quasars within <2 cMpc we find density enhancements of (<2 cMpc )=63 21 and (<2 cMpc )=35 16 around the quasars J0100+2802 and J1148+5251, respectively.
The other two fields around the super-luminous quasar J0100+2802 and the quasar J1148+5251, show density contrasts of (<2 cMpc )=63 21 and (<2 cMpc )=35 16 , respectively.

gmw_00002230.name: Q1208+1011
gmw_00002230.overview: Q1208+1011 is a high-redshift quasar at a redshift of approximately 3.80 [arXiv:1712.01205, arXiv:2007.15657]. It is a gravitationally lensed system featuring two split images [arXiv:0801.1692], though some debate persists regarding whether it is a lens or a binary quasar [arXiv:2007.15657].

sources.summary: The paper treats Q1208+1011 as a gravitationally lensed quasar, applies a revised magnification factor, and obtains spectral parameters consistent with earlier Chandra analysis.

quotes.quote: Q1208+1011. --- This gravitationally lensed quasar was originally found by Hazard, McMahon, Sargent (1986). It comprises two split images separated by 0 47 with a brightness ratio of about 4, based on the observations with ground-based telescopes (Magain et al. 1992) and the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) (Bahcall et al. 1992b). Giallongo et al. (1999) derived a total magnification factor of _t=22 , which was later revised to _t = 3.1 by Barvainis Ivison (2002). We extracted the spectrum of this quasar by summing the counts from all images and then scaled down the luminosity according to the latter magnification factor listed above.

Dai et al. (2004) analyzed the \ data of this quasar and fitted the spectrum with a power law and a Gaussian emission line without intrinsic absorption. They found a best-fitting photon index of = 2.3 0.2 and derived value of = -1.62 . We repeated the reduction of the same \ data (Obs-ID: 3570 PI: Garmire). The spectrum was binned with a minimum of 10 counts per bin. The spectral parameters that we derive are consistent to those obtained by Dai et al. (2004).

sources.summary: The paper uses Q 1208+1011 as a missed high-redshift quasar lens, noting its brightness, redshift, quality flag issue, and optical colors near the stellar locus.

quotes.quote: (1) Q 1208+1011 This object is a known quasar lens Magain1992,Bahcall1992 ( m_ i =16.77 ) with a redshift of z=3.80 . One of the non-fatal quality flags is raised and it is not included in any of the high redshift inclusion regions. Finally, this Quasar is located too close to the stellar locus in optical color space (see Fig. fig_cc_missed ), to be selected by previous SDSS campaigns.

sources.summary: The paper cites Q1208+1011 as a debated z=3.8 quasar lens or binary; multiple images, nonidentical spectra, missing lens galaxy, and proximity-effect evidence favor lensing.

quotes.quote: The quasar Q1208+1011 at z 3.8 , once the highest redshift quasar known Hazard86 , showed indications of being strongly lensed via the detection of multiple images at small angular separation Magain92,Bahcall92img . While subsequent spectroscopic investigation showed that both sources were quasars at the same redshift, their broad emission lines were not perfectly identical Bahcall92spec , and no lensing galaxy has been conclusively identified Lehar00 , leaving the true nature of the source as a lingering question mark. There has been a long standing debate Mortlock99 about whether sources like Q1208+1011 are lenses vs. binary quasars Hennawi06,Hennawi10 , but Giallongo99 argued that the lensing hypothesis for Q1208+1011 is strongly preferred from the relative lack of proximity effect in its spectrum, demonstrating the utility of the proximity effect for identifying gravitational lenses.

gmw_00002231.name: J0542+4951
gmw_00002231.overview: J0542+4951 is a compact steep-spectrum quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.545 [arXiv:1211.1300]. The active galactic nucleus features an elongated radio structure spanning 400 milliarcseconds [arXiv:0904.0478, arXiv:astro-ph/9801020] and has exhibited multi-year variations in its radio flux density [arXiv:1211.1300].

sources.summary: The paper considers quasar 3C 147 as an ultrahigh-energy cosmic-ray source candidate, noting its luminosities, Faraday rotation, event-box coincidence, large redshift, and possible viability for S0 primaries.

quotes.quote: The quasar 3C 147 and the Seyfert galaxy MCG 8-11-11 are attractive candidates. Lying within the 1 error box of the primary's incoming direction, the quasar 3C 147 has a large radio luminosity ( 7.9 10^ 44 ^ 1 ) and an X-ray luminosity of about the same order of magnitude, indicative of a large number of strongly accelerated electrons in the region. It also produces a large Faraday rotation, with rotation measure RM = -1510 50 ^ 2 , indicative of a large magnetic field over large distances. It is noteworthy that this source is within the error box of a UHE event seen by the Yakutsk detector. However, 3C 147 lies at a red shift of about z=0.545 , well beyond z < 0.0125 adopted in Ref. \ elbert as the distance upper limit for the source of UHE proton primaries.

If UHE cosmic rays are S^0 s, we will show that their range is at least an order of magnitude greater than that of a proton, putting MCG 8-11-11 (and possibly even 3C 147) within range of the Fly's Eye event.

This would be necessary for a very distant source such as 3C 147 to be acceptable, since the required particle flux for the detected flux on Earth already pushes its luminosity limit. Assuming that the 3.2 10^ 20 event of Fly's Eye came during its exposure to 3C 147, the resulting time-averaged flux is 11 ^ 2 ^ 1 , which is greater than the X-ray luminosity of 3C 147 elbert .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C147 as a compact steep-spectrum quasar and VLA flux calibrator whose radio flux changed by repeated multi-year rises and drops before continuing to decline.

quotes.quote: 3C147 is a compact steep-spectrum quasar at redshift z = 0.545. VLBI imaging shows structure of maximum extent of 200 milliarcseconds ( Ross09 ). A VLA high resolution image at 43 GHz, made with high resolution data, is shown in the left panel of Fig. fig:3C147Maps .

The small angular size and high spectral flux density of 3C147 have resulted in it being commonly used as a flux density scale calibrator by the VLA. This source has undergone significant changes in flux density over time, as shown in the right panel of Fig. fig:3C147Maps . For all bands other than , a rise of 5 to 10 was seen between 1990 and 1998, again with the larger increases being seen at higher frequencies.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0542+4951 as very resolved at 327 MHz, with a 400 mas elongated structure, missing northeast MERLIN emission, and a southeast extension seen across frequencies.

quotes.quote: J0542+4951 (3C147) 3C147 is very resolved at 327 MHz and shows an elongated structure that spans 400 milliarcseconds, largely consistent with that observed by EVN-MERLIN at 1.6 GHz davisetal96 . The image is also in good agreement with higher resolution VLBA observations made at 8.5 GHz and 2.3 GHz fey&char00 . We do not detect the emission seen by MERLIN to the North-East of the peak emission, that davisetal96 interpret as evidence of outflow from a counter jet. There is als0 an extended structure to the South-East of the peak of the emission, which is also observed at 1.6 GHz with EVN-MERLIN and, 2.3/8.5 GHz (VLBA).

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 147 as an attractive AGN candidate source for clustered ultra-high-energy cosmic rays, but argues protons from its great distance are unlikely while S^0 particles could arrive with little energy loss.

quotes.quote: An even more attractive source is the AGN 3C 147, but its distance is at least 1200 Mpc .

It is evidently highly unlikely that the highest energy cosmic ray events can be due to protons from MCG 8-11-11, and even more unlikely that two or three high energy protons could penetrate such distances or originate from 3C 147.

A candidate astrophysical source, 3C 147, has been identified for the triplet containing two of the highest energy events. At well over a gigaparsec, it is near enough for S^0 's to have arrived with little energy loss.

gmw_00002232.name: J1331+3030
gmw_00002232.overview: J1331+3030 is a compact steep-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1211.1300, arXiv:1510.06763] at a redshift of z = 0.849 [arXiv:2005.11535] that has also been classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1705.07905, arXiv:2112.02632]. It features a parsec-scale, one-sided radio jet [arXiv:1611.01234] and exhibits highly stable linear polarization [arXiv:2212.08274], though its circular polarization is variable [arXiv:1709.08742].

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 286 as a compact steep-spectrum radio source newly classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 with a radio jet and gamma-ray detection.

quotes.quote: We present here another interesting case of CSS/HERG, that of 3C 286 (z = 0.85). This object is a well known CSS classified for the first time by Peacock82 . It is often used as a calibrator at centimeter wavelengths for both total flux and linear polarization.

It shows both the turnover in the radio spectrum around 300 MHz, a spectral index between 1.4 and 50 GHz of = -0.61 . It also exhibits the compact morphology typical of CSS: while it remains unresolved at kpc scales, a core-jet structure is visible on pc scale. The jet inclination has been estimated of 48 ^ with respect to the line of sight e.g., see and references therein An17 .

The H line, now visible, has a FWHM of (1811 169) km s ^ 1 , a ratio [ O III ] /H of 1.03 0.05, and Fe II multiplets are clearly visible in the spectrum (see Fig. fig:1 ). The corresponding uncertainties were calculated with a Monte Carlo method described by Berton16b . These parameters clearly indicate that 3C 286 can be classified as NLS1.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 286 as an AGN/quasar used as a stable EVPA reference, with long-stable flux and polarized flux and ALMA polarization measurements showing no time trend.

quotes.quote: Commonly, the AGN 3C 286 is considered as the source which best satisfies these three conditions Agudo+2012, Perley&Butler2013a, Perley&Butler2013b . 3C 286 is a quasar with an angular size of a few arcseconds at centimeter wavelengths Akujor+1995 . The flux density S_ from 1 to 50 GHz has been stable to within 1 over 30 years Perley&Butler2013a and exhibits a power-law spectrum with a spectral index 0.61 (defined by S_ ^ ) An+2017 . Likewise, the polarized flux is very stable, albeit showing a slow secular increase of less than 2 per century at any given frequency Perley&Butler2013b .

The EVPA and fractional polarization of 3C 286 measured with ALMA do not show any systematic trend with time (see Appendix sec:3c286 ). In addition, we found that the standard deviations of the EVPA are close to the mean of the measurement uncertainties at all four ALMA frequencies. This suggests that the EVPA of 3C 286 measured with ALMA is stable over the 3-year observing period at frequencies up to 346.5 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper selects 3C286 as an unusually stable compact steep-spectrum quasar calibrator, linking that stability to its resolved polarized structure and lack of optically thick nuclear emission.

quotes.quote: 3C286 is a compact steep-spectrum quasar at redshift z=0.846. High resolution VLA images show the source has a steep-spectrum extension of length 2.5 arcseconds to the SW, and a smaller knot of emission to the east, as shown in Fig. fig:3C286Maps . VLBI, EVN, and MERLIN images show a highly polarized linear structure of maximum 50 milliarcseconds extent ( Cot97b , Jia96 , Aku95 ).

The milliarcsecond structure of this source has some very unusual features. The VLBA imaging shows that the compact central structure shown in the figure is steep spectrum, resolved to the VLBA, and highly polarized throughout. There appears to be no optically thick central nuclear emission.

sources.summary: The paper investigates 3C 286 as a gamma-ray compact steep-spectrum quasar, identifying a one-sided parsec-scale jet, a radio core, and mildly relativistic motion.

quotes.quote: The quasar 3C 286 is one of two compact steep spectrum sources detected by the Fermi /LAT. Here, we investigate the radio properties of the parsec(pc)-scale jet and its (possible) association with the ray emission in 3C 286. The Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) images at various frequencies reveal a one-sided core--jet structure extending to the southwest at a projected distance of 1 kpc.

The pc-scale radio properties of the ray quasar 3C 286 are investigated in detail. The radio core is for the first time identified from its compact structure detected in high-frequency, high-resolution VLBI images and its inverted radio spectrum.
This preliminary proper motion measurement implies a mildly relativistic, subluminal jet moving at a moderate inclination angle.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 286 as a millimeter polarisation calibrator, confirming stable flux and linear polarisation while finding evidence for variable circular polarisation.

quotes.quote: Because of the known total flux density and polarisation stability of 3C 286 at centimetre wavelengths, also demonstrated in the short millimetre regime and this paper Agudo:2012p17464 , the target was used as a control source for our calibration. Figure 3c286 shows the time series of 3 and 1 mm total flux density ( S ), linear polarisation degree ( m_ L ), linear polarisation angle ( ), and circular polarisation ( m_ C ) of fully calibrated measurements of 3C 286 taken during the course of our program (see also Table t:3c286 where we give their weighted averages and standard deviations). These measurements confirm the results shown in Agudo:2012p17464 where we demonstrate the low level of variability of the source in S , m_L , and at both 3 and 1 mm, and therefore its goodness as total flux density and polarisation calibrator also at short millimetre wavelengths.

Closer inspection of the circular polarisation data at 3 and 1 mm, presented here for 3C 286, shows that the observed scatter is nearly twice as high as the median values over the entire sample as given at the end of Section ss:polcal . Four 3 mm observations and one at have signal--to--noise ratios 3 and therefore formally qualify as actual m_ C detections. Contrary to the other Stokes parameters, circular polarisation in this source does not appear to be stable.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 286 as a bright compact radio quasar example, measuring its 9.4 GHz flux and showing its near point-like core-jet structure gives near-zero closure phase.

quotes.quote: The first example employs observations of the radio quasar, 3C 286, which is a bright and highly compact object, often used for flux density and complex bandpass calibration at radio wavelengths. We employ the JVLA in its largest (`A') configuration, and selected three antennas from the array, corresponding to a triangle with spacings (baselines) of 12.4 km, 7.5 km, and 15.0 km projected on a plane perpendicular to the direction of the phase center (coincident with the position of 3C 286). The flux density of the target object, 3C 286, at the observing wavelength of =3.2 cm ( =9.4 GHz), measured on these antenna spacings is 4.4 Jy ( 1 Jy = 10^ 26 W m ^ 2 Hz ^ 1 ). 3C 286 is the dominant source of emission in the field of view. It has a compact core-jet structure, which on the spatial frequencies being considered herein essentially appears as an unresolved, point-like object .
The nearly point-like structure of 3C 286 implies a closure phase very close to zero, which further implies that the three fringe NPCs will intersect nearly at a point.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 286 as a high-redshift Fermi-listed source, originally a compact steep-spectrum radio source and later an NLSy1 from optical spectroscopy, with SDSS spectra used in the Fe II analysis.

quotes.quote: The ray detection supports the presence of a relativistic jet as the origin of the radio power for these sources komossaetal06 . They were selected because high-quality optical spectra were available for the three of them. The three ray sources are 1H 0323+342 ( B2 0321+33 1H 0323+342 J03246+341), 3C 286 ( J13311+305), and PKS 2004-447 ( J20079--445).

J13311 is a rather high redshift ( z 0.850 ) object, identified as a ray source in all Fermi catalogs (e.g., ackermannetal15 ). Originally it was classified as a compact steep-spectrum (CSS) source based on its radio morphology peacockwall82 , and later as a NLSy1 from optical spectroscopy bertonetal17,yaokomossa21 .

The spectra of these three sources were obtained using the Asiago 1.22m Telescope for J03246 (full description of the data reduction in foschinietal15 ), the SDSS archive for J13311, and the FORS2 instrument on the Very Large Telescope for J20079--445 bertonetal21 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 286 as a quasar calibrator: bright, unresolved, strongly polarized, near-zero RM, repeatedly observed, and modeled for total-intensity frequency dependence.

quotes.quote: The quasar 3C 286 is an excellent calibrator for complex gains and leakages since it is bright (12--17 Jy from 1--2 GHz), isolated, unresolved by the ATA, strongly linearly-polarized ( 10 ), and widely used as a flux standard. Furthermore, 3C 286 has RM 0 rad m ^ 2 r83 , which makes it very easy to identify frequency-dependent calibration problems. As a result, each observing period was scattered with repeated, on-axis observations of 3C 286. On day 1, 3C 286 was not up during the entire observation, so 3C 138 was used to extend the time- and antenna-dependent gain calibration in time; 3C 286 was always used exclusively for leakage and frequency-dependent gain calibration. The frequency dependence of total intensity of 3C 286 is based on the flux scale of b77 as parameterized by o94 .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 286 as a compact radio source/NLS1 with a steep spectrum, core-lobe structure, stable radio emission, high polarization, and resolved VLBI components.

quotes.quote: 3C 286 also named B1328+307, at redshift of z=0.849 (Cohen et al. 1977). The steep radio spectrum has a spectral index of =0.61 between 1.4 and 50 GHz and then a turnover at about 300 MHz, below which it is flat till 75 MHz (An et al. 2017). The source displays a primary core and a second lobe 2.6 arcsec to the south-west (Spencer et al. 1989; An et al. 2017). The radio emission at 15 GHz of this source is rather stable and no significant variation is observed in the past 10 years. The high polarization of the source has been detected in the radio band (Akujor Garrington 1995).

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C286 as a blazar associated with an H-ATLAS source, showing low-frequency excess, synchrotron-dominated emission, variability, and gamma-ray detection.

quotes.quote: A search of the NED yielded the identification of two H-ATLAS sources brighter than 35 mJy at 500 m (J120123.1 + 002830 in the equatorial fields and J133108.4 + 303034 in the NGP field) with two known blazars not in BZCAT (the second blazar is the core of the bright radio source 3C 286).

The low-frequency excess is particularly conspicuous in the cases of 3C286 (J133108.4+303034) and 3C287 (J133038.1+250901); its contribution at 1.4 GHz makes the spectral index between 1.4 and 5 GHz, conventionally used to discriminate between flat- and steep-spectrum sources, somewhat steeper than the canonical boundary of 0.5 . However, the data show that the synchrotron emission of these sources extends up to IR/optical wavelengths, as is the case for blazars, and straightforwardly account, in particular, for the sub-mm H-ATLAS flux densities. Further evidence of the blazar nature of these sources is the strong variability especially in the optical/UV and X-ray bands. Moreover, 3C286 was also detected in gamma-rays with a gamma-ray/synchrotron energy ratio in the blazar range FermiLAT2019_4LAC .

sources.summary: The paper discusses 3C 286 as an early quasar/radio source, comparing it with white-dwarf-like quasar colors and describing its optical emission as a mix of synchrotron and thermal components.

quotes.quote: Prior to this, these objects were found to be radio sources whose optical appearance was star-like instead of galaxy-like - the first such source to be identified was 3C 48 1961S&T...21..148M followed by 3C 196 and 3C 286 1962PASP...74R.406M,1963ApJ...138...30M .

We recall that 1963ApJ...138...30M had noted a similarity between the colours of old novae/white dwarfs and quasars (3C 48, 3C 196, 3C 286). This was supported by 1967ApJ...147.1219B who commented on the similarities in the absorption spectra of the white dwarf Hz 29 1957ApJ...126...14G and quasars.

For example, 1963ApJ...138...30M found that while they could extrapolate the optical flux for 3C 48 and 3C 196 from the radio, it was not possible to do so for 3C 286 - one way to understand this would be that the optical emission in 3C 48 and 3C 196 is dominated by synchrotron emission whereas in 3C 286, its a combination of synchrotron and thermal emission.

sources.summary: The paper reports J1331+3030 images consistent with higher-frequency VLBA data, extended in the MERLIN direction at 327 MHz, and lacking the reported counter jet.

quotes.quote: J1331+3030 (3C286) Our images of 3C286 are consistent with other VLBA images at higher frequencies e.g. . The 327 MHz observations are clearly extended in the same direction as that seen in the MERLIN images at 18 cm akuetal94 . We do not see the counter jet report by akuetal94 .

sources.summary: The catalog reports 3C 286 as a newly detected compact steep-spectrum source in 3LAC, included in the clean sample and having medium-symmetric-object morphology.

quotes.quote: Two new compact steep-spectrum (CSS) sources are detected: 3FGL J1330.5+3023 (3C 286) and 3FGL J0824.9+3916 (4C +39.23B). While both are CSS, the latter is a duplicate association (the other association being the FSRQ blazar 4C+39.23) so is not in the Clean Sample. The former has the morphology of a medium symmetric object (MSO), like that of the LAT-detected FSRQ 4C +55.17 mcc11 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 286 as a steep-spectrum radio quasar associated with a Fermi source and discusses its MSO/core-jet interpretation.

quotes.quote: 3C 286 and 3C 380 are bright steep spectrum radio quasars (SSRQ) at redshift z = 0.849 and z = 0.692, respectively. 3C 286, associated with the ray source 3FGL J1330.5 + 3023, has the morphology of a medium-sized symmetric object (MSO) (Fig. 3C286 ). Cotton et al. (1997) suggested that 3C 286 may be a core-jet source in which the relativistic core is beamed away from our line of sight and it is not detected, and what we see is the jet that curves through our line of sight.

gmw_00002233.name: J1746+6226
gmw_00002233.overview: The radio source J1746+6226 features an extended core component at 327 MHz [arXiv:0904.0478]. This low-frequency emission is accompanied by a counter-jet hot spot located approximately 0.2 arcseconds to the northeast, which is positioned diametrically opposite to the galaxy's higher-frequency jet [arXiv:0904.0478].

sources.summary: The paper finds J1746+6226 has an extended 327 MHz core and reveals a northeast counter-jet hot spot opposite the higher-frequency jet.

quotes.quote: J1746+6226 (4C +62.29) At 327 MHz, we observe an extended core component with a morphology that is consistent with higher frequency VLBA observations at 2.3 GHz and 8.6 GHz . However, our images also reveal a counter jet with a hot-spot located approximately 0.2 arcseconds to the North-East of the extended core. The PA of this counter jet feature is diametrically opposite to the higher frequency jet that is seen on the other side of the core.

gmw_00002234.name: J1829+4844
gmw_00002234.overview: J1829+4844 is a steep-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1510.06763] located at a redshift of z = 0.692 [arXiv:1712.06314, arXiv:2005.11535]. The source features a curved parsec-scale jet with superluminal motion [arXiv:2005.11535], a large diffuse radio halo [arXiv:1712.06314], and variable gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1801.09381].

sources.summary: Strong pileup required masking the core; the updated analysis agrees on spectral index with prior work but finds calibration and pileup handling explain earlier flux differences.

quotes.quote: 3C 380 : the strong pileup for this source led us to mask a central circle of radius 1.5 arcsec. Our result is in agreement with that found by prieto96 using ROSAT data ( =1.58 0.13 ) and consistent with the photon index recently published by marshall05 : =1.61 0.09 . However there is a discrepancy between our and their measure of the X-ray flux. We and marshall05 extracted the spectrum from the same area; we used slightly different background, but the background is negligible for such a high-flux source.

However, the big difference between the two studies is the calibration. Our newer analysis has taken into account the charge transfer inefficiency and the ARF is better determined. Moreover, without using a pileup model, Marshall et al. found a poor fit, with a spectrum that was inconsistently flatter than that in the current work, and consequently a lower 1-keV flux density. Marshall et al. attributed the flat spectrum and bad fit to pileup

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1829+4844 with 3C380, a powerful steep-spectrum FR II quasar, and finds that only its core and nearer-lobe hot spot appear at 327 MHz.

quotes.quote: J1829+4844 is identified with the very powerful steep spectrum radio source, 3C380. Observations at decimetre and centimetre wavelengths have identified this object as an FR II source that is elongated along the line-of-sight wilketal91,meganetal06 . This source has a very extended structure, with a number of compact components that corresponds to the core coincident with the optical quasar with a short (15 mas) jet, a hot-spot in the nearer lobe 0.7 arcseconds from the core, and another slightly larger hot-spot 0.4 arcseconds from the first meganetal06 . The lobes are located to the North-West of the core. Only the core and the hot-spot at 0.7 arcseconds from the core are visible in the VLBA observations at 327 MHz.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1828 + 487/3C 380 as a Fermi-detected compact steep-spectrum radio source with a large diffuse halo and extended MERLIN/VLBI jet structure.

quotes.quote: 1828 + 487 (J1829 + 4844) The radio source 1828 + 487 (3C 380) is at a redshift of z = 0.692 1996ApJS..107..541L . The Fermi /LAT identified the source as 3FGL J1829.6+4844 with a 24.1 detection significance in the 100 MeV--300 GeV energy range 2015ApJS..218...23A . The image made with the VLA in A+B-array at 1.6 GHz shows a large diffuse halo with a size of 6 surrounding a central compact component 1993MNRAS.264..298M . The source was mapped with the Multi-Element Radio Linked Interferometer Network (MERLIN) which detected structure up to an overall angular extent of about 6 (corresponding to a linear size of 50 kpc). The radio morphology suggests that the source belongs to the class of compact steep-spectrum (CSS) sources 1984Natur.308..619W,1985A&A...143..292F .

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 380 as a steep-spectrum compact radio source with superluminal motion, bent jet geometry, complex pc-scale structure, and FR II radio-galaxy interpretation.

quotes.quote: 3C 380 located at z=0.692 (TXS 1828+487, Wilkinson et al. 1991). It has a steep radio spectrum with =0.7 between 300 MHz and 5 GHz and the extent extends to 1 (Readhead Wilkinson 1980). The superluminal motion in the outer regions of the jet is observed and corresponds to an apparent velocity 8c , indicating that the outer part of the VLBI-scale jet is within 10^ of the line-of-sight. However, the absence of strong variability at radio and optical bands may imply an intrinsic bend near the core-jet, and the core-jet points 30^ away while the overall source axis is within 10^ of the line-of-sight (Wilkinson et al. 1991). Using the multi-epoch VLBI observations, Polatidis Wilkinson (1998) revealed a curved pc-scale jet with complex substructure and an apparent acceleration from the core to 100 pc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 380 as an SSRQ gamma-ray source whose emission reaches 300 GeV, shows possible high-energy deviation, and varies on 30-day and shorter adaptive bins.

quotes.quote: 3C 380: Along with 3C 207, it is the first SSRQ detected by in the band abdomisaligned . Its spectra can be described by PL with =2.43 0.03 detected with 36.2 and its emission extends up to 300 GeV (Fig. sed_all [ e ] ). We note an indication of deviation of the PL with respect to the data above 10s of GeV but the PLEC modeling with =2.36 0.05 and E_ =(55.57 50.74) GeV (Fig. sed_all (e) red color) is preferred over PL only at the level of 1.4 . Its emission is variable in 30-day scales but the adaptively binned light curve shows that sometimes the flux increases also in short periods: for example on MJD 55184.71 within 21.3 days the flux increased up to (8.45 1.37) 10^ 8 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 .

sources.summary: The catalog notes 3C 380 was first gamma-ray detected in 1LAC, is a steep-spectrum radio quasar in 3CRR, and has a flat-spectrum core with superluminal jet motion.

quotes.quote: The gamma-ray detections of 3C 207 and 3C 380 were first reported in 1LAC. They appear in the 3CRR catalog Lai83 by virtue of their bright low-frequency emission due to the presence of kpc-scale extended steep-spectrum radio lobes, thus are formally classified as steep-spectrum radio quasars (SSRQs). However, they contain pronounced flat-spectrum radio cores with superluminal motions measured in their parsec-scale jets, indicating that they are the most well-aligned sources to our line of sight amongst the SSRQs in the 3CRR e.g., Wil91,Hou13,Lis13 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 380 as a steep-spectrum radio quasar whose large complex radio structure and superluminal jet motion argue against a young-CSO classification.

quotes.quote: 3C 286 and 3C 380 are bright steep spectrum radio quasars (SSRQ) at redshift z = 0.849 and z = 0.692, respectively.

3C 380, associated with the ray source 3FGL J1829.6 + 4844, was part of the sample of powerful CSS objects selected by Fanti et al. (1990). However, its complex radio structure extending on a total linear size of 70 kpc (largely exceeding the optical host galaxy) (Fig. 3C380 ) and the detection of superluminal motion along the jet (Polatidis Wilkinson 1998) suggest that 3C 380 is a classical SSRQ, rather than a young CSO.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 380 as a highly variable quasar whose ISO and millimeter SED is dominated by synchrotron emission from a flat-spectrum radio core.

quotes.quote: For one of the quasars (3C 380) the ISO photometry and the mm flux can be fitted by a power-law ^ 0.75 which joins smoothly into the radio spectrum (near minimum) of this highly variable source. Obviously, the mid- to far-infrared SED is totally dominated by synchrotron emission from the radio core which exhibits the flattest radio spectrum in our sample. Note that the level of the synchrotron power-law is so high that even a luminous (10 ^ 11 L _ ) dust component could easily be hidden underneath.

gmw_00002235.name: J1902+3159
gmw_00002235.overview: J1902+3159 is a 17th magnitude quasar [arXiv:0904.0478]. It exhibits a complex core-jet radio structure, featuring a compact core and a hot-spot component separated by approximately 15 milliarcseconds [arXiv:0904.0478].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1902+3159 as the quasar 3C395, comparing its core-jet structure across frequencies and finding an additional faint extended component at 327 MHz.

quotes.quote: J1902+3159 (3C395) 3C395 is identified with a 17th magnitude quasar geld&whit94 , and has a complex radio structure on a wide range of angular scales. At 8.3 GHz with the VLBA its core-jet radio structure consists of two components (core and a hot-spot) separated by approximately 15 mas, with the hot-spot located South-West of the core laraetal97 . VLBI images at 326 MHz, taken by show a compact and unresolved core, and an extended regio of emission separated by approximately 65 mas South-West of the core. Our results agree well with these low resolution images. There is also a fainter, extended component observed in our image at P.A. 111.7 ^ .

gmw_00002236.name: 6C*0052+471
gmw_00002236.overview: 6C*0052+471 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=1.935 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127]. It exhibits strong, broad CIV and CIII] emission lines, weaker Lyman-alpha emission, and a decreasing continuum that suggests possible dust reddening [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127].

sources.summary: The paper classifies 6C*0052+471 as a z=1.935 quasar with broad CIV and CIII] emission, weaker Ly emission, and possible dust reddening.

quotes.quote: 6C*0052+471 Strong and broad CIV 1549 and CIII ] 1909 emission lines confirm this is a quasar at z=1.935 . There is also evidence for weaker Ly emission and a decreasing continuum blueward of the CIV emission. This may be due to light reddening of the quasar by dust (e.g.

gmw_00002237.name: 6C*0058+495
gmw_00002237.overview: 6C*0058+495 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.173 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127]. Its spectrum displays strong [OII] 3727 emission alongside weaker lines of CIII], CII], and MgII, as well as an unusually strong [SII] 4072 emission feature [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 6C*0058+495 as a z=1.173 radio galaxy, noting multiple emission lines and an unusually strong possible [SII] feature.

quotes.quote: 6C*0058+495 Strong [ OII ] 3727 emission along with weaker lines corresponding to CIII ] 1909 , CII ] 2326 and MgII 2799 identify this as a radio galaxy at z=1.173 . There is also a line which corresponds to [ SII ] 4072 which is not usually as strong as the line seen in our spectrum at the corresponding wavelength for this redshift. This emission line does not have any contamination from cosmic rays and is apparent in the 2-D spectra. Comparison of the [ OII ] and [ SII ] line fluxes for radio galaxies using the ratios of McCarthy (1993) gives a [ OII ] to [ SII ] ratio of 25:1, and the ratio in our spectrum is 5:2. Thus, if real, this feature is unusually strong for a radio galaxy spectrum.

gmw_00002238.name: 6C*0106+397
gmw_00002238.overview: 6C*0106+397 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.284 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127]. Its redshift was confirmed via three emission lines detected with the Keck telescope [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127].

sources.summary: The paper confirms 6C*0106+397 as a z=2.284 radio galaxy from three emission lines and distinguishes a nearby foreground galaxy from the radio source.

quotes.quote: 6C*0106+397 A blind spectrum was taken pointed at the mid-point of the hotspots with LRIS on the Keck telescope and three definite emission lines confirm this as a radio galaxy at z=2.284 . Emission-line fluxes are estimated using a spectrophotometric standard taken on a different night, so the emission-line luminosities may be unreliable. The limited wavelength coverage of the LRIS spectrum means that we have no data on the Ly line. There is also a bright object ( K = 17.8 in an 8 arcsec aperture) 5 arcsec to the south-east of the radio galaxy ID which was aligned with our slit. A strong emission line at 6084 which we associate with [ OII ] 3727 , and a blue continuum at the position of this object, lead us to conclude that it is a foreground galaxy at z = 0.632 and is not associated with the radio emission.

gmw_00002239.name: 6C*0135+313
gmw_00002239.overview: 6C*0135+313 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.199 [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127]. The galaxy features a small projected radio emission size of approximately 15 kiloparsecs and exhibits split Lyman-alpha emission, which may be caused by associated neutral hydrogen absorption [arXiv:astro-ph/0106127].

sources.summary: The paper describes 6C*0135+313 as a compact z=2.199 radio galaxy with split Ly emission, possible associated H I absorption, and weak CIV and HeII lines.

quotes.quote: 6C*0135+313 z=2.199 radio galaxy with strong Ly and CIII ] 1909 . The Ly emission is split into two peaks at 3893 and 3909 with a trough between them. This is possibly due to H I absorption associated with the host galaxy. The projected linear size of the radio emission is small ( D 15 kpc), consistent with the results of van Ojik et al. (1997) in which small ( < 50 kpc) radio sources are more likely to exhibit associated H I absorption. The CIV 1549 and HeII 1640 lines have FWHM < 10 and could be spurious.

gmw_00002240.name: SL2SJ020833-071414
gmw_00002240.overview: SL2SJ020833-071414 is a grade B strong gravitational lens system [arXiv:1307.4764]. Its lensed features include a double image of a bright compact component and a low-surface-brightness ring, which likely contains an unresolved active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper describes SL2SJ020833-071414 as a grade B lens with a double compact image, low-surface-brightness ring, and modeling difficulties caused by an unresolved AGN-like source component.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ020833-071414. The lensed features of this system consist of a double image of a bright compact component and a low surface brightness ring. The model cannot fully reproduce the image of the bright double but this is probably due to the presence of a compact unresolved component, like an AGN. AGNs in the source plane are difficult to model with a pixelized reconstruction technique, because the image regularization process smoothes our model images. This effect is present in other lenses with sharp peaks in the source surface brightness distribution. Since the signal-to-noise ratio of the \ image is low and no additional information comes from spectroscopy, this lens is given a grade B.

gmw_00002241.name: SL2SJ021325-074355
gmw_00002241.overview: SL2SJ021325-074355 is a high-redshift lens galaxy located at a redshift of 0.717 [arXiv:1307.4764]. It lenses a background source that features two distinct bright components, which are perturbed by a nearby foreground massive elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper models SL2SJ021325-074355 as a high-redshift lens whose two-component source image is perturbed by a nearby foreground massive elliptical galaxy.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ021325-074355. The source lensed by this high redshift galaxy ( z_d = 0.717 ) appears to have two separate bright components. Our source reconstruction confirms this picture. There is a massive elliptical galaxy in the foreground that may be providing extra deflection to the light coming from the source, thus perturbing the image. This perturber is very close to the observer ( z=0.0161 , from SDSS) and therefore its lensing power is greatly reduced with respect to the main deflector. We model the mass distribution from this galaxy with an additional SIE with centroid and PA fixed to the light distribution and R_ Ein and q as free parameters.

gmw_00002242.name: SL2SJ021411-040502
gmw_00002242.overview: SL2SJ021411-040502 is a gravitational lens system [arXiv:1307.4764]. The system lenses a source containing two bright components, with one component forming a large arc and its counter-image, while the second component forms a double image with smaller magnification [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper says SL2SJ021411-040502 lenses a two-component source and that the adopted pixelized reconstruction favors a different Einstein-radius solution from Paper I.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ021411-040502. The source has two bright components, one of which is lensed into the big arc and its fainter couter-image. The second component forms a double of smaller magnification. This lens was modeled in Paper I where we explained how there are two lens models, with different Einstein radii, that match the image configuration. The pixelized source reconstruction technique adopted here to model the lens favors the solution alternative to the one chosen in Paper I.

gmw_00002243.name: SL2SJ021737-051329
gmw_00002243.overview: SL2SJ021737-051329 is a strong gravitational lens system in a cusp configuration [arXiv:1307.4764, arXiv:2404.18897]. Modeling of the system requires either a mass centroid offset from the light center or a large external shear to match the curvature of its large arc [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper describes SL2SJ021737-051329 as a cusp-configuration lens requiring either a shifted mass centroid or substantial external shear to match the arc curvature.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ021737-051329. This lens system is in a cusp configuration, meaning that the source lies just within one of the four cusps of the astroid caustic of the lens. Either a mass centroid offset from the light center or a large shear is required to match the curvature of the big arc. This was also needed in Paper I and previously found by Tu++09 . Here, we find the amount of external shear to be _ ext = 0.11 0.01

sources.summary: The paper reports that models of HSCJ021737-051329 are visually similar, agree on position angle, but show disagreement in Einstein radius and especially axis ratio.

quotes.quote: HSCJ021737-051329 : The best-fit models of this lens by YL, S19 and S23 (see their fig. B.5) are similar and agree well on _ E . Our _ E shows deviation from other methods. The PA is robustly constrained as all the methods give strongly consistent predictions.
