The predicted axis ratios are comparable for S19 and YL but the other two methods are not consistent with this, making q the least well-constrained parameter despite the apparent similarity in their mass models.

gmw_00002244.name: SL2SJ022346-053418
gmw_00002244.overview: SL2SJ022346-053418 is a grade B strong gravitational lens system [arXiv:1307.4764]. The system features an extended arc with a higher redshift than the lens, while its potential counter-image is either barely visible [arXiv:2404.18897] or shows a different color and lacks spectroscopic detection [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper treats SL2SJ022346-053418 as a grade B system, using only the extended arc because the possible counter-image has different color and lacks spectroscopic detection.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ022346-053418. The CFHT image shows an extended arc and a bright knot at the opposite side with respect to the lens. Although this latter component might be the counter-image to the arc, its color is different and it is not detected spectroscopically. Therefore only the arc is used for the lensing analysis. The main arc has a higher redshift than the lens, however the lens model is not definitive in assessing whether this system is a strong lens. This is therefore a grade B lens.

sources.summary: The paper treats HSCJ022346-053418 as a lens with a barely visible counterimage, making model degeneracies likely, while most methods agree approximately on lens parameters.

quotes.quote: HSCJ022346-053418 : Since the counter image of the arc, if any, is barely visible in this lens, we expect that the degeneracies in various models will become more apparent here. The model images of YL, S19 and S23 (see their fig. B.6) are visually similar. The model parameters _ E and q , for all four methods, are roughly consistent within the quoted errorbars. The PAs from S19, S23 and network are also approximately consistent within the errorbars, while the PA from YL deviates from this.

gmw_00002245.name: SL2SJ084934-043352
gmw_00002245.overview: SL2SJ084934-043352 is a strong gravitational lensing system [arXiv:1307.4764]. The system is classified as a grade B lens because it lacks a spectroscopic detection of its background source and displays only a single visible arc [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper assigns SL2SJ084934-043352 grade B, noting a single visible arc, a fixed mass-position angle requirement, and no spectroscopic background-source detection.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ084934-043352. Only one arc is visible in the CFHT image. In order to obtain a meaningful lens model we need to fix the PA of the mass profile to that of the light. This system is a grade B due to the lack of spectroscopic detection of the background source.

gmw_00002246.name: SL2SJ085019-034710
gmw_00002246.overview: SL2SJ085019-034710 is a disk galaxy that acts as a strong gravitational lens [arXiv:1307.4764]. Its lensing effect produces a bright arc, though a predicted counter-image remains undetected and may be obscured by the disk or dust extinction [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper describes SL2SJ085019-034710 as a disk-galaxy lens producing a bright arc, with an undetected predicted counter-image possibly hidden by the disk or extinction.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ085019-034710. The CFHT image shows a bright arc produced by the lensing effect of a disk galaxy. The lens model predicts the presence of a counter-image opposite to the arc, but it is not bright enough to be distinguished from the disk of the lens. In addition, such a counterimage could suffer from substantial extinction.

gmw_00002247.name: SL2SJ085559-040917
gmw_00002247.overview: SL2SJ085559-040917 is a strong gravitational lensing system [arXiv:1307.4764]. The system features a clear, main blue arc at a redshift of 2.95 [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper assigns SL2SJ085559-040917 grade B because its redshift-2.95 main blue arc is clear but other faint blue features do not yet define an unambiguous lens configuration.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ085559-040917. The main blue arc of this system is at redshift 2.95. The other blue features seen in CFHT data however are too faint for us to establish an unambiguous interpretation of the lens configuration. Therefore we conservatively assign grade B to this system. Higher resolution photometry is needed to confirm this lens.

gmw_00002248.name: SL2SJ090106-025906
gmw_00002248.overview: SL2SJ090106-025906 is a galaxy system classified as a grade A strong gravitational lens [arXiv:1307.4764]. Its lens model is convincing, and the background source has been spectroscopically detected [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SL2SJ090106-025906 as grade A, despite a masked cosmic ray, because the lens model is convincing and the background source is spectroscopically detected.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ090106-025906. The WFPC2 image of this system is contaminated with a cosmic ray, which has been masked out in our analysis. Our lens model predicts an image at the position of the cosmic ray, the presence of which cannot be verified with our data. The model however appears to be convincing and the background source is spectroscopically detected, therefore this is a grade A lens.

gmw_00002249.name: SL2SJ095921+020638
gmw_00002249.overview: SL2SJ095921+020638 is a strong gravitational lensing system located in the COSMOS survey [arXiv:1307.4764]. The system features a lensed source galaxy with a redshift of 3.35 and an estimated Einstein radius of 0.74 arcseconds [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper revisits SL2SJ095921+020638 with XSHOOTER data, finding a slightly higher source redshift and an Einstein radius close to a previous model.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ095921+020638. This system, belonging to the COSMOS survey had previously been modeled by Ang++09 . These authors report a source redshift of 3.14 0.05 whereas we find a slightly greater value of 3.35 0.01 based on our own XSHOOTER data (Paper IV). They report an Einstein radius 0 71 in close agreement with our 0 74 0 04 estimate.

gmw_00002250.name: SL2SJ140123+555705
gmw_00002250.overview: SL2SJ140123+555705 is a strong gravitational lensing system characterized by a cusp-like configuration [arXiv:1307.4764]. High-resolution imaging reveals three distinct images of a single bright knot on its arc, though its counter-image remains too faint to be detected [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper models SL2SJ140123+555705 as a cusp-like system where new WFC3 data reveal arc detail and produce an Einstein radius inconsistent with Paper I.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ140123+555705 is a cusp-like system: three images of a single bright knot can be identified on the arc. The counter-image however is too faint to be detected in the WFC3 snapshot. This lens was already modeled in Paper I. The Einstein radius that we obtain here is not consistent with the value reported then. This is because the current model is obtained by analyzing newly obtained WPC3 data, which reveal more details on the arc.

gmw_00002251.name: SL2SJ140533+550231
gmw_00002251.overview: SL2SJ140533+550231 is a strong gravitational lensing system containing two lens galaxies of comparable brightness [arXiv:1307.4764]. The lensed image of this system displays four images of a bright knot [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper describes SL2SJ140533+550231 as a two-lens-galaxy system whose four knot images require two SIE components with degenerate Einstein radii.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ140533+550231. This is a particular system, in that there are two lens galaxies of comparable brightness. The lensed image shows four images of a bright knot. We model the system with two SIE components, centered in correspondence with the two light components. Our inference shows a substantial degeneracy between the Einstein radii of the two lenses.

gmw_00002252.name: SL2SJ140546+524311
gmw_00002252.overview: SL2SJ140546+524311 is a strong gravitational lensing system [arXiv:1307.4764]. It features a quadruply imaged bright compact component, which requires a relatively large shear to match the counter-image opposite the arcs [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper describes SL2SJ140546+524311 as a system with a quadruply imaged compact component and a relatively large shear needed to match the counter-image.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ140546+524311. This system shows a quadruply-imaged bright compact component. Two of the images are almost merged. A relatively large shear is required to match the position and shape of the counter-image opposite to the arcs.

gmw_00002253.name: SL2SJ140614+520253
gmw_00002253.overview: SL2SJ140614+520253 is a candidate strong gravitational lensing system [arXiv:1307.4764]. The system features a bright extended arc and several blue blobs, though its interpretation as a lens remains complex [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper keeps SL2SJ140614+520253 as grade B because the CFHT blue blobs lack a common-source model and only the bright extended arc is modeled.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ140614+520253. A few different blue blobs are visible in the CFHT image, but there is no working lens model that can associate them with the same source. As done in Paper I, we model only the bright extended arc. The resulting Einstein radius differs from the value of Paper I. This reflects the fact that the interpretation of this system as a lens is not straightforward. This is Grade B until future \ data shed more light on the actual nature of this system.

gmw_00002254.name: SL2SJ141137+565119
gmw_00002254.overview: SL2SJ141137+565119 is a strong gravitational lensing system that features a complete Einstein ring [arXiv:1307.4764]. The lensed source galaxy has a redshift of z = 1.420 [arXiv:1202.3852] and contains a bright northern knot that may indicate local substructure [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper describes SL2SJ141137+565119 as the only WFC3-imaged system in the sample, with tight constraints on its lens potential and source properties.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ141137+565119 is the only system having WFC3 imaging presented here. The signal-to-noise ratio is good and allows us to get tight constraints on the potential and on the source whose redshift is found to be z_ s =1.420 . The source half-light radius in the F475X band is R_ eff,s = 0 0.058 0 001 0.490 0.008 and its magnitude is m_ s =25.86 0.04 . We note that the addition of a very compact core would improve the fit further as our single source component does not reproduce the full complexity of the source light distribution.

sources.summary: The paper describes SL2SJ141137+565119 as a complete ring whose model misses flux in a bright northern knot, possibly because of substructure near an unresolved knot.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ141137+565119 shows a complete ring. Our lens model cannot account for all the flux in one bright knot on the arc, North of the lens. This could be the result of the presence of substructure close to the highly magnified unresolved knot that requires a minute knowledge of the PSF.

gmw_00002255.name: SL2SJ142003+523137
gmw_00002255.overview: SL2SJ142003+523137 is a disk galaxy that produces a lensed gravitational arc [arXiv:1307.4764]. The reconstructed source of this lens is compact, while its predicted counter-image is too faint to be detected and may be affected by extinction [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SL2SJ142003+523137 as a disk galaxy producing a lensed arc, with a compact source and an undetected possibly extincted counter-image.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ142003+523137. This disk galaxy is producing a lensed arc. The reconstructed source is compact and difficult to resolve. The predicted counter-image of the arc is too faint to be detected and possibly affected by extinction.

gmw_00002256.name: SL2SJ220329+020518
gmw_00002256.overview: SL2SJ220329+020518 is a strong gravitational lensing system that features a bright arc [arXiv:1307.4764]. It also contains a possible counter-image close to its center, which may instead be a blue substructure associated with the lens [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper models SL2SJ220329+020518 using only the bright arc because the possible central counter-image may instead be lens-associated substructure.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ220329+020518. This system shows a bright arc, and a possible counter-image close to the center. However, we are not able to fit both the light from the arc and the candidate counter-image. On the other hand, our spectroscopic analysis reveals OII emission at the redshift of the lens (Paper IV), which suggests that the blue bright spot close to the center might be a substructure associated with the lens and not the source. We model the system using light from the arc only. Our model predicts the existence of a faint counter-image that cannot be ruled out by our snapshot observation.

gmw_00002257.name: SL2SJ220629+005728
gmw_00002257.overview: SL2SJ220629+005728 is a strong gravitational lensing system [arXiv:1307.4764]. It features a secondary lens component near one of its arcs that has a color similar to the main lens [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper models a secondary component near one arc of SL2SJ220629+005728 as a singular isothermal sphere and finds a small Einstein radius contribution.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ220629+005728. The image shows a secondary component with a color similar to the main lens, in the proximity of one of the arcs. This component could contribute to the overall lensing effect. We modeled it with a singular isothermal sphere. The fit yielded a small value for its Einstein radius as in Paper I.

gmw_00002258.name: SL2SJ221326-000946
gmw_00002258.overview: SL2SJ221326-000946 is a disky lens galaxy [arXiv:1307.4764]. Its gravitational lensing features include a merging pair and a third image of the same bright component on the arc, though no counter-image is visible [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper describes SL2SJ221326-000946 as a disky lens with a merging pair and third image on the arc, but no visible counter-image.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ221326-000946 is a disky lens. A merging pair and a third image of the same bright component are identified on the arc. No counter-image is visible in our images.

gmw_00002259.name: SL2SJ221929-001743
gmw_00002259.overview: SL2SJ221929-001743 is a galaxy that acts as a strong gravitational lens [arXiv:1307.4764]. It has a single visible source component at a spectroscopic redshift of z=1.02 [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper assigns SL2SJ221929-001743 grade B because the single visible source component poorly constrains the model and the best-fit model lacks multiple images.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ221929-001743. Only one source component, at a spectroscopic redshift of z=1.02 , is visible in the CFHT image. The constraints that this image provides on the lens model are not good enough and we need to fix the position angle of the mass to that of the light. The best-fit model does not predict multiple images.

gmw_00002260.name: SL2SJ222012+010606
gmw_00002260.overview: SL2SJ222012+010606 is a strong gravitational lensing system featuring a foreground lens galaxy and a brighter blue arc at a higher redshift [arXiv:1307.4764]. A secondary blue component is also visible, though it is unlikely to be a counter-image of the main arc [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper considers SL2SJ222012+010606 a grade C system where low stellar mass and velocity dispersion make the secondary blue component unlikely to be a counter-image.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ222012+010606. The CFHT image shows two blue components on opposite sides of the lens. The brighter arc is measured to be at a higher redshift than the lens, while we have no spectroscopic information on the fainter blob. The lens model that we obtain is only partly satisfactory, because it predicts a mass centroid off by 1.5 pixels from the light centroid. Moreover, the stellar mass and velocity dispersion of the foreground galaxy are unusually low in relation to the measured Einstein radius. It seems then plausible that the secondary source component is not a counter-image to the main arc.

gmw_00002261.name: SL2SJ222217+001202
gmw_00002261.overview: SL2SJ222217+001202 is a strong gravitational lens system [arXiv:1307.4764]. Ground-based imaging of the system reveals a visible arc with no clear counter-image [arXiv:1307.4764].

sources.summary: The paper models SL2SJ222217+001202 as a ground-based lens with an arc but no clear counter-image, using a Gaussian prior on the lens position angle.

quotes.quote: SL2SJ222217+001202. An arc with no clear counter-image is visible in the ground-based image of this lens. We put a Gaussian prior on the lens PA, centered on the light PA and with a 10 degree dispersion, in order to obtain a meaningful model of this lens. Grade B.

gmw_00002262.name: VCC1491
gmw_00002262.overview: VCC1491 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the Virgo cluster near M87 [arXiv:1411.5513]. It is a spectroscopically passive galaxy [arXiv:1411.5513] characterized by a red color [arXiv:1411.5513, arXiv:astro-ph/0104122] and a shallow red gradient toward its center [arXiv:astro-ph/0104122].

sources.summary: The review presents VCC1491 as a Virgo dwarf elliptical near M87, morphologically similar to VCC1499 but redder and spectroscopically passive.

quotes.quote: Two such cases are offered by VCC1491 and VCC1499 reproduced in Fig. e1499 . They are located within 1^ projected distance from M87 in the Virgo cluster. Seen on the exquisite B-band photographic plate taken by Binggeli et al. (1985) for the construction of the Virgo Cluster Catalog (VCC) they look morphologically identical and they were both classified as dEs.

In spite of their similar stellar mass [ M_ star (1491) = 10^ 8.62 M _ , M_ star (1499) = 10^ 8.23 M _ ] , when seen on multiband CCD images they appear dramatically different in color: (g-i)_ 1491 =1.06; (g-i)_ 1499 =0.59 , and spectroscopically (see Fig. e1499 , right panel). While VCC1491 has a spectrum typical of a passive galaxy, VCC1499 has a post-star-burst (PSB) spectrum (both spectra are integrated over the whole galaxy). Unfortunately for neither galaxies kinematical measurements are available, but we would not be surprised if VCC1499 was a fast rotator (Cappellari et al.

sources.summary: The paper uses VCC1491 as a contrasting dwarf elliptical example, describing it as red, photometrically similar in V magnitude to VCC1499, and having a shallow red central gradient.

quotes.quote: An illustrative and meaningful example of the wide range spanned in color and color gradient by dEs is offered by the two galaxies VCC1491 and 1499 which happen to lie 1.5 arcmin apart in the same frame (see Fig. 7). The two have V mag differing by 0.01 mag, thus they are indistinguishable galaxies in all respects. However VCC1491 is almost as red (B-V=0.82, B-H=3.42) as a giant elliptical, while on the opposite VCC1499 (dE-pec) is almost as blue (B-V=0.49, B-H=2.67) as a typical dI. Moreover VCC1499 shows a strong blue central excess, while VCC1491 presents a shallower red gradient toward its centre (see Fig. gradient and profiles in Fig. 10).

gmw_00002263.name: VCC1499
gmw_00002263.overview: VCC1499 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1411.5513] classified as peculiar [arXiv:astro-ph/0104122] and located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1411.5513]. It exhibits a blue color [arXiv:1411.5513], a strong blue central excess [arXiv:astro-ph/0104122], and a post-starburst spectrum with strong Balmer absorption lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0104122].

sources.summary: The review presents VCC1499 as a Virgo dwarf elliptical near M87 with blue color, a post-starburst spectrum, and a likely recent late-to-early-type transformation.

quotes.quote: Two such cases are offered by VCC1491 and VCC1499 reproduced in Fig. e1499 . They are located within 1^ projected distance from M87 in the Virgo cluster. Seen on the exquisite B-band photographic plate taken by Binggeli et al. (1985) for the construction of the Virgo Cluster Catalog (VCC) they look morphologically identical and they were both classified as dEs.

In spite of their similar stellar mass [ M_ star (1491) = 10^ 8.62 M _ , M_ star (1499) = 10^ 8.23 M _ ] , when seen on multiband CCD images they appear dramatically different in color: (g-i)_ 1491 =1.06; (g-i)_ 1499 =0.59 , and spectroscopically (see Fig. e1499 , right panel). While VCC1491 has a spectrum typical of a passive galaxy, VCC1499 has a post-star-burst (PSB) spectrum (both spectra are integrated over the whole galaxy). Unfortunately for neither galaxies kinematical measurements are available, but we would not be surprised if VCC1499 was a fast rotator (Cappellari et al.

sources.summary: The paper describes VCC1499 as a blue dE-pec galaxy with strong blue central excess, Balmer absorption, and post-starburst activity consistent with an E+A-like history.

quotes.quote: However VCC1491 is almost as red (B-V=0.82, B-H=3.42) as a giant elliptical, while on the opposite VCC1499 (dE-pec) is almost as blue (B-V=0.49, B-H=2.67) as a typical dI. Moreover VCC1499 shows a strong blue central excess, while VCC1491 presents a shallower red gradient toward its centre (see Fig. gradient and profiles in Fig. 10). Using the Carelec spectrograph (Lemaitre et al. 1990) attached to the OHP 1.93m telescope we obtained in february 2000 long slit spectra for the two objects, shown in Fig. spectra . The spectral signatures of the two galaxies are significantly different: 1491 resembles a typical dE galaxy, while 1499 has a much bluer continuum and strong Balmer absorption lines (E.W. H_ 8 ) typical of E+A galaxies which have experienced an intense burst of star formation ended about 1-2 Gyrs ago (Poggianti Barbaro, 1996).
This is supported by the evidence of post-starburst activity found in the dE-pec VCC1499.

gmw_00002264.name: IC 3418
gmw_00002264.overview: IC 3418 is a low-surface-brightness dwarf irregular galaxy in the Virgo Cluster [arXiv:1006.5746, arXiv:1304.2560]. Ram pressure stripping has completely stripped the galaxy of its atomic gas [arXiv:1811.03647], quenching star formation in its main body [arXiv:1305.7113, arXiv:1811.03647] and leaving a 17-kiloparsec star-forming tail [arXiv:1011.1665, arXiv:1304.2560].

sources.summary: The paper describes VCC1217 / IC3418 as a dwarf irregular in Virgo whose gas stripping produced blue knots, filaments, truncated star formation, and a 17 kpc wake of young stellar blobs.

quotes.quote: A complex of H emitting blobs with strong FUV excess is associated to the dIrr galaxy VCC1217 / IC3418 (Hester et al. 2010), and extends up to 17 Kpc in the South-East direction. These outstanding features can be morphologically divided into diffuse filaments and compact knots, where most of the star formation activity traced by H takes place. We investigate the properties of the galaxy and the blobs using a multiwavelength approach in order to constrain their origin. We collect publicly available data in UV and H and observe the scene in the optical U,g,,i bands with LBT. The photometric data allows to evaluate the star formation rate and to perform a SED fitting separately of the galaxy and the blobs in order to constrain their stellar population age.

Several aspects of our analysis indicate that the ram pressure stripping picture is the most favorable one (in agreement with Hester and al. 2010), suggesting that VCC1217 has been recently stripped by the interaction with the Virgo Cluster IGM. The color profiles, the spectroscopy and SED fitting of the galaxy all support the scenario consisting in a truncation of the star formation in the last few hundreds Myr. The ensemble of blue knots and filaments stretching more than 17 Kpc South East of the galaxy is a remarkable feature.

sources.summary: The paper presents IC3418 as a ram-pressure stripped Virgo dwarf with severe molecular-gas deficiency, a possible surviving nuclear reservoir, and dense-gas limits in its UV/H gas-stripped tail.

quotes.quote: We present IRAM 30m sensitive upper limits on CO emission in the ram pressure stripped dwarf Virgo galaxy IC3418 and in a few positions covering H ii regions in its prominent 17 kpc UV/H gas-stripped tail. In the central few arcseconds of the galaxy, we report a possible marginal detection of about 1 10^6 M_ of molecular gas (assuming a Galactic CO-to-H _2 conversion factor) that could correspond to a surviving nuclear gas reservoir. We estimate that there is less molecular gas in the main body of IC3418, by at least a factor of 20, than would be expected from the pre-quenching UV-based star formation rate assuming the typical gas depletion timescale of 2 Gyr. Given the lack of star formation in the main body, we think the H _2 deficiency is real, although some of it may also arise from a higher CO-to-H _2 factor typical in low-metallicity, low-mass galaxies. The presence of H ii regions in the tail of IC3418 suggests that there must be some dense gas; however, only upper limits of < 1 10^6 M_ were found in the three observed points in the outer tail.

The galaxy IC3418 (VCC1217) is a peculiar dwarf galaxy located very close to the cluster core, with a projected distance of only 1 ( 290 kpc) from M87 (see Fig. FigVirgoA ), and a line-of-sight velocity with respect to the cluster mean of about -1000 km s ^ 1 . It is very likely on its first orbit through the Virgo center, and probably close to its nearest approach to M87. No H i was detected anywhere in the galaxy down to a 3 limit of 8 10^6 M_ per beam chung2009 ; in the main body of the galaxy there is also no H emission hester2010 .

sources.summary: IC 3418 is used as a dwarf-galaxy comparison whose fireball tail contains young-star trails, outer gas heads, HI/HII regions, and a more advanced stripping stage.

quotes.quote: The stellar upturn with gas and star formation at its head is morphologically similar to the ``fireball" structures observed trailing the Virgo cluster dwarf galaxy IC 3418 kenney14 and likely has a similar origin. The stripped gas has progressed downwind of the galaxy, leaving behind a trail of newly-formed stars that decouple from the accelerated ISM. A linear structure of young stars is created as the gas cloud continues to be accelerated by ram pressure, leaving no gas between the newly-formed stars and the galaxy. This type of structure, with an outer gas head and a tail of stars extending toward the galaxy, is seen on both large and small spatial scales in the tail of IC 3418. In the IC 3418 fireball structures, the newly-formed stars are visible in the UV and optical, and there is a small amount of HI coincident with several HII regions at the outer end of the tail, nearly 17 kpc from the main body of the galaxy.

There are good reasons that the stripping process may not be identical for the two galaxies, since the dwarf galaxy IC 3418 is less massive and in projection is located in a denser part of the cluster, near M87 (277 kpc; McLaughlin99 ), where ram pressure would be stronger. The main body of IC 3418 appears to be completely stripped, while there is still significant gas remaining in the disk of NGC 4522. Although IC 3418 is clearly at a later evolutionary stage of stripping than NGC 4522, the two galaxies' tails probably formed in similar ways.

sources.summary: The paper describes VCC 1217 as a disturbed Virgo dwarf star-forming galaxy near VCC 1183, but argues its fireball tail is more consistent with ram-pressure stripping than interaction.

quotes.quote: VCC 1183 VCC 1183 is in close projection to the most disturbed dwarf star-forming galaxy in Virgo, VCC 1217 Hester10 , as seen in Figure colormap . This Figure shows a deep ultraviolet (UV) color map that combines the far UV and near UV channels of GALEX taken by the GUViCS collaboration GALEX Ultra Violet Virgo Cluster Survey, Boselli11 . Despite their apparent projected proximity ( 22 kpc apart), their radial velocities 1300.9 0.5 km s ^ 1 versus 176 km s ^ 1 , respectively --- this work; Kenney13 suggest that they are unbound.

This timescale is neither consistent with the luminosity-weighted age of the stellar populations of the KDC (2 Gyr) nor with the age of the stellar populations of the tail of star-forming clumps (fireballs) observed in VCC 1217, which has been dated to be younger than 400 Myr Fumagalli11,Kenney13 . The energy available for an encounter between these two galaxies is very little because it scales as V^ 2 , where V is their radial velocity difference BinnTrem87 . This energy is not enough to generate a KDC in VCC 1183 or a tail of fireballs in VCC 1217. In addition, there are several evidence, such as the structural features and lack of old stars in VCC 1217's tail, that point ram-pressure stripping as the mechanism responsible for the fireballs on VCC 1217 Kenney13 .

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 3418 as a Virgo dwarf irregular undergoing ram pressure stripping, with a 17 kpc star-forming trail whose compact source shares the galaxy's velocity and association.

quotes.quote: IC 3418 is a dwarf irregular galaxy falling into the Virgo cluster, and a 17 kpc long trail is seen behind the galaxy, which is considered to have formed due to ram pressure stripping. The trail contains compact knots and diffuse blobs of ultraviolet and blue optical emission and, thus, it is a clear site of recent star formation but in an unusual environment, surrounded by a million degree intra-cluster medium.

We focus on IC 3418, a galaxy likely falling into the Virgo cluster for the first time at a very high speed (nearly 1000 km s ^ 1 ; vollmer01 ), especially on its 17 kpc long trail which is considered to have formed behind the galaxy due to ram pressure stripping hester10,fumagalli11 . The trail is manifested as a chain of blobs seen both in GALEX martin05 UV continuum and H emission line images hester10,fumagalli11 .

Our medium-resolution spectrum shows that the heliocentric velocity is 99 km s ^ 1 , which is very close to that of IC 3418 ( 38 km s ^ 1 ; gavazzi04 ). Their similar recession velocities as well as spatial association of SDSSJ1229+1122 with the trail of IC 3418 (Figure 1) strongly support that they are physically associated to each other within the Virgo cluster.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 3418 as a Virgo low-surface-brightness galaxy whose UV/H-alpha tail traces ongoing star formation in a ram-pressure-stripped turbulent wake.

quotes.quote: Galaxy Evolution Explorer observations of IC 3418, a low surface brightness galaxy in the Virgo Cluster, revealed a striking 17 kpc UV tail of bright knots and diffuse emission. H imaging confirms that star formation is ongoing in the tail. IC 3418 was likely recently ram pressure stripped on its first pass through Virgo. We suggest that star formation is occurring in molecular clouds that formed in IC 3418's turbulent stripped wake.
IC 3418 is a member of the Virgo Cluster at a projected distance of 450 kpc ( 94 ) from the cluster's core with a recessional velocity of 38 km s^ 1 Gavazzi04 .

sources.summary: The review uses IC 3418 as a Virgo dwarf example where ram pressure stripped atomic gas and suppressed disc star formation leave a star-forming tail.

quotes.quote: An example of the dramatic effects of a dense environment on the transformation of SFDGs is given by IC 3418, a and deficient dwarf in the Virgo cluster (J achym et al. 2013). The galaxy is at 280 kpc in projection from the massive elliptical M87 at the core of the cluster, with less than 1 of the expected \ and \ mass. All the atomic gas has been stripped from its main body, and CO is only marginally detected in the center. The star formation is quenched in the disc of this galaxy, but it shows a tail of star-forming regions originated from ram-pressure stripped gas (Fig. fig3 , central and right panels; J achym et al. 2013; Kenney et al. 2014).

sources.summary: The review describes IC 3418 as a Virgo dwarf stripped of gas, recently quenched after starburst activity, with ram-pressure-accelerated gas clumps trailing UV knots.

quotes.quote: An exception to this rule is the spectacular IC 3418 (VCC 1217) close to the core of the Virgo cluster (Hester et al. 2010; Fumagalli et al. 2011; Jachym et al. 2013; Kenney et al. 2014). The galaxy, totally stripped of its gas, quenched its activity of star formation 300 to 400 Myr ago probably after a starburst activity. In the tail, the observed H peaks are displaced from the UV emitting knots, suggesting that the gas clumps are continuously accelerated by ram pressure, leaving behind new stars decoupled from the gas (Kenney et al. 2014).

gmw_00002265.name: A1664's BCG
gmw_00002265.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 1664 contains a massive molecular gas reservoir [arXiv:1309.0014] and exhibits a 31-kiloparsec filamentary structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0606305]. Its central active galactic nucleus has undergone a mechanical outburst [arXiv:1810.00881], driving high-velocity gas flows [arXiv:1309.0014, arXiv:1810.00881] and fueling star formation [arXiv:0902.4720, arXiv:astro-ph/0606305].

sources.summary: ALMA observations show the Abell 1664 BCG contains about 1.1e10 solar masses of molecular gas in systemic and high-velocity components; the high-velocity gas may be a nuclear flow or AGN-driven outflow.

quotes.quote: We report ALMA Early Science CO(1-0) and CO(3-2) observations of the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) in Abell 1664. The BCG contains 1.1 10^ 10 of molecular gas divided roughly equally between two distinct velocity systems: one from 250 to +250 centred on the BCG's systemic velocity and a high velocity system blueshifted by 570 with respect to the systemic velocity. The BCG's systemic component shows a smooth velocity gradient across the BCG center with velocity proportional to radius suggestive of solid body rotation about the nucleus.

The high velocity system consists of two gas clumps, each 2 across, located to the north and southeast of the nucleus. Each has a line of sight velocity spread of 250-300 . The velocity of the gas in the high velocity system tends to increase towards the BCG center and could signify a massive high velocity flow onto the nucleus. However, the velocity gradient is not smooth and these structures are also coincident with low optical-UV surface brightness regions, which could indicate dust extinction associated with each clump. If so, the high velocity gas would be projected in front of the BCG and moving toward us along the line of sight in a massive outflow most likely driven by the AGN.

sources.summary: The paper describes A1664 3 as a galaxy-associated radio source near the A1664 relic, giving its spectral index and noting that no jets connecting it to the relic are detected.

quotes.quote: To the north east, the relic appears connected to an unresolved radio source associated with the galaxy A1664 3 pim06 . The high resolution image at 325 MHz with the GMRT resolves this compact source (Fig. 7). The spectral index map between 325 and 1400 MHz and the corresponding uncertainty map are given in Fig. 8.
The source A1664 3 has a spectral index of -0.5.
The relic appears connected to the galaxy A1664 3 in the images. However, no evidence for jets from the galaxy connecting to the relic has been found.

sources.summary: The paper links A1664's BCG to a central AGN outburst, cavity power, molecular gas at the BCG systemic velocity, and cooling that may fuel accretion.

quotes.quote: The central AGN appears to have recently undergone a mechanical outburst, as evidenced by our detection of cavities. These cavities are the X-ray manifestations of radio bubbles inflated by the AGN, and may explain the motion of cold molecular CO clouds previously observed with ALMA . The estimated mechanical power of the AGN, using the minimum energy required to inflate the cavities as a proxy, is P_ cav = (1.1 1.0) 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 , which may be enough to drive the molecular gas flows, and offset the cooling luminosity of the ICM, at L_ cool = (1.90 0.01) 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 . This mechanical power is orders of magnitude higher than the measured upper limit on the X-ray luminosity of the central AGN, suggesting that its black hole may be extremely massive and/or radiatively inefficient. We map temperature variations on the same spatial scale as the molecular gas, and find that the most rapidly cooling gas is mostly coincident with the molecular gas reservoir centered on the BCG's systemic velocity observed with ALMA and may be fueling cold accretion onto the central black hole.

The X-ray centroid is found at (RA, Dec) = ( 13^h03^m42 465, -24^ 14'44 671 ), about 5.6 kpc ( 2.45 ) SW from the cluster emission peak, and 6.5 kpc ( 2.84 ) from the BCG, found at (RA, Dec) = ( 13^h03^m42 540, -24^ 14'42 020 ).

sources.summary: The paper discusses the central galaxy in A1664, finding two emission-line velocity systems, an 8-arcsec filament, extreme cooling, and star formation before an AGN heating event.

quotes.quote: In A1664 the two emission-line velocity systems seen in our previous spectroscopy appear in both and 2 emission, with notable morphological differences. The recession velocity of the red component of increases linearly with decreasing radius, particularly along an 8 filament aligned with the major axis of the underlying galaxy and the cluster X-ray emission. These kinematics are modelled as gravitational free-fall as gas cools rapidly out of the hot phase.

A1664 was the most spectacular and complex of the sample of four CCGs in our earlier H study with VLT-VIMOS (Wilman, Edge Swinbank 2006). The H emission was characterised by a 31 filamentary structure passing through the nucleus in a NE-SW direction, an orientation shared by the larger-scale cluster X-ray emission.

sources.summary: The paper finds the A1664 CCG has bright H emission with a 31 kpc filament, two velocity components, and likely disturbance by a nearby infalling galaxy.

quotes.quote: With a extinction-corrected H slit luminosity of 1.6 10^ 42 , the central cluster galaxy of A1664 is among the brightest H emitters in the optical spectroscopic survey of the ROSAT Brightest Cluster Sample (BCS; Crawford et al. 1999); fits to the excess blue continuum light imply a visible star formation rate of 23 (see also Allen 1995). The complexity of the line emission in A1664 is evident in the reduced two-dimensional frame of one of the quadrants, shown in Figure 1. The emission spans a large range in velocity and at many spatial locations two velocity components are required to satisfactorily model the H + [ NII ] 6548,6584 profiles. From Fig. fig:A1664FLUX , we see that the H emission has a linear filamentary structure spanning approximately 13 arcsec (31 kpc) and passing through the nucleus of the central cluster galaxy along a north-east -- south-west axis. Two velocity components are detected along its whole length: the red component peaks on the nucleus of the CCG, but the blue component exhibits two maxima (labelled B1 and B2) located approximately 1.5 arcsec from the nucleus, along an axis perpendicular to the general direction of the filament.

gmw_00002266.name: COS-87259
gmw_00002266.overview: COS-87259 is a massive, extremely red galaxy at a redshift of z = 6.853 [arXiv:2206.00018, arXiv:2108.01084]. It hosts a heavily obscured, radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2206.00018, arXiv:2108.01084] and is undergoing vigorous, obscured star formation [arXiv:2206.00018, arXiv:2108.01084].

sources.summary: The paper identifies COS-87259 as a z=6.853 massive reionization-era galaxy with strong [CII] and dust emission, vigorous obscured star formation, and a heavily obscured luminous AGN.

quotes.quote: We present band 6 ALMA observations of a heavily-obscured radio-loud ( L_ 1.4 \ GHz =10^ 25.4 W Hz ^ 1 ) AGN candidate at z_ phot =6.83 0.06 found in the 1.5 deg ^2 COSMOS field. The ALMA data reveal detections of exceptionally strong [ CII ] 158 m ( z_ [ CII ] =6.8532 ) and underlying dust continuum emission from this object (COS-87259), where the [ CII ] line luminosity, line width, and 158 m continuum luminosity are comparable to that seen from z 7 sub-mm galaxies and quasar hosts. The 158 m continuum detection suggests a total infrared luminosity of 9 10^ 12 L_ with corresponding very large obscured star formation rate (1300 M_ /yr) and dust mass ( 2 10^9 M_ ). The strong break seen between the VIRCam and IRAC photometry perhaps suggests that COS-87259 is an extremely massive reionization-era galaxy with M_ 1.7 10^ 11 M_ . Moreover, the MIPS, PACS, and SPIRE detections imply that this object harbors an AGN that is heavily obscured ( _ _ 9.7 m =2.3 ) with a bolometric luminosity of approximately 5 10^ 13 L_ .

Regardless, the very bright ALMA detections of COS-87259 make it clear that this object is undergoing vigorous obscured star formation activity comparable to z 7 sub-mm galaxies and quasar hosts (Fig. fig:literatureComparison ). The range of stellar masses ( 10^ 10-11 M_ ) and SFRs (1300-3000 M_ /yr) inferred for COS-87259 suggest a specific SFR between 13 to 300 Gyr ^ 1 , consistent with the range of sSFRs inferred among otherwise typical extremely UV-luminous ( 22 ) z 7-8 galaxies Topping2022_REBELS .

sources.summary: The paper presents COS-87259 as an archetypal z=6.853 obscured AGN whose infrared continuum and silicate absorption would let SALTUS constrain luminosity, obscuration, and host-ISM dust.

quotes.quote: The archetype example is COS-87259 which is confirmed to lie at z=6.853 within the 1.5 deg ^2 COSMOS field endsley23 . Several broadband photometric detections with Spitzer/MIPS, Herschel/PACS, and Herschel/SPIRE indicate that this obscured AGN has a flux density of approximately 0.3 to 10 mJy that rises between observed-frame 34 to 230 (Figure fig:agnendsley ). The unprecedented mid- and far-infrared sensitivity and spectroscopic capabilities of SALTUS will not only dramatically improve constraints on the bolometric AGN luminosity of this system, but also enable the much-needed measurement of the rest-frame 9.7 silicate absorption feature in this system. This silicate absorption feature is a highly valuable tracer of the amount of obscuration towards heavily-buried AGN like COS-87259, which cannot be detected in deep X-ray imaging e.g. hickox18 . This feature also provides an estimate of the amount of obscuration due to the host galaxy ISM rather than circumnuclear dust.

COS-87259 was found within a relatively small field and we expect future surveys to rapidly identify many more similar systems e.g. lyu23 . These will be excellent targets for pointed follow-up with SALTUS to deliver statistical constraints on the black hole growth rates, as well as extent and origin of obscuration among the most luminous, buried AGN in the reionization era.

sources.summary: The paper presents COS-87259 as a likely z 6.8 massive, dusty Lyman-break galaxy with compact steep-spectrum radio emission, obscured star formation, an obscured radio-loud AGN, and a possible overdensity.

quotes.quote: This source, COS-87259, exhibits a sharp flux discontinuity (factor > 3) between two narrow/intermediate bands at 9450 and 9700 and is undetected in all nine bands blueward of 9600 , as expected from a Lyman-alpha break at z 6.8 . The full multi-wavelength (X-ray through radio) data of COS-87529 can be self-consistently explained by a very massive (M _ =10^ 10.8 M _ ) and extremely red (rest-UV slope = -0.59 ) z 6.8 galaxy with hyperluminous infrared emission ( L_ IR =10^ 13.6 L _ ) powered by both an intense burst of highly-obscured star formation (SFR 1800 M _ yr ^ 1 ) and an obscured ( _ _ 9.7 m = 7.7 2.5 ) radio-loud (L _ 1.4 \ GHz 10^ 25.4 W Hz ^ 1 ) AGN. The radio emission is compact (1.04 0.12 arcsec) and exhibits an ultra-steep spectrum between 1.32--3 GHz ( =-1.57^ +0.22 _ 0.21 ) that flattens at lower frequencies ( = -0.86^ +0.22 _ 0.16 between 0.144--1.32 GHz), consistent with known z>4 radio galaxies.

We also demonstrate that COS-87259 may reside in a significant (11 ) galaxy overdensity, as common for systems hosting radio-loud AGN. While we find that low-redshift solutions to the optical+near-infrared data are not preferred, a spectroscopic redshift will ultimately be required to establish the true nature of COS-87259 beyond any doubt. If confirmed to lie at z 6.8 , the properties of COS-87259 would be consistent with a picture wherein AGN and highly-obscured star formation activity are fairly common among very massive (M _ >10^ 10 M _ ) reionization-era galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper treats COS-87259 as the only similar known heavily obscured z~7 radio source, spectroscopically confirmed at z=6.8 and useful for inferring undercounted radio-loud QSOs.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, a powerful, heavily obscured radio source ( L_ Bol 10^ 46 erg s ^ 1 ) was discovered at z 7 (COS-87259, Ends22 ) -- and even this object posed more questions than it answered. COS-87259, first identified in the COSMOS field thanks to the broad bandwidth and depths accessed in the COSMOS survey, was recently spectroscopically confirmed at z=6.8 via [ CII ] detection in ALMA Band 6 observations ends23 . Bona fide evidence of the central engine in COS-87259 was discovered due to its bright radio emission.

The detection of this source (COSW-106725) and COS-87259 ends23 within the epoch of z=6.8 -8 in a 1.5 ^2 field hints that the space density of luminous, radio-loud QSOs at these epochs may be underestimated by over a factor of 2000.

sources.summary: The paper singles out COS-87259 as the reddest, [C II]-confirmed galaxy in the sample, likely hosting an obscured AGN that may affect its inferred age and stellar mass.

quotes.quote: Notably, the reddest galaxy in our sample (COS-87259) is spectroscopically confirmed via [ C II ] and likely hosts a heavily obscured active galactic nucleus endsley2022 , leading to possible uncertainties on its age and stellar mass. However, we investigate the influence of COS-87259 on our inferred age distribution and find that the distribution is only minimally affected by its inclusion (see Section \ sec:discussion ).

We re-derive the age distribution excluding objects with UV slopes of > -1 (including COS-87259, which may have a slightly uncertain age due to possible contamination by an active galactic nucleus) and find a marginally larger fraction of the distribution with ages 250 Myr ( 33_ 7 ^ +8 per cent).

gmw_00002267.name: NGC 4016
gmw_00002267.overview: NGC 4016 is an SBd-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:1112.4058, arXiv:1704.04344] that forms the northern component of the interacting pair Arp 305 [arXiv:0904.0670, arXiv:1704.04344]. It features a dusty disk with a warped neutral hydrogen structure [arXiv:0904.0670, arXiv:1704.04344] and a figure-eight inner shape likely caused by a bar [arXiv:0904.0670].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4016 as Arp 305’s northern SBdm companion, with a misshapen bulge, dusty disk, and figure-eight inner structure likely related to a bar.

quotes.quote: The Northern galaxy in Arp 305, NGC 4016, is designated SBdm (NED) and shows a misshapen bulge with a dusty disk. In the inner disk, a curious figure-eight shape is seen (see Figure 2). There does not seem to be any evidence to suggest that this figure-eight is an accretion feature or a wind flow. It seems too symmetric in both the GALEX and SDSS images to result from either. We suspect that the figure-eight formation in NGC 4016 is the result of a bar (see the third frame of Figure 3 in rom08 ).

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC4016 because of an apparently unrealistically low catalog color, but its own photometry and velocity comparison do not support an anomalous M/L.

quotes.quote: NGC4016 is an SBd-type galaxy. We included it in our sample due to its unrealistically low color index (B-V)=0.03 in the NED, which we do not confirm (see Table table2 ). The rotational velocity v_ max disk determined from the photometric density estimate using our color and luminosity measurements turns out to be lower than the total rotational velocity v_ max (see Table table4 ). This gives no grounds for attributing it to objects with anomalous M/L .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4016 as one member of the interacting Arp 305 pair, with GMRT H I emission showing a mostly regular but warped rotating disk.

quotes.quote: For black NGC 4016, \ emission is detected in the channel maps (Figures 8 and 9) within a velocity range 3348 \ to 3496 . The black NGC 4016 iso--velocity contours (Figure fig4 ) indicate reasonably regular rotation in the \ disk, with a north-south (N--S) kinematic axis. The closed iso--velocity contours at the \ disk edges indicate the disk is warped with the velocity gradient black becoming progressively shallower towards the north. NGC 4016 has black a GMRT V _ HI = 3454 7 black , with W _ 20 130 7 , close to the W _ 50 133 15 \ from the single dish on--line data from .

gmw_00002268.name: NGC 4017
gmw_00002268.overview: NGC 4017 is a nearly face-on SABbc spiral galaxy that contains an H II region nucleus [arXiv:0904.0670]. It is the more disturbed member of the interacting pair Arp 305, displaying a rotating disk, two tidal tails, and extended ultraviolet emission [arXiv:1704.04344, arXiv:0904.0670].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 4017 as the more disturbed Arp 305 spiral, with regular disk rotation plus tidal-tail structures and multiple H I components.

quotes.quote: In the black channel maps (Figures 8 and 9) \ emission is detected for NGC 4017 in the velocity range 3292 \ to 3595 . Its GMRT V _ HI = 3439 7 \ black agrees well with the heliocentric optical radial velocity black for NGC 4017 (3449 2 ). black The GMRT W _ 20 = 258 7 \ is also similar to the W _ 50 black = 253 5 \ from single dish on--line data haynes11 . The black NGC 4017 iso--velocity contours in the \ velocity field (Figure fig4 ) also show a fairly regular rotation black pattern (PA = 111 ). A position velocity (PV) diagram for a cut along the black NGC 4017 major axis (Figure fig6 ), shows the \ line centre to be 3447 7 \ and maximum rotation velocity to be 150 7 \ black (before inclination correction).

black Overall the \ morphology and kinematics for NGC 4017 suggest multiple \ structures in NGC 4017: (i) a regular rotating \ disk with a similar inclination to the optical galaxy (48 ) and PA 111 ; (ii) a \ (iii) \ (iv) the \ counterpart which is distinguishable black by black its local column density peak black , although , not kinematically; and the extensive \ which is not clearly distinguishable as a separate kinematic structure from the \ bridge black or NW tidal tail.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4017 as Arp 305’s primary, a nearly face-on SABbc galaxy with two tidal tails, extended UV emission, and an ocular star-forming waveform.

quotes.quote: Arp 305 is a very wide pair with the primary galaxy, NGC 4017, to the South and the companion, NGC 4016, to the North (Figure 1). Using velocities from the NASA Extragalactic Database (NED), and assuming a Hubble constant of 75 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , we find Arp 305 is at a distance of 50 Mpc. NGC 4017 is designated as SABbc (NED) and has been classed as an `H II region nucleus' galaxy by dah85 . It appears nearly face-on, with two tidal tails, one pointing northwest and one to the southeast. The UV images show much extended emission to the northwest and southeast far outside the main disk.

gmw_00002269.name: Bridge TDG candidate
gmw_00002269.overview: The Bridge TDG candidate is a tidal dwarf galaxy candidate projected within the tidal debris of Arp 305 [arXiv:1704.04344]. It exhibits a star formation rate of 0.2 solar masses per year, and its Spitzer infrared colors suggest that stellar debris may have seeded its formation [arXiv:1704.04344].

sources.summary: The Bridge TDG candidate is projected in Arp 305 tidal debris; its H I mass, velocity, linewidth, star formation, and Spitzer colors support a tidal-dwarf interpretation.

quotes.quote: The \ properties of the Bridge TDG candidate black include: black \ 6.6 10 ^8 \ and V _ HI = 3500 7 black , which is in good agreement with the velocities of the parent galaxies . Additionally black the TDG's \ linewidth of 30 \ and modest velocity gradient together with its SFR of 0.2 \ add to the evidence favouring the bridge candidate being a genuine TDG. black The Bridge TDG's Spitzer 3.6 m and 4.5 m counterparts with a [ 3.6 ] - [ 4.5 ] colour 0.2 mag suggests stellar debris may have seeded its formation .

gmw_00002270.name: Himiko
gmw_00002270.overview: Himiko is a giant, luminous Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy at redshift z ~ 6.6 [arXiv:1204.4456, arXiv:1305.2202]. High-resolution imaging reveals a multiple-component morphology suggestive of a merging system [arXiv:1605.05325], while observations indicate it is a metal-poor, gas-rich, and essentially dust-free galaxy [arXiv:1505.04758].

sources.summary: The paper treats Himiko as a giant z=6.595 Lyman-alpha blob, reporting no CO detection and an upper limit on molecular gas relative to its high stellar mass.

quotes.quote: We have used the Green Bank Telescope to carry out a deep search for redshifted \ line emission from an extended ( > 17 kpc) Ly blob (LAB), ``Himiko'', at z 6.595 . Our non-detection of \ emission places the strong 3 upper limit of L^ _ CO < 1.8 10^ 10 ( V/250)^ 1/2 K pc ^2 on the CO line luminosity.

The target of our CO line observations, ``Himiko'', is a giant ( 17 kpc) LAE at z=6.595 , discovered in the Subaru/ XMM-Newton Deep Survey ouchi09 . Himiko has an extremely high Ly line luminosity, L(Ly ) =(3.9 0.2) 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , well into the LAB category. The Ly emission yields a star-formation rate (SFR) of 36 M _ /yr ouchi09 ; However, this is likely to be an underestimate by a factor of at least a few, given that the Ly line is both resonantly scattered by dust, and, for these redshifts, attenuated due to absorption by the damping wing of neutral hydrogen in the intergalactic medium. The SFR of Himiko is thus likely to be significantly larger than the estimate of 36 M _ /yr. ouchi09 used fits of stellar synthesis models to the spectral energy distribution over the optical to near-infrared (near-IR) wavelength range to obtain an extremely high stellar mass, (0.9 - 5) 10^ 10 M _ , indicating that Himiko is a massive galaxy.
Our constraint on the CO line luminosity implies an upper limit of M(H _2 ) < 1.4 10^ 10 M _ on the molecular gas mass, assuming a ULIRG conversion factor, X_ CO = 0.8 M _ (K pc ^2 ) ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper compares simulated far-infrared line emission to Himiko, describing its high-redshift LAE nature, large Ly-alpha nebula, uncertain star-formation rate, and sub-solar metallicity constraint.

quotes.quote: Recently, walter2012 have tried to detect the [ CII ] emission in Himiko , one of the the most luminous LAEs at z=6.6 ouchi2009 . However, they end up only with a 1 upper limit of 0.7 mJy km s^ 1 . The large size of the Himiko Ly emitting nebula ( 17 kpc ) makes this object one the most massive galaxies discovered at such high redshifts ouchi2009, wagg2012 . From this point of view, Himiko 's properties closely resemble those of the prototypical galaxy selected from our simulation. Moreover, the radius of the region within which we distributed the stars ( 1-2 kpc) is consistent with the Himiko half-light radius (1.6 kpc) observed by Ouchi et al. (2009).

At Z=Z_ , our model slightly exceeds the 1 =0.7 mJy km s^ 1 upper limit on the [ ] intensity found in Himiko through PdBI observations walter2012 . This result suggests that the gas metallicity in this source must be sub-solar.

sources.summary: The paper models Himiko's UV-to-FIR SED and ALMA limits, finding that continuous star formation implies high dust temperature and a tight dust-mass upper limit.

quotes.quote: We apply these models to Himiko (as a representative UV-bright object) and a composite SED of z>5 Lyman break galaxies (LBGs). For Himiko, Model A requires a significant dust extinction, which leads to a high dust temperature >70 K for consistency with the ALMA upper limit. This high dust temperature puts a strong upper limit on the total dust mass M_ d 2 10^6 M _ , and the dust mass produced per supernova (SN) m_ d,SN 0.1 M _ . Such a low m_ d,SN suggests significant loss of dust by reverse shock destruction or outflow, and implies that SNe are not the dominant source of dust at high z . Model B allows M_ d 2 10^7 M _ and m_ d,SN 0.3 M _ .
hereafter O13 Ouchi:2013aa observed a Lyman (Ly )-emitting gas blob `Himiko' at z = 6.6 using ALMA. O13 put an upper limit of 0.0521 mJy (3 ) at 1.2 mm for Himiko.

sources.summary: The paper describes HIMIKO as a z=5.95 luminous Ly-alpha emitter with ALMA clumps, measured size and linewidth, and only upper-limit dust constraints that leave SNe and AGB dust production unresolved.

quotes.quote: Discovered with the Subaru telescope at redshift z 5.95, HIMIKO was at that moment the most luminous Ly emitter Ouchi2009 . Recent observations of this object using ALMA in band 6 revealed the presence of three clumps. From the [ CII ] detections the size of HIMIKO and the velocity linewidth were measured HIMIKOmod . The upper limit on the continuum emission of S158 m < 27 Jy was also reported.

HIMIKO, CR7, and SXDF only have upper limits for the mass of dust, so we cannot rule out either SNe or AGB stars as dust producers. We can only conclude that one SN in these galaxies could not produce more than 0.01 M_ of dust.

sources.summary: The paper uses Himiko ALMA non-detection to constrain its total dust mass and dust produced per supernova, favoring reverse-shock destruction in the high-redshift LAE.

quotes.quote: We focus on the dust enrichment of a specific, luminous LAE (Himiko, z 6.6 ) for which a stringent upper limit of 52.1 Jy ( 3 ) has recently been obtained from ALMA continuum observations at 1.2 mm. We predict its submillimetre dust emission using detailed models that follow SN dust enrichment and destruction and the equilibrium dust temperature , and obtain a plausible upper limit to the dust mass produced by a single SN: m_ d,SN < 0.15 - 0.45 M _ , depending on the adopted dust optical properties. These upper limits are smaller than the dust mass deduced for SN 1987A and that predicted by dust condensation theories, implying that dust produced in SNe are likely to be subject to reverse shock destruction before being injected into the interstellar medium.

Total dust mass Our first aim is to obtain an upper limit on the dust mass in Himiko, given the ALMA upper-limit of 52.1 Jy ( 3 ) at an observational wavelength of 1.2 mm (a rest wavelength of 158 , see O13 for observational details).

Therefore, the upper limit obtained for Himiko favours reverse shock destruction with an ambient density of n_ H 1 cm ^ 3 , although a scenario with no dust destruction is still permitted provided we use low- _ dust species such as SN _ con (Table tab:kappa ).

sources.summary: The paper targets Himiko as a luminous z=6.595 LAE, notes its large extent and stellar mass, and places FIR and dust-obscured SFR limits.

quotes.quote: The first source is an exceptionally luminous LAE at z=6.595 (Ouchi et al. \ 2009) dubbed Himiko by these authors. Himiko is by far the brightest and most spatially extended ( 3" ) LAE known at these redshifts, with a lower limit to the SFR derived from the rest--frame UV emission (with no correction for dust attenuation) of 25 M _ yr ^ 1 . Himiko is even detected at 3.6 m using Spitzer, implying a significant underlying stellar mass of 4 10 ^ 10 M _ (Ouchi et al. \ 2009).

Conservatively assuming a SED shape typical of local spiral galaxies we derive upper limits for the FIR--based star formation rates (SFRs) of 70 M _ yr ^ 1 , 50 M _ yr ^ 1 and 40 M _ yr ^ 1 for Himiko , IOK--1 and GRB 090423 , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper cites Himiko as an extended z=6.6 LAE undetected by ALMA, with a high inferred escape fraction and likely low metallicity and dust content.

quotes.quote: At higher redshift, Ouchi13 recently failed to detect both 1.2 mm continuum and the \ emission line from the extended z=6.6 LAE `Himiko' with ALMA. Using their limit on L_ IR yields >0.80 ( 1 ) -- significantly higher than expected from the global evolution see also Figure fig:escape Hayes11 , although as Himiko is spatially extended the relevant physical effects may be different. We also caution that at z=6.6 the CMB temperature ( 20 K) can make it harder to detect a galaxy's dust emission e.g. daCunha13 , an effect that Ouchi13 did not explicitly include in their calculations, and which could increase the far-infrared luminosity limit and decrease the \ limit. However, the high \ is consistent with the hypothesis that Himiko has low metallicity and low dust content Ouchi13 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Himiko as the previous benchmark bright z=6.6 Ly emitter and shows comparable or brighter LAEs are less rare than previously thought.

quotes.quote: The brightest Ly emitter in their sample, Himiko , with a luminosity of 3.5 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 Ouchi2009,Ouchi2013 , has been seen as a very rare source, a triple merger, one of its kind. Because there has been only one very bright source known, the error on its number density is large, such that there is a factor 30 offset between the fitted luminosity function and the data.

CR7 is detected at only half of the narrow-band filter transmission, leading to a 2" luminosity of 5.8 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , which is a factor 2 higher than our estimate from the photometry. Based on MAG-AUTO , the luminosity is 9.6 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , and thus a factor 2.5 brighter than Himiko .

sources.summary: The paper includes Himiko as a spectroscopically confirmed z=6.6 Lyman-alpha emitter in the bright LBG sample, noting its multiple-component HST morphology and use as a comparison object.

quotes.quote: At z 7 the morphology of the brightest LBGs is uncertain due to the lack of high-resolution near-infrared imaging of these rare galaxies, however there have been hints of highly clumpy systems from the detailed study of two extremely bright Lyman- emitters (LAEs) nicknamed `Himiko' Ouchi2009 and `CR7' Sobral2015 . Both of these LAEs are also bright in the rest-frame UV continuum ( m_ AB 25 ), where they appear to consist of multiple components in high-resolution HST /WFC3 imaging, suggestive of a merging system Ouchi2013 . Despite the intense study of these galaxies, with only two objects it is unclear whether the extended clumpy morphology observed is typical for all bright star-forming galaxies at z 7 or is exclusive to strong Lyman- emitters.
The sample included the z = 6.6 LAE `Himiko' Ouchi2009 , which was reselected as a lower redshift LBG ( z 6.4 ) using our broad-band data.

sources.summary: The paper uses Himiko as a z=6.6 luminous blue galaxy that is metal-poor, gas-rich, essentially dust-free, and limited to a low dust-to-stellar mass ratio.

quotes.quote: `Normal' galaxies appear to be essentially dust free, with a prototypical example being the luminous blue galaxy (LBG) `Himiko' ( z=6.6 ), for which the estimated star-formation timescale is _ = M_ stars / SFR 150 Myr Ouchi13,Fisher14 . Himiko is metal-poor and gas-rich galaxy with a metallicity that is only a few percent (at most) of the solar value Z_ and essentially no molecules and dust emission detected. An upper limit to the dust-to-stellar mass ratio of 5.0 10^ 4 can be derived, though.

gmw_00002271.name: SDSS J082905.46+140406.7
gmw_00002271.overview: SDSS J082905.46+140406.7 is a potential spiral galaxy with a passive-looking optical spectrum [arXiv:1801.03456]. However, emission line strengths suggest it may be a LINER, and it is considered a strong candidate for hosting an offset active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1801.03456].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this possible spiral as a LINER-like passive-spectrum galaxy with a 239 mas radio offset and strong offset-AGN candidacy.

quotes.quote: SDSS J082905.46+140406.7 Single CLASS detection of 22.3 mJy (NVSS J082905+140407), offset by 239 mas from the SDSS position. The SDSS spectrum looks like that of a passive galaxy, but the OSSY line strengths indicate that this galaxy may be a LINER. A potential spiral galaxy, and a strong candidate for an offset-AGN host.

gmw_00002272.name: SDSS J084002.36+294902.5
gmw_00002272.overview: SDSS J084002.36+294902.5 is a nearby elliptical radio galaxy [arXiv:1208.4581] that hosts a hidden active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1208.4581] and exhibits optical shells and dust lanes from a past merger [arXiv:1208.4581]. The galaxy features complex radio jets [arXiv:1208.4581, arXiv:2301.07138] and a bipolar ionized gas outflow driven by jet-environment interactions [arXiv:2007.14977].

sources.summary: The paper models 4C+29.30 as a nearby radio-loud hidden AGN with absorbed hard X-rays, diffuse soft emission, complex radio structure, and merger-linked optical shells and dust lanes.

quotes.quote: The hard ( >2 keV) X-ray spectrum of 4C+29.30 can be decomposed into an intrinsic hard power-law ( 1.56 ) modified by a cold absorber with an intrinsic column density N_ H, z 5 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 , and its reflection ( | /2 | 0.3 ) from a neutral matter including a narrow iron K emission line at the rest frame energy 6.4 keV. The reflected component is less absorbed than the intrinsic one with an upper limit on the absorbing column of N^ refl _ H, z <2.5 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 . The X-ray spectrum varied between the XMM-Newton and Chandra observations.

We hypothesize that 4C+29.30 belongs to a class of `hidden' AGN containing a geometrically thick torus. However, unlike the majority of them, 4C+29.30 is radio-loud. Correlations between the scattering fraction and Eddington luminosity ratio, and the one between black hole mass and stellar velocity dispersion, imply that 4C+29.30 hosts a black hole with 10^8 M _ mass.

4C+29.30 is a low-redshift ( z=0.0647 ) radio source with a moderate radio luminosity 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 (Siemiginowska et al. 2012) hosted by an elliptical galaxy. It was first studied in the radio and optical bands by van Breugel et al. (1986). Observed complex radio morphology was resolved into jets, knots, lobes, and a diffuse tail. A number of optical emission lines were measured, and their relative intensities indicated photoionization by a non-stellar continuum attributed to the central active galactic nucleus (AGN). The radio emission and the optical line emitting gas were found to be strongly linked suggesting interaction between the radio source and its environment. In addition, shells and dust lanes observed in the optical band (van Breugel et al. 1986) provide evidence of a past merger with a gas-rich disk galaxy which possibly triggered the rejuvenated AGN activity.

sources.summary: The paper notes 4C+29.30 has complex soft-band jet morphology but nuclear hard emission; models require variability, yet do not significantly prefer N_H changes over flux changes.

quotes.quote: 4C+29.30 Data reduction/fitting: The data shows a complex morphology in the soft band, including a jet further out from the nucleus see e.g. Siemiginowska2012 . The usual 5 radius source region was used, but the background was extracted from a nearby 10 radius circle, rather than an annulus, in order to avoid contamination. Furthermore, Chandra observation 1 has low quality, forcing us to use 5 cts/bin, and fit with C-statistic. The table shows therefore total Stat. instead of ^2 . The emission was extracted as usual (avoiding the jet emission), but the larger region (needed to include the PSF) resulted in including a larger fraction of hot gas. An additional constant was used to weight the normalization of apec , but both and data were compatible with having the same exact kT . A second observation exists (Obs. ID: 0504120201) which was not used, at it fell on the same day as the used observation (Obs. ID: 0504120101) and was much shorter see e.g. Sobolewska2012 . All emission at > 2 keV originates in the nucleus, therefore the data is not affected by the jet presence.

Analysis of results: The data is well-fitted by all models, which are in good agreement. We note that observations 2 5 took place within 1 week, which likely explains the lack of flux/ N_ H,los variability among those observations. While it is clear from the ^2_ red comparison that the data requires some form of variability ( T>20 ), neither intrinsic flux nor N_ H,los variability is preferred over the other. The one exception to this is perhaps borus02 , which shows a tension of >3 between model and data when no N_ H,los variability is allowed. This is likely due to the high obscuration the model predicts for the observation. In any case, the tension is not significant enough, and we classify this source as `Non-variable in N_ H,los '.

sources.summary: The paper studies 4C +29.30 as a merger radio galaxy whose ionized gas shows jet-linked bipolar outflows, strong blueshifts, high dispersions, and energetics capable of affecting host evolution.

quotes.quote: We investigate the ionized gas excitation and kinematics in the inner 4.3 6.2 kpc ^ 2 of the merger radio galaxy 4C +29.30. Using optical integral field spectroscopy with the Gemini North Telescope, we present flux distributions, line-ratio maps, peak velocities and velocity dispersion maps as well as channel maps with a spatial resolution of 955 pc. We observe high blueshifts of up to 650 in a region 1'' south of the nucleus (the southern knot -- SK), which also presents high velocity dispersions ( 250 ), which we attribute to an outflow. A possible redshifted counterpart is observed north from the nucleus (the northern knot -- NK). We propose that these regions correspond to a bipolar outflow possibly due to the interaction of the radio jet with the ambient gas.

We estimate a total ionized gas mass outflow rate of M _ out = 25.4 +11.5 7.5 with a kinetic power of E = 8.1 +10.7 4.0 10^ 42 , which represents 5.8 +7.6 2.9 of the AGN bolometric luminosity. These values are higher than usually observed in nearby active galaxies with the same bolometric luminosities and could imply a significant impact of the outflows in the evolution of the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 0836+299 as the lone nearby FRI radio galaxy, with diffuse halo-scale X-ray emission and intrinsic absorption likely associated with dusty material.

quotes.quote: The nearby FRI radio galaxy 0836+299 has interesting properties which distinguish it from the other quasars in our sample. This is the only source with evidence for diffuse X-ray emission on the galaxy's halo scale, with X-ray luminosities consistent with other FRIs (Worrall et al. 2001). Interestingly, we find evidence for excess absorption from the core, with column density N _H 5 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 \ (Table 5).

Our findings for 0836+299 (and for other FRIs studied at X-rays by our group) show that at least some FRIs possess excess X-ray absorption. The optical extinction implied by the X-ray column density is A _V 240 mag, using Galactic gas-to-dust ratios (A _V 5 10^ 22 N _H mag/cm ^ 2 ). A nuclear dust lane has been observed in 0836+299 in ground-based optical images (van Breugel et al. 1986) and in our HST images (Scarpa et al. 2003). In fact, the source has a flat _ ox index and is a luminous IR source detected with IRAS at 25 and 60 m (Golombek et al. 1988), indicating the presence of dust. The amount of reddening for the optical nuclear light is A _V=1.2 mag (van Breugel et al. 1986). This is much less than the reddening derived from our X-ray column. Therefore, it seems likely that at least part of the X-ray absorber originates in a different component than the gas responsible for the optical reddening (unless that gas-to-dust ratio is much different from Galactic; e.g., Maiolino et al. 2001). The origin of the X-ray absorbing medium in 0836+299, as in other FRIs studied by us (Sambruna et al. 2003), is not clear.

sources.summary: The paper describes 0836+29-I as a possible merger and restarting radio source, with large-scale aged emission and compact parsec-scale structure resembling the arcsecond source.

quotes.quote: 0836+29-I ( 4C 29.30 ) This source ( z=0.0647 , also referred to as B2 0836+29A ) has been often confused in the literature with the cD galaxy B2 0836+29 (0836+29-II, at z = 0.079 ), the brightest galaxy in Abell 690, which was studied by . 0836+29-I was studied in detail by , and recently by . It is a possible merger system ( ). On the large scale the source shows clear evidence of intermittent radio activity. There is a large scale structure about 9' in size, with an estimated age of about 200 Myr ( ); a more compact structure shows a central core and two bright spots and an extended emission about 1' in size with an age lower than 100 Myr.

sources.summary: The paper describes a Seyfert-like diffuse Sa galaxy with a 262 mas CLASS offset, strong emission lines, no visible 4000 break, and extended radio-galaxy core.

quotes.quote: SDSS J084002.36+294902.5 AGN class: Seyfert, Morphology: diffuse, Sa (NED) Single CLASS detection of 10.1 mJy (4C +29.30), offset by 262 mas from the SDSS position. The optical images show a red nucleus surrounded by a blue haze. A second object, offset by approximately 5 arcsec, is not detected by CLASS or Chandra . The spectrum shows exceptionally strong [ O iii ] emission compared to other lines, and there is no visible 4000 break. The AGN is classified as a Seyfert 2 by .

gmw_00002273.name: SDSS J084307.11+453742.8
gmw_00002273.overview: SDSS J084307.11+453742.8 is a radio galaxy [arXiv:1411.0361] classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1801.03456] that may host a disk [arXiv:1801.03456]. It features a core-jet radio structure [arXiv:1411.0361] and exhibits deep neutral hydrogen 21-cm absorption [arXiv:1411.0361, arXiv:2010.01769].

sources.summary: The paper describes B3 0839+458 as a flat-spectrum point/core-jet radio source with small lobes, high radio power, systemic narrow deep H I absorption, and a slight blueshifted asymmetry.

quotes.quote: The VLBA observations at 5 GHz, as part of the VLBA Imaging and Polarimetry survey (VISP, ), classify it as a core-jet source. The lobes have sizes of approximately 3.5 mas and are separated by 6 mas ( =0.2 kpc at z=0.1919 ). The radio power of the source is P_ 1.4 GHz 3 10^ 25 W Hz ^ 1 . In our work, we detect the \ at the systemic velocity of the galaxy ( v=57   ). The line is narrow and deep, suggesting that the \ is settled in a rotating disk around the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper presents this source as a promising offset-AGN host, with a bright CLASS offset, possible disk, OSSY AGN classification, and need for better optical centering.

quotes.quote: SDSS J084307.11+453742.8 Single, bright, CLASS detection of 135 mJy (NVSS J084307+453743), offset by 232 mas from the SDSS position. find a slightly lower offset of 177 mas for this source. The SDSS images suggest that this galaxy may possibly host a disk. Although the SDSS spectrum has no obvious emission lines, the source was classified as an AGN using the OSSY data. However, OSSY failed to find the O i _ 6300 line, so the type of AGN is unknown.

sources.summary: The paper treats J084307.11+453742.8 as an associated absorber target, noting deep HI 21-cm absorption, integrated optical depth near 29.79 km/s, and a two-component Gaussian profile.

quotes.quote: Although most of the HI frequencies of our targets locate within the 1150 - 1250 MHz frequency range, which is severally affected by satellite RFI, one of our target, J084307.11+453742.8, does show a deep HI 21-cm absorption. The HI absorption feature is shown in Fig. fig:J0843HI . The integrated optical depth from -200 km/s to 200 km/s is about 29.79 km/s, which is a little bit larger than the value from maccagni17 (Table tab:fast_sample ). We fit the absorption profile with two Gaussian components and the resulting peak optical depths and FWHMs are _ p1 = 0.58 , FWHM _1 = 23.83 km/s, _ p2 = 0.18 , FWHM _2 = 78.13 km/s. The narrow component has a much deeper and narrower absorption profile comparing to maccagni17 .

gmw_00002274.name: SDSS J091445.53+413714.2
gmw_00002274.overview: SDSS J091445.53+413714.2 is classified as an offset active galactic nucleus candidate [arXiv:1801.03456] and is bisected by a 10 kpc dust lane [arXiv:2210.11724]. The galaxy features a coincident radio core alongside a northern radio lobe that contains a secondary peak [arXiv:2210.11724].

sources.summary: The paper flags this galaxy as an offset-AGN candidate with a 357 mas CLASS offset, a spectral-line offset selection, dust lane, and significant Rayleigh deviation.

quotes.quote: SDSS J091445.53+413714.2 Single CLASS detection of 9.8 mJy (B3 0911+418), offset by 357 mas from the SDSS position. This object was selected by as an offset-AGN candidate on the basis of a spectral-line offset. There is a clear dust lane, which would make the optical centre more difficult to determine, but it still represents a good follow-up candidate. This offset deviates from the fitted Rayleigh distribution with 4-sigma confidence.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0914+413 as dust-lane bisected, with uncertain bulge-disk decomposition, a northern radio lobe with a secondary peak, and a radio core coincident with the galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0914 + 413 : The galaxy is bisected by a prominent, 10 kpc dust lane. The complicated dust lane introduces significant uncertainties to the bulge-to-disk decomposition. The northern lobe has a second peak with an intensity half of that of the main one, but no counterpart is found in the HST image and AllWISE catalog. In addition to the two lobes, a radio core coincides with the galaxy.

gmw_00002275.name: SDSS J103258.89+564453.3
gmw_00002275.overview: SDSS J103258.89+564453.3 is a compact galaxy characterized by a core in a diffuse halo and a single CLASS radio detection offset by 207 mas from its SDSS position [arXiv:1801.03456]. Although its spectrum appears to be that of a passive elliptical galaxy, its line strengths suggest it may be a LINER and a strong candidate for an offset-AGN host [arXiv:1801.03456].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this compact galaxy as a strong offset-AGN candidate, with a radio offset, prominent core, diffuse halo, passive spectrum, and LINER-like line strengths.

quotes.quote: SDSS J103258.89+564453.3 (MCG +10-15-099) Morphology: compact (NED) Single CLASS detection of 28.8 mJy (7C 1029+5700), offset by 207 mas from the SDSS position. This galaxy has a very prominent core in a diffuse halo, and the SDSS spectrum appears to be that of a passive elliptical galaxy. However, OSSY line strengths indicate that this galaxy may be a LINER. We consider this galaxy a strong candidate for an offset-AGN host.

gmw_00002276.name: SDSS J111916.53+623925.7
gmw_00002276.overview: SDSS J111916.53+623925.7 is a passive elliptical or S0 candidate galaxy featuring a bright nucleus [arXiv:1801.03456]. Although its optical spectrum shows little evidence of active galactic nucleus activity, its line strengths suggest it may be a LINER [arXiv:1801.03456].

sources.summary: The paper describes this bright-nucleus passive elliptical/S0 candidate as LINER-like, with a 303 mas CLASS offset that deviates from the Rayleigh model at 4 sigma.

quotes.quote: SDSS J111916.53+623925.7 Single CLASS detection of 45.5 mJy (NVSS J111916+623926), offset by 303 mas from the SDSS position. The SDSS spectrum is that of a passive elliptical with little evidence of AGN activity, but the OSSY line strengths indicate that this galaxy may be a LINER.
